Clinical Note Robustness Report

Model: llama3.1:8b | Synonym validation: 3-layer (pattern filter → structural check → LLM self-verification)

Perturbation validation method: Each synonym passes three independent checks before being applied: (1) entity category pattern filter blocks inherently unsafe entity types (negations, polarities, laterality, etc.), (2) structural rules enforce word-count and length constraints, (3) LLM self-check asks the model to verify clinical equivalence and grammatical fit. This replaces hardcoded word blocklists with a generalizable system.
STABLE — exact ICD match SUBCATEGORY DRIFT — same 3-char family CATEGORICAL DRIFT — different disease (primary metric)

Summary

Total Samples: 100

Valid for Analysis: 83 (gate: LLM clinical equivalence = YES)

Skipped (wrong baseline): 17

Categorical Drift Rate: 18.1% (primary robustness metric)

Subcategory Drift Rate: 15.7% (minor specificity shifts — not counted as true errors)

Avg Semantic Similarity: 0.9982

Avg Changes Per Note: 7.2

Original Text Perturbed Text
HPI: This was a woman, who in the remote past, had undergone a total abdominal hysterectomy for benign disease. years prior to the present admission, she apparently had required hospitalization for a presumed small intestinal obstruction that had resolved without need for surgery. However, the Patient presented to our emergency room 3 days prior to the present operation with a several-day history of abdominal discomfort, nausea and vomiting. A plain film of the abdomen followed by a CT scan suggested a distal fairly high-grade small bowel obstruction. She had essentially no gas in the colon and a convincing transition point in the very distal ileum with markedly dilated small intestine proximal to this. Her admission white blood cell count was elevated. She had mild right lower quadrant tenderness. I initially managed her with nasogastric decompression. However, her nasogastric tube was never very productive. She remained distended. On the third hospital day, her white blood count remained elevated at 14.6. She longer complained of discomfort. She actually had 1 or 2 small recorded bowel movements and reported passing flatus. However, she remained quite distended. A plain film the abdomen showed persistently dilated small intestine, measuring up to 5-6 cm. There was a paucity of gas within the colon. Although her situation was not completely straightforward, I found it disturbing that her white blood count remained elevated and that she had not made truly significant progress. Accordingly, I advised the Patient and her family to undergo exploratory surgery to relieve her obstruction, and also to be certain that she did not have ischemic enteritis as a cause for her elevated white blood cell count. They freely consented to proceed. Hospital Course: woman, who in the remote past, had undergone a total abdominal hysterectomy for benign disease. years prior to the present admission, she apparently had required hospitalization for a presumed small intestinal obstruction that had resolved without need for surgery. However, the Patient presented to our emergency room 3 days prior to the present operation with a several-day history of abdominal discomfort, nausea and vomiting. A plain film of the abdomen followed by a CT scan suggested a distal fairly high-grade small bowel obstruction. She had essentially no gas in the colon and a convincing transition point in the very distal ileum with markedly dilated small intestine proximal to this. Her admission white blood cell count was elevated. She had mild right lower quadrant tenderness. She was initially managed with nasogastric decompression. However, her nasogastric tube was never very productive. She remained distended. On the third hospital day, her white blood count remained elevated at 14.6. She no longer complained of discomfort. She actually had 1 or 2 small recorded bowel movements and reported passing flatus. However, she remained quite distended. A plain film the abdomen showed persistently dilated small intestine, measuring up to 5-6 cm. There was a paucity of gas within the colon. Although her situation was not completely straightforward, and the decision was made to take the Patient to the OR for exploratory laparatomy. The Patient tolerated the procedure well and recovered on the floor. The Patient passed gas on POD6 and his diet was slowely advanced. Patient was having bowel movements on POD7. The Patient was discharged with AVSS, ambulating, tol dit, voiding and habing bowel movements. HPI: HPI: yo G1P0 at GA presents for PIH eval. Patient reports having BP 140s/80s on , rpt 2 hours later 121/66. She attributes this to anxiety. Started a 24hr urine collection early this morning. Woke up this morning and noticed that her feet were suddenly more swollen that usual, same on both sides. Mild headache earlier, although this has now resolved. No RUQ/epigastric pain/vision changes/SOB. No ctxns. +AFM. No LOF/VB. Hospital Course: She was admitted with a diagnosis of preeclampsia and underwent IOL with cytotec. SHe delivered on a healthy baby girl. Her insulin was managed on insulin drip while in active labor and her blood suagrs were well controlled. The Jsolin was consulted as well. She went home on PPD 3 after 24 hours of magnesium sulfate. Her PP BP were all less than 150/100 and no antihypertensive was started. SHe will be seen in the office in less than one week after discharge home. PP depression was reviewed. HPI: HPI: yo G1P0 at GA presents for PIH eval. SHe delivered on a healthy baby girl. Her insulin was managed on insulin drip while in active labor and her blood suagrs were well controlled. The Jsolin was consulted as well. She went home on PPD 3 after 24 hours of magnesium sulfate. Her PP BP were all less than 150/100 and no antihypertensive was started. SHe will be seen in the Clinic in less than one week after discharge home. PP depression was reviewed.

HPI: is a woman with primary CNS lymphoma discovered after she presented with gait disturbance and imbalance. She was initially started on dexamethasone and posterior fossa mass mostly disappeared. She was monitored and it quickly returned along with symptoms, so had open biopsy in . Once lymphoma was confirmed she started high-dose methotrexate. After cycle 5, she had residual disease so continued on the Q2-week regimen. She presents today for admission for cycle 10 high dose methotrexate. Cycle 8 she had hand flushing/numbness after starting methotrexate, so infusion was stopped, but successfully finished the following day after premeds with Benadryl and dexamethasone. She had started having hives several days before that infusion. Cycle 9 she again had similar symptoms, infusion stopped after <20% of the dose. a few days later she had diffuse hives. She saw an allergist after discharge and IgE mediated methotrexate allergy was confirmed. On day of discharge, she reports feeling "tired", but "ready to roll" (home). She currently denies any problems, including hives or f/c. She reports the mild rash she has on her chest is chronic and non-pruritic. She reports typically having low grade temps with chemo. She endorses her usual mild nausea, which is controlled with prn ondansetron. No emesis, no cp, no sob, no diarrhea, no constipation, no abdominal pain and no dysuria. REVIEW OF SYSTEMS (on admit): (+) Per HPI (-) She denies fever, chills, night sweats, recent weight loss or gain. She denies headache, rhinorrhea or congestion. Denies cough, shortness of breath, or wheezing. She denies chest pain, chest pressure, or weakness. She denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain. She denies dysuria, arthralgias or myalgias. All other ROS negative Hospital Course: year old woman with CNS lymphoma admitted for high dose methotrexate, found to have methotrexate allergy after previous infusion reactions. CNS lymphoma: Cycle 10 HD MTX. She received the methotrexate via desensitization protocol developed by allergist and pharmacy. High dose methotrexate therapy required developing a high peak concentration, so her total dose was increased to 10,000 mg (planned dose for cycle 9 was 9,300 mg) to allow more of the drug to be given during the final 2 hours of the desensitization. She had some scratchy throat during the last step of the infusion. The infusion was stopped for about 15 minutes and she received additional benadryl. The infusion was restarted and she tolerated the rest of the infusion. She did not develop hives or palmar warmth/erythema which she has had before. She had not wheezing, shortness of breath, or hypotension. Ptosis, bilateral: Chronic for past years. Initially concerning for myasthenia , antibodies negative. Thrush: Resolved. Patient reports brushing her tongue with toothpaste after being on nystatin swish & swallow was most effective. Will continue home nystatin s&s. DVT ppx: Ambulation (pt prefers no heparin) Diet: Regular mtx IV access: Port Precautions: None Code status: Full DISPO: Will discharge to home since mtx is <0.1. , MD, pager HPI: is a woman with primary CNS lymphoma discovered after she presented with gait disturbance and imbalance. She was initially started on dexamethasone and posterior fossa mass mostly disappeared. She was monitored and it quickly returned along with symptoms, so had open biopsy in . Once lymphoma was confirmed she started high-dose methotrexate. After cycle 5, she had residual disease so continued on the Biweekly regimen. She presents today for admission for cycle 10 high dose methotrexate. Cycle 8 she had hand flushing/numbness after starting methotrexate, so infusion was stopped, but successfully finished the following day after premeds with Benadryl and dexamethasone. She had started having hives several days before that infusion. Cycle 9 she again had similar symptoms, infusion stopped after <20% of the dose. a few days later she had diffuse hives. She saw an allergist after DISCHARGE and IgE mediated methotrexate allergy was confirmed. On day of DISCHARGE, she reports feeling "tired", but "ready to roll" (home). She endorses her usual mild nausea, which is controlled with prn ondansetron. No emesis, no cp, no sob, no diarrhea, no constipation, no abdominal pain and no dysuria. Denies cough, shortness of breath, or wheezing. She denies chest pain, chest pressure, or weakness. She denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain. She denies dysuria, arthralgias or myalgias. All other ROS negative Hospital Course: year old woman with CNS lymphoma Hospitalized for high dose methotrexate, found to have methotrexate allergy after previous infusion reactions. CNS lymphoma: Cycle 10 HD MTX. She received the methotrexate via desensitization protocol developed by allergist and pharmacy. High dose methotrexate therapy required developing a high peak concentration, so her total dose was increased to 10,000 mg (planned dose for cycle 9 was 9,300 mg) to allow more of the drug to be given during the final 2 hours of the desensitization. She had not wheezing, shortness of breath, or hypotension. Ptosis, bilateral: Chronic for past years. Initially concerning for myasthenia , antibodies negative. Thrush: Resolved. Patient reports brushing her tongue with toothpaste after being on nystatin swish & swallow was most effective. Will continue Nystatin cream. DVT ppx: Ambulation (pt prefers no heparin) Diet: Regular mtx IV access: Port Precautions: None Code status: Full DISCHARGE: Will DISCHARGE to home since mtx is <0.1. , MD, pager

HPI: This is a gentleman with history of bilateral lower extremity claudication with discomfort on ambulation, greater on the left lower extremity relative to the contralateral limb. He was consented for a bilateral lower extremity arteriogram and possible angioplasty and stenting. Hospital Course: Mr. was admitted on with claudication.He agreed to have an elective surgery. Pre-operatively, she/he was consented. A CXR, EKG, UA, CBC, Electrolytes, T/S - were obtained, all other preperations were made. It was decided that she would undergo a: left fem endarterectomy; fem-BK pop bypass with NRGSV He was prepped, and brought down to the operating room for surgery. Intra-operatively, he was closely monitored and remained hemodynamically stable. He tolerated the procedure well without any difficulty or complication. Post-operatively,he was extubated and transferred to the PACU for further stabilization and monitoring. He was then transferred to the VICU for further recovery. While in the VICU he recieved monitered care. When stable he was delined. His diet was advanced. A consult was obtained. When he was stabalized from the acute setting of post operative care, he was transfered to floor status On the floor, he remained hemodynamically stable with his pain controlled. He progressed with physical therapy to improve her strength and mobility. He continues to make steady progress without any incidents. He was discharged to a rehabilitation facility in stable condition. To note did have atrial tachycardis. Put on Amio drip. EP consult. tachycardia resovled. Original Text Perturbed Text
HPI: Mr. is a with cryptogenic cirrhosis decompensated with hepatic encephalopthy who presents with altered mental status. According to his , he likes to drive to appointments and does not like to take medications prior to driving. He saw the nutritionist 3 days prior to admission and the dentist the following day. He reportedly became confused on the evening prior to admission, at which time he took at least 1 dose of lactulose after she reminded him. In the ED, initial vital signs were as follows: 97.0 50 154/95 12 100% RA. On exam, he was alert and oriented to self only, lethargic at times and occasionally agitated. Admission labs were not changed significantly from baseline. EKG was interpreted as sinus bradycardia. Urinalysis was unremarkable. Noncontrast head CT was within normal limits. Bedside ultrasound did not show ascites. CXR also was performed. Vital signs prior to transfer: 98.7 46 102/59 12 100% RA. On arrival to the floor, he is lethargic, but responsive to sternal rub. Hospital Course: Mr. is a with cryptogenic cirrhosis decompensated with hepatic encephalopthy who presented with altered mental status. Active Issues: (1)Hepatic encephalopathy: Confusion was attributed to hepatic encephalopathy in the setting of lactulose noncompliance and resolved with resumption of home lactulose and rifaximin regimen. Infectious work up, including blood and urine cultures and CXR, was unrevealing while noncontrast head CT was negative for acute intracranial abnormality. There was no significant ascites on bedside ultrasound in the ED, hence low suspicion for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. There was no evidence of portal vein thrombosis on right upper quadrant ultrasound with Doppler. He was urged to adhere to lactulose/rifaximin regimen at home and advised on multiple occasions by different providers not to drive, given risk to himself and others in the likely event of recurrent encephalopathy. (2)Cryptogenic cirrhosis: Liver and synthetic function remained at baseline throughout admission. home furosemide 80mg bid, held initially in anticipation of copious loose stools as above, was continued, as was home eplerenone 50mg bid. He is not on nadolol in the presence of grade 1 varices as of . Lactulose and rifaximin were continued as above. (3)Leukocytosis: He developed leukocytosis of unclear significance to 15.1 on hospital day 3 in the absence of fever or localizing signs/symptoms of infection. Urine culture was negative at that time, and repeat blood cultures have shown no growth to date. White blood cell count downtrended the following day without dedicated intervention. Inactive Issues: (1)Hypertension: He remained normotensive throughout admission on propranolol 20mg bid in the setting of copious loose stools as above, with home dose 40mg bid resumed at discharge. (2)Hypothyroidism: home levothyroxine 150mcg daily was continued throughout admission. (3)Gastroesophageal reflux: home omeprazole 20mg daily was continued throughout admission. Transitional Issues: Left portal vein was not fully evaluated on right upper quadrant ultrasound with Doppler due to limited acoustic window. Repeat ultrasound may be advised in 4 to 6 weeks. Pending studies: blood cultures x2 ( ) and x2 ( ). Code status: Full. HPI: Mr. is a with cryptogenic cirrhosis decompensated with hepatic encephalopthy who presents with altered mental status. HPI: y/o male with a history of fulminant cryptogenic hepatic failure s/p OLT complicated by graft loss due to ischemic cholangiopathy requiring liver/kidney transplant , and h/o VRE cholangitis who presents s/p ERCP. . Of note he was recently admitted after transitioning from daptomycin/tigecycline to levofloxacin which caused transaminitis, fatigue and night sweats thought to be due to either recurrence of his VRE cholangitis, progressive cholangiopathy, recurrent biliary strictures or new bile duct injury. He was discharged on daptomycin and levofloxacin. . He has had persistent pruritis and night sweats despite continuation of antibiotics. ERCP was completed today to elucidate the biliary architecture, no obstruction or dilitation was present. He tolerated the procedure well, no current complaints. . Review of systems: (+) Per HPI (-) Denies fever, chills, recent weight loss or gain. Denies headache, sinus tenderness, rhinorrhea or congestion. Denied cough, shortness of breath. Denied chest pain or tightness, palpitations. Denied nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation or abdominal pain. No recent change in bowel or bladder habits. No dysuria. Denied arthralgias or myalgias. Hospital Course: is a year old male with a history of fulminant cryptogenic hepatic failure s/p 2 liver transplants, h/o VRE cholangitis on daptomycin/levofloxacin who presents with persistent pruritis and night sweats. He had an ERCP which did not show any clear source of cholangitis or obstruction. It did show a dilated CBD 12 mm and moderate patchy sclerosis of the intrahepatic biliary tree. He was continued on daptomycin and levofloxacin. His outpatient ID physician was contacted. It was decided to continue daptomycin IV until , but continue levofloxacin for suppression. He will have basic labs drawn on and then will have follow up at the liver where his PICC line will be removed. He was noted to have increased creatinine of 1.3 (above baseline 0.8). He was given a fluid challenge of 1 liter NS without change in his creatinine. We advised him to remain hydrated and he will have his creatinine checked . If persistently elevated, we recommend initial workup with renal ultrasound, urine cultures and lytes. He was hyperglycemic during his hospital stay, admitting to recent diet incompliance. He was continued on his home ISS and encouraged to follow a strict diabetic diet. He had no evidence of DKA. He was FULL CODE for this admission. He had an ERCP which did not show any clear source of cholangitis or obstruction. It did show a dilated CBD 12 mm and moderate patchy sclerosis of the intrahepatic biliary tree. He was continued on daptomycin and levofloxacin. His outpatient ID Doctor was contacted. It was decided to continue daptomycin IV until , but continue levofloxacin for suppression. He will have basic labs drawn on and then will have follow up at the liver where his PICC line will be removed. He was noted to have increased creatinine of 1.3 (above baseline 0.8). He was given a fluid challenge of 1 liter NS without change in his creatinine. We advised him to remain hydrated and he will have his creatinine checked . If persistently elevated, we recommend initial workup with renal ultrasound, urine cultures and lytes. He was hyperglycemic during his hospital stay, admitting to recent diet incompliance. He was continued on his home ISS and encouraged to follow a strict diabetic diet. He had no evidence of DKA. He was FULL CODE for this admission.
ICD: K85.8 ICD: K85.8

HPI: with dermatomysitis, diabetes, severe hypertriglyceridemia, FSGS glomerular dz, p/w abd pain. She has had hypertriglyceridemia for the past several years, although was noted to have triglycerides of 4966 on . On triglyderides were . Her were concerned about this lab and contacted her advising her to come to the ED. However at that point she was asymptomatic and so she decided not to come in. On , she developed abdominal pain after eating cheerios cereal and the vomiting. The pain was across her abdomen, severe and worse with the food. She presented to the ED where vitals: t 96.1 p 90 bp 141/91. Bedside US showed no stones. Abd CT showed pancreatic fluid with stranding c/w pancreatitis. She was given IVF, IV dilaudid and anti-emetics. She was admitted to hospitalist service for further management. Hospital Course: This is a yo F with dermatomysitis, diabetes mellitus, severe hypertriglyceridemia, primary FSGS glomerular disease/nephrotic syndrome, presenting with acute pancreatitis in the setting of severe hypertrigylceridemia. Pt basically with mildly prolonged course to slowly improving pancreatitis - issues noted below - at time of d/c tolerating full liquid diet without difficulty, will need close with PCP, , pancreas/GI clinic with Dr. , and clinic.
Acute Pancreatitis: Likely secondary to hypertriglyceridemia. At baseline, the pts are 4000-5000 per report in past. She was noted to have level of 14,779 at on per prior report, level now down to 762 at time of d/c. Abdominal US is negative for gallstones. CT on neg for necrosis - pt still with persistant pain/distension through hospitalization - numbers showing improvement - but with Ca levels low, given concern on , GI was consulted - got MRI ab - results did not show any areas of necrosis, just diffuse inflammatory changes. Pt sx with cont improvement following - d/c PCA pump , 0.5 dilaudid did not cover pain well but with breakthrough pain controlled, was controlled with 1mg dilaudid prn. Pt now tolerating full liquids well at time of d/c. CRP noted elevated in 200 range - given clinic improvements, lipase at 65 at time of d/c, and good po intake pt was stable to be out of hospital and with close , intructions given to pt. -MRI of pancreas noted -cont full liquid diet for days with very light solids (like plain toast), slowly advance and gentle diet for next week, then only low fat/diabetic diet following - with Dr. as above in wks (number given to pt to make appt)
Severe hypertriglyceridemia: Followed by Dr. cardiology with regards to her lipids, who saw pt in-patient. Pt states she had not been taking her gemfibrozil over the past month due to changing insurance. Baseline have been 4000-5000. It sounds as though pt also is non-compliant with her medications (including gemfibrozil) as well as with her diet. She also started OCPs a month ago, which could be worsening her levels -gave gemfibrizol in-patient with lowering sig (also noted more from npo status), then finally got confirmed outpt insurance coverage of tricor as recommended by Dr. - changed on to tricor on at 145mg po qdaily - cont as oupt - in , if not improving much to also start fish oils at that point per cardiology recs -stopped OCP here -started ASA for cardiac prevention (changed to 81mg qd from 325) -SW consulted for insurance barriers - currently pt with medicare
Diabetes, poorly controlled in general - currently controlled, with complications: HgbA1c 10.0 on at . Per last note at , pt is very noncompliant again more to funding. Not on SSI at home, just supposed to be on NPH 80 U twice daily -gave NPH while NPO initially (is on 80 U twice daily at home), BS prior uncontrolled increasing regime slowly - but then evening, BS lower - NPH held BS still in nml range, mildly increasing to upper 100s/few readings in low 200s at time of d/c but still with many in nml range. -cont SSI and will be d/c with humolog per recs who saw pt on day of d/c given pt diet will be slowly increasing
Nephrotic syndrome/Primary FSGS: Followed by Dr. as outpatient. Pt was just diagnosed with primary FSGS back in . Not on given h/o angioedema. Was not placed on increased dose of prednisone given her poorly controlled DM/insulin resistance. Was supposed to start cellcept, but has not due to initially insurance not covering it, and then pt worried her flu shot would cause problems with it. -per Dr. off on cellcept in the setting of acute pancreatitis as it has a lot of GI side effects -renal consulted -held aldactone 25 mg daily in the setting of acute illness/dehydration - cont to hold at time of d/c with PCP to - re-start once more stable -prot/cr ratio noted at 6.9 -note, pt should be on ppi ppx in the future if she is to continue on daily prednisone and with cellcept -PTH levels noted sig elevated - renal to note levels in
Hypocalcemia: Currently may have some component of sequestration in setting of pancreatitis (Ca dropped from 8.6 on to on , in 5 range for few days, then on improved to 7 range (nml with alb). -d/c standing CaCO3 order - cont to follow levels -per renal, after discharge pt should be on Vit D 50,000 units weekly (given that Vit D is not absorbed well in nephrotic syndrome) -PTH levels noted
Dermatomyositis: In the past she has been on numerous courses of therapy for her dermatomyositis including prednisone, Imuran, and intravenous immunoglobulin. Currently only on prednisone. -continue prednisone 10 mg daily -on PPI for ppx -apparently has not wanted to be on a bisphosphonate per last rheumatology note BUT now currently willing, will plan on starting alendronate at time of d/c aldendronate 35mg qwks (now that pt is eating), but started Ca/Vit D this admission
Rash, drug related - occured early worsend in pm, d/c Ca Gluconate with resolution of rash am? Possible additative in IV Ca Gluconate? No further sx with gemfibrozil (so likely additive with Ca Gluconate IV. Completely resolved now and stable for >3day prior to d/c.
HTN, benign: holding aldactone/lasix in the setting of acute pancreatitis/dehydration. Cont to monitor with PCP . (volume status on exam more euvolemic at time of d/c).
: Evidence of fatty liver disease with marked steatosis and fibrosis on liver biopsy in . Followed by Dr. . Lipid mgmt as above. -continue ursodiol
Hypothyroidism: Continue synthroid (last TSH elevated in the setting of not having taken her synthroid). Will need close PCP - (need to check levels 4wks after d/c).

HPI: Mrs. is a PMHx for large right sided liver tumor s/p right trisegmentectomy with Patch angioplasty of left hepatic vein using bovine patch on who has been readmitted for elevated Alk phos to 1113 from 436. Patient overall had an uncomplicated post-operative course. She was extubated on POD1. Patient was transferred out of the ICU in stable condition. Patient continued to progress with increased oral intake and was discharged to rehab on . Patient was seen in clinic and was found to have an elevated Alk phos 3 times the level at which she was discharged at. On re-admission, hepatic ultrasound was performed which did not demonstrate any biloma collection or collection. Patient's roux tube was uncapped. Currently, she denies nausea/vomiting/fever/chills HPI: Mrs. is a Past Medical History for large right sided liver tumor s/p right trisegmentectomy with Patch angioplasty of left hepatic vein using bovine patch on who has been readmitted for elevated Alkaline phos to 1113 from 436. HPI: HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS: Mr. is a year old male with past medical history significant for hypertension and alcohol use disorder who presents as a transfer from BI-P with newly diagnosed extensive right lower extremity DVT extending to IVC. Patient reports that he went to sleep on with his right lower extremity appearing normal. He states that he woke up this morning and noticed his RLE was diffusely swollen and redness. The Patient describes discomfort in leg, but denies any sharp stabbing pains. The Patient denies any chest pain or shortness of breath. Denies any fevers but did endorse some chills. He denies any recent immobilization, long car rides, history of DVT/PE. He is not on blood thinners. He denies any recent orthopedic procedures. He did have a open reduction internal fixation of the right tib-fib approximately years ago. He states that his right leg has not returned to prior since surgery but he has no difficulty ambulating on it. He presented to BI-P where he had an ultrasound of his right lower extremity which showed clot in the right femoral vein extending down into the right popliteal vein and superiorly into the inferior vena cava. The Patient also had an x-ray that was unremarkable. He was started on a heparin gtt. Vascular surgery at recommended transfer to . In the ED, initial VS were: T 98.3, HR 68, BP 150/84, RR 18, SpO2 97% RA Exam notable for: Gen: Comfortable, No Acute Distress HEENT: NC/AT. EOMI. Neck: No swelling. Trachea is midline. No JVD Cor: RRR. No m/r/g. Pulm: CTAB, Nonlabored respirations. Abd: Soft, NT, ND. Bowel sounds present Ext: The right lower extremity is significantly asymmetrically swollen. There is 2+ pitting edema all the way up to the thigh. The medial aspect of the thigh is erythematous and tender to palpation. Patient has equal pulses in the DPs and TPs. Skin: No rashes. No skin breakdown Neuro: AAOx3. Gross sensorimotor intact. Psych: Normal mentation. Heme: No petechia. No ecchymosis. ECG: not performed Labs showed: -CBC: WBC 6.3, Hgb 15.8, Plt 239 -Coags: INR 1.1, PTT 67.2 -Lactate: 2.6 Imaging showed: CT TORSO W/ CONTRAST: 1. Thrombus is seen extending from the right femoral veins into the distal IVC superior to the iliac bifurcation. 2. Aneurysmal ascending thoracic aorta measuring up to 4.6 cm. Consults: -Vascular surgery: continue heparin gtt, elevate the right lower extremity and apply compressive ace wrap, obtain CT venogram to better characterize thrombus, keep NPO at midnight for possible intervention Patient received: -IV heparin gtt -PO simvastatin 20mg Transfer VS were: T 97.9, HR 69, BP 153/85, RR 18, SpO2 97% RA On arrival to the floor, Patient reports the story outlined above. History is difficult to obtain as Patient is somewhat tangential. He is oriented to self and location but not to date. He denies any right leg pain but does note leg fullness. Denies any chest pain or shortness of breath. Denies any recent immobilization (flights, travel), surgeries, or hospitalizations. Is uncertain if he is up-to-date on age appropriate malignancy screening but believes he has had a colonoscopy in the past. He is uncertain about weight gain/loss. Additionally, he notes having several pruritic lesions on his upper legs and abdomen that also started since yesterday. Uncertain about any insect bites and wonders if this could be beg bugs. Per son, there is some concerns about alcohol use disorder and he should be monitored for withdrawal symptoms. HPI: Mr. is a male with a past medical history of alcohol abuse (drinking since age , recently in IOP program at ), alcoholic hepatitis with 3 prior hospitalizations (no seizures), gastritis, and a right eye prosthesis, who presents for alcohol detox with labs concerning for alcoholic hepatitis. The patient was last admitted ~1 month ago with a similar presentation and his withdrawal was complicated by alcoholic hepatitis, blood-streaked emesis, confusion and hallucinations. Steroids were not given due to score of 2.7. He was managed with diazepam and maalox for gastritis. Social work saw him and gave him resources to connect with IOP at following discharge, which he did. His preference now is to go to an facility but he was told his insurance didn't cover it. He recently presented to his PCP, , for addiction psychiatry referral, stating that he has been having drinks a day and wanted referral to a psychiatrist. He claims that when he tries to stop drinking he feels high levels of anxiety but expressed a desire to quit drinking nonetheless. At the appointment, he was referred to Dr. addiction psychiatrist at , and referred nonurgently to the . Today he presents for detox because he was told by his primary care doctor that his "liver is damaged." He says he drinks alcohol every day but does not drink a lot - he had stopped in when he went to detox and rehab and had not had a drink in 3 weeks until yesterday. Endorses drinking 5 nips yesterday and 2 today after having a disagreement with his ex-wife. Also endorses marijuana use yesterday but denies other illicits. On review of systems, he reports diffuse abdominal pain that is constant and chronic in nature, worse with eating, and gnawing in nature. Hospital Course: Alcoholic hepatitis Mild AGMA (bicarb 19) Elevated lipase (120, 2x ULN) Seen last month here for alc hep, now back with recurrence. Transaminitis and elevated bilirubin are likely in the setting of alcohol use however labs are uptrending as of 10 days ago. HBV/HCV unremarkable on last admission. AGMA is c/w element of starvation ketosis and lipase elevation is likely in the setting of chronic alcohol abuse. -LFTs are trending down today -hepatology consulted, appreciate recs, MRCP with no obstruction, ceruloplasmin was checked and WNL, no further work up recommended so far, holding on liver biopsy, etOH abstinence Alcohol withdrawal Alcohol use disorder History of withdrawal on last admission c/b hallucinations and confusion. No h/o seizures or ICU admissions for etOH. Last drink today in the ED today. Endorses mild tremors and anxiety. - did not undergo significant withdrawal, no seizures here - PO thiamine daily - Multivitamin and folic acid - SW following - addiction psych OP appointment - psych also saw him here and continued on his Psych regimen with OP PSych follow up. -plan for outpatient etOH program Adcare which has been enrolled in from before. He tells us that he is going to go there starting tomorrow morning. CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES: Hypertension - Continue amLODIPine 10 mg PO DAILY Hyperlipidemia - holding statin with liver enzymes elevation for now and plan to resume once approved by his PCP or . Anxiety Depression - continue Zoloft, Seroquel and hydroxyzine PRN. Transitional issues: -Repeat complete metabolic panel within 5 days -Outpatient follow-up with his PCP, , psychiatry and follow-up with outpatient rehab program. Total time spent on the discharge process by me personally was greater than 30 minutes, most of which was spent in counseling and discharge coordination. Original Text Perturbed Text
HPI: year old man with pmh of GERD and HTN, had recent stress test for c/o 3 weeks of DOE, lightheadedness, fatigue and nonproductive. He had limited echo at Dr and was referred today for CXR showed "large Left lung mass". Chest CTA was recommended and that showed type A dissection so he was transferred to ER for emergent surgical evaluation. Hospital Course: The patient was brought to the Operating Room on where he underwent emergent repair of Type A dissection. Overall the patient tolerated the procedure well and post-operatively was transferred to the CVICU in stable condition for recovery and invasive monitoring. POD 1 found the patient extubated, alert and oriented and breathing comfortably. Pressors and Epi weaned. The patient was neurologically intact and hemodynamically stable. Beta blocker was initiated and the patient was gently diuresed toward the preoperative weight. He developed AFib. Lopressor titrated and Amio/ Warfarin initiated. The patient was transferred to the telemetry floor for further recovery. Chest tubes and pacing wires were discontinued without complication. Lisinopril resumed for hypertension and titrated to home dose as tolerated. Diuresis titrated for orthostatic hypotension. The patient was evaluated by the Physical Therapy service for assistance with strength and mobility. By the time of discharge on POD 7 he was ambulating freely, the wound was healing and pain was controlled with oral analgesics. The patient was discharged to home with services in good condition with appropriate follow up instructions. PCP has agreed to follow anti-coagulation through . HPI: Mr. is a year-old right-handed man with no known significant past medical history who initially presented to with severe headache and subsequent "aphasia" and right hemiparesis who was transferred to the when he was found to have left basal ganglia hemorrhage. According to the patient's son and nephew, the patient was feeling well on . In fact, he went outside to do some yard work. While performing the yard work, he suddenly developed a severe headache that seemed to involve his entire head. He started stumbling around, and his wife helped him to sit down. He seemed to develop difficulty speaking and right-sided weakness. Accordingly, she called . He was initially transported to , where he was found to have a blood pressure of 190/90. Initial examinations describe a leftward gaze deviation and flaccidity of the right limbs. A non-contrast CT of the head revealed a left basal ganglia hemorrhage. He was intubated and given a dilantin load as seizure prophylaxis prior to transfer to the for further evaluation and care. . At the time of his arrival, a non-contrast CT of the head was repeated and demonstrated relative stability of the lesion. Neurosurgery evaluated the patient and indicated no acute neurosurgical intervention was warranted. Accordingly, neurology was asked to participate in the patient's care. Hospital Course: : Mr. initially presented with headache, aphasia, right neglect, and right hemiparesis. Over the course of his hospitilization he recovered partial function of the right side, but failed multiple swallow evaluations. Neuro: Mr. initially had dense right hemiplegia, which improved significantly by discharge. He demonstrated neglect of the right side, by discharge he responded to questions arising from the right. He had significant weakness ofthe right lower extremities, both toes were downgoing by discharge. He has significant dysarthria. Cardiac: Mr. initially presented with significant hypertension, and throughout his hospital stay he remained difficult to control. He was started on Verapamil, Lisinopril, and Labetalol. He had elevated creatinine on lisinopril and it was discontinued, with potential to be restarted at a later time. Respiratory: Mr. was initially intubated, for level of consciousness and secretions, and was extubated successfully after several days. He had several chest xrays (most recent on which appeared to have atelectasis of the right lower lobe, but no focal consolidations. Renal: Mr. creatinine was initially elevated. Due to his difficult to treat hypertension and lack of medical care previously, secondary causes of hypertension were investigated. Urine Pr:cr was 0.2, and renal doppler u/s was normal. FEN/GI: Pt failed multiple swallow evaluations and a PEG tube was placed on by Gastroenterology. On he was able to coordinate chewing and was placed on a pureed and thin liquid diet, with most nutrition still continuing via the PEG tube. HPI: RHD M involved in an altercation overnight with gun shot wounds to his right back, left thigh, and left forearm, extensive bleeding from left forearm and blood per rectum. patient initially seen at and transferred to for further care. Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to the Plastic/Hand surgery service on status post multiple gun shot wounds including the left distal radius, the left thigh and the right back areas. Ortho Trauma surgery did primary evaluation and found the left upper extremity demonstrated a grade 1 open fracture of the distal radius which was extra-articular, highly comminuted and associated with significant bone loss. There was no palpable radial artery and the CTA did not show perfusion of the vessel. There was good perfusion with ulnar artery clinically. (good ulnar pulse, warm perfused hand). Vascular surgery was then consulted and found left upper extremity with open fracture, no palpable radial pulse, although + palmar arch signals. Partial visualization of left upper extremity on CTA with concern for distal radial artery injury. At that point, Hand Surgery was consulted and patient was taken to the OR for Left upper extremity irrigation and debridement open wound, Left distal radius open reduction, internal fixation, repair of left radial artery with segmental reversed saphenous vein graft interposition from right lower extremity. Left thigh irrigation and debridement of multiple open wounds. The patient tolerated the procedures well. . Neuro: post-operatively, the patient received dilaudid PCA with good effect and adequate pain control. When tolerating oral intake, the patient was transitioned to oxycodone PO. Oxycodone did not adequately control patient's level of pain so this was discontinued and patient was started on dilaudid PO with which he found better pain control. patient complained of right sided lower extremity weakness on so physical therapy consult was requested and found complaints to be clinically accurate with RLE decreased sensation in L2/3 dermatomal pattern correct in each). Pt reported numbness and pins and needles sensation in anterior medial thigh. In addition, light touch on R he reported felt "dulled" compared to light touch on L in L2/3 dermatome distribution. R hip flex , R knee ext , R knee flex , R DF . Motor Function: (motor control, learning) and positive Babinski on the R. Normal tone t/o RLE. Neurology consult was requested and patient was seen on . Neurology agreed with concern for upper lumbar root or upper lumbar plexus disruption leading to iliopsoas weakess and absent patellar reflex and since MRI was not an option due to presence of bullets/fragments, it was decided that a CT of lumbar spine/pelvis with contrast should be obtained. CT found no retroperitoneal abscess or hematoma that would cause compression of the upper lumbar nerve roots or upper plexus structures. Neurology felt patient symptoms seemed more consistent with perhaps traction or inflammatory lesions of these peripheral structures. Overall, it was felt that patient would benefit from addition of anti-inflammatory and anti-spasmodic meds and that no follow up with Neurology was required unless symptoms did not improve or worsened. Ibuprofen, Flexeril and Tylenol were added to patient's medication regimen. patient continued to work with Physical Therapy and on found patient to be independent with gait using R crutch and ascending stairs with rail vs crutch but that patient continued to have difficulty descending stairs with crutch. A plan was made to have patient return home and ascend his apartment stairs and reside in his apartment for several days while practicing strengthening exercises to RLE before attempting to descend stairs with assistance. . CV: The patient had mild elevated blood pressures during hospital admission. It was unclear if these were related to pre-existing essential hypertension or more to pain, trauma and anxiety. patient was started on Lopressor 12.5 mg BID while in hospital and blood pressures were well controlled. The patient and his girlfriend were encouraged and assisted to find a Primary Care Physician for the patient upon discharge to help monitor blood pressure as an outpatient. . Pulmonary: The patient was stable from a pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. . GI/GU: post-operatively, the patient was given IV fluids until tolerating oral intake. His diet was advanced on POD 1, which was tolerated well. He was also started on a bowel regimen to encourage bowel movement. Foley was removed on POD 2. Intake and output were closely monitored. . ID: post-operatively, the patient was started on IV cefazolin which continued until POD 4 and was discontinued. The patient's temperature was closely watched for signs of infection. . Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin during this stay, and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. . At the time of discharge on POD 5, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating with crutch, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. Left upper extremity was maintained in OR splint with good perfusion noted to digits. Left thigh bullet wounds with incisions/suture sites clean and intact without signs of infection. Right back bullet wound clean with dry dressing intact and no signs of infection. patient completed stair climbing trial with physical therapy with Physical Therapy prior to discharge utilizing one right sided crutch. patient will have follow up with Dr. service on to follow up on all gun shot injuries and re-assess all injuries at that time. Advil, Flexeril and Tylenol were added to Patient's medication regimen. Patient continued to work with Physical Therapy and on found Client to be independent with gait using R crutch and ascending stairs with rail vs Walker but that Patient continued to have difficulty descending stairs with crutch. Left upper extremity was maintained in OR splint with good perfusion noted to digits. Left thigh bullet wounds with Wound sites clean and intact without signs of infection. Right back bullet wound clean with dry dressing intact and no signs of infection. Patient completed stair climbing trial with physical therapy with Physical Therapy prior to discharge utilizing one right sided crutch. Original Text Perturbed Text
HPI: year old man with atrial fibrillation but otherwise unknown who has been having episodes of lightheadedness and syncope for the past day and a half. Per his wife, he has not reported any associated chest pain, SOB, or palpitations, though has been extremely fatigued for the past week. After each syncopal episode he wakes confused and tired. Yesterday afternoon he had another episode and afterward was unresponsive. She tried calling the patient's PCP who called her back after some time later and advised her to call . When EMS arrived they found him to be in asystole, with no response to 0.5mg atropine so transcutaneous pacing was initiated with possible recovery of mental status. He was initially taken to where ekg showed complete heart block with slow ventricular escape rhythm. Labs significant for WBC 19 with 11% bands, trop 0.063. . Upon arrival to the ED patient was tachypneic and satting 80% on RA which improved to 94% on NRB. Question as to whether patient was awake and responsive. He subsequently developed respiratory distress with sats in the low and was intubated, CXR with pulmonary edema, likely secondary to 3L NS he received prior to arrival. He was given lasix 80mg IV x2. Post-intubation he was hypotensive to the systolic so was started on levophed. A right IJ cordis was placed as well as transvenous pacing wire, however there was poor capture so the patient continued to be transcutaneously paced until arrival to the CCU. . On arrival to the CCU patient was intubated on fentanyl and versed and on levophed. The pacing wire was adjusted using fluoroscopy with good capture, threshold 1 mAmp, rate 80bpm. MAPs initially improved but then again dropped to the requiring increased doses of levophed. Chief Complaint: year old man with atrial fibrillation but otherwise unknown who has been having episodes of lightheadedness and Fainting episode for the past day and a half. Per his wife, he has not reported any associated chest pain, SOB, or palpitations, though has been extremely fatigued for the past week. After each syncopal episode he wakes confused and tired. Yesterday afternoon he had another episode and afterward was unresponsive. She tried calling the patient's PCP who called her back after some time later and advised her to call . When EMS arrived they found him to be in asystole, with no response to 0.5mg atropine so transcutaneous pacing was initiated with possible recovery of mental status. He was initially taken to where ekg showed complete heart block with slow ventricular escape rhythm. Labs significant for WBC 19 with 11% Neutrophils, trop 0.063. . Upon arrival to the ED patient was tachypneic and satting 80% on RA which improved to 94% on NRB. Question as to whether patient was awake and responsive. He subsequently developed respiratory distress with sats in the low and was intubated, CXR with pulmonary edema, likely secondary to 3L NS he received prior to arrival. He was given lasix 80mg IV x2. Post-intubation he was hypotensive to the systolic so was started on levophed. A right IJ cordis was placed as well as transvenous pacing wire, however there was poor capture so the patient continued to be transcutaneously paced until arrival to the CCU. . On Admission to the CCU patient was intubated on fentanyl and versed and on levophed. The pacing wire was adjusted using fluoroscopy with good capture, threshold 1 mAmp, rate 80bpm. MAPs initially improved but then again dropped to the requiring increased doses of levophed.

HPI: Ms. is a with history of symptomatic cholelithiasis who presents with abdominal pain. Patient reports that in the past few weeks she has had 3 separate episodes of abdominal pain. She states that the first one occurred several weeks ago after eating some popcorn. This been lasted around 1 to 2 hours. She saw her primary care physician in clinic, who did an abdominal ultrasound and then referred her to surgery clinic for consideration of a cholecystectomy. She states that he was concerned that she might be having gastritis, and so she started to take omeprazole. The current episode of abdominal pain started the night prior to admission. She states that she woke up in significant pain. Pain is located in epigastric area, is constant and severe, and radiates to the back. Associated with nausea and vomiting. Reports no bloody vomiting, no fevers, chills, no chest pain or shortness of breath. She initially presented to . Labs notable for WBC 12, Hct 45.1. BUN 13 and Cr 0.7. AST 726, ALT 478, AlkPhos 248, Tbili 2.2, lipase 558. Abd U/S suggestive of pancreatitis, negative for acute cholecystitis, with mild CBD dilation. Transferred to for consideration of MRCP or ERCP. On review of records, was seen by Dr in surgery clinic on for consideration of a cholecystectomy. He felt that her pain was more consistent with gastritis than biliary colic. She was started on zantac twice a day with plan for follow up. In the ED: Initial vital signs were notable for: T 97.7, HR 67, BP 136/79, RR 16, 96% RA Exam notable for: Abdomen- Soft. Tender to palpation across upper abdomen. No rebound or guarding. Labs were notable for: - CBC: WBC 6.5 (72.6%n), hgb 13.6, plt 224 - Lytes: 141 / 108 / 7 AGap 12 ------------- 73 3.6 \ 21 \ 0.6 - LFTs: AST: 485 ALT: 543 AP: 211 Tbili: 0.9 Alb: 3.8 - INR 1.1 Studies performed include: - CXR with no acute findings Consults: Case was discussed with ERCP, who recommended MRCP Hospital Course: TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: acute pancreatitis abdominal pain cholelithiasis Patient presenting with abdominal pain with findings of cholelithiasis, mildly dilated CBD on RUQUS. LFTs and tbili improved (2.2 -> 0.9). Lipase 558 at as well, down to 71 here. Likely passed stone. MRCP showing pancreatitis without obstructing stone and no signs of abscess. Initially on abx day 1 . Zosyn initially --> ctx/flagyl on , then DC'd on once no signs of infection. D/w ERCP, no scope; plan to refer to for CCY inpatient. Pain improving significantly to , tolerated rapid advancement of diet on . To surgery service on , laparoscopic cholecystectomy performed on this date. Umcomplicated procedure. Patient discharged home for outpatient follow up on the same day. Acute hypoxemic respiratory failure (resolved): Patient found to have a new O2 requirement in the ED, though now back on room air. Asymptomatic. CXR without acute findings. No HF Hx and euvolemic. No subsequent O2 needs. CHRONIC/STABLE PROBLEMS: Hyperlipidemia: held home atorvastatin given elevated LFTs; restarted on DC Hypertension: continued home amlodipine, Metoprolol. On day 2 held HCTZ d/t relative hypotension; instructed to restart her home medications upon discharge. HPI: with multiple prior SBO's requiring several exploratory laparotomies ( ) now presents with abdominal pain for 5 days, nausea, and dry heaves. Pain started , got a bit better on and with a self-imposed clear liquid diet, but on and became more severe, constant, and did have intermittent exacerbations of worse generalized abdominal pain along with nausea and dry heaves, but no actual emesis. Her chronically patent gastrostomy tract has also been draining a bit more than usual the past couple days, requiring more frequent dressing changes. Had a BM today as well as some flatus around 530pm, and before that last BM was (normally has BM's every other day). Presented to the ED, labs showed dehydration (hemoconcentrated to Hct of 50), leukocytosis with WBC of 12, and acidosis (HCO3 of 13), and a CT showed a high-grade SBO so surgery is now consulted. Hospital Course: The patient presented on with a high grade small bowel obstruction. The patient was treated non-operatively. A NG tube was placed while the patient was in the ED and the patient was placed on IV fluids. On HD 1, the patient began to have spontaneous return of bowel function - passing flatus and having two bowel movements. The patient underwent an NG tube clamp trial on HOD2 and had minimal residuals. The patient's diet was then slowly advanced until she was tolerating a regular diet. The patient's home warfarin was held on admission and the patient received BID SQH for DVT prophylaxis. The patient was resumed on her home warfarin prior to discharge. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating diet as above per oral, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient was discharged home without services. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan. HPI: Mr. is a with PMH significant for CAD s/p DES to the LAD in , HTN, HLD, chronic pancreatic insufficiency, severe OA of the hips (R>L), h/o venous stasis and boarderline CKD (baseline Cr 1.1-1.2) who presents to the ED after a fall 1 week prior. Patient is speaking only and his ex-wife is at the bedside to help with translation. Patient was in his usual state of health until one week prior when he slipped while in the shower. He did not fall to the ground and was able to catch himself. No headstrike or LOC. However, since that time, he has been unable to ambulate or bear weight on the RLE. He describes his pain as being in intensity mostly localized to his right hip/groin region and right knee. He was seen in the this afternoon for this pain and was referred to the ED for further evaluation. Of note, Patient has been evaluated by Dr. in the past for possible THA but this was deferred. He has received both hip and knee injections with the last being and , respectively. In the ED, initial vitals were: 99.3, 96, 169/81, 18, 98% RA. Labs were significant for: WBC 10.1, H/H 13.4/40.2, plts 248. INR 1.0. Cr 1.0, troponin < 0.01. Patient was reporting mild 'chest pressure' upon arrival in the ED which was nonradiating and currently not present during my evaluation. An EKG was done and was unchanged from baseline EKG done in . On evaluation, Patient was upset that he would not be getting surgery tonight and was requesting his ambien. He is reporting mild R hip discomfort but greatly improved from prior. He denied chest pain, SOB, palpitations, diaphoresis, cough, lightheadedness, N/V, abdominal pain. Hospital Course: A/P: with PMH significant for CAD s/p DES to the LAD in , HTN, HLD, chronic pancreatic insufficiency, severe OA of the hips (R>L), h/o venous stasis and boarderline CKD (baseline Cr 1.1-1.2) who presents to the ED after a fall 1 week prior and found to have a nondisplaced R femoral neck/head fracture. ACTIVE ISSUES: Nondisplaced R femoral neck/head fracture: Pt found to have nondisplaced R femoral neck/head fracture on hip x ray on admission. He was taken to the OR on by orthopedic surgery for total hip replacment but the surgeon found him to have copious granular past-appearing material inside the hip joint concerning for infection. He underwent debridement and biopsies were sent and hip replacement was delayed pending culture results. He was started on vanc empirically. Biopsies and pathology report all came back negative for acute infection and were suggestive of avascular necrosis. Vanc was discontinued and pt was taken back to the OR on for an uncomplicated total hip arthroplasty. AVN may have been due to delayed treatment of fracture. He was treated with lovenox which he will continue for four weeks. Delirium: After his first surgery, he began briefly confused and agitated overnight. He was found to have 14 WBC in his urine so was started on ceftriaxone given his delirium, but this was discontinued shortly as Patient had no symptoms and urine culture was negative. He was given haldol and benadryl overnight, with no improvement in his delirium. He had also received a scopolamine patch earlier that day. His delirium was likely multifactorial, post-operative status, pain, constipation, and medication effect. After holding the above medications his delirium resolved. : On in the afternoon, he was found to have low UOP and creatinine increased to 1.4 (from baseline 0.9). He was taking minimal POs at this time. A foley was placed, the Patient was given IVF boluses, and UOP increased (1700 cc in 24 hours). Pt's irbesartan/hctz were held. Upon recheck, Cr had downtrended to 1.0. was likely prerenal poor PO intake. Anemia: Pt has chronic mild normocytic anemia. His hemoglobin slowly decreased from 13.3 to 7.9 on weight:bold;">discharge (nadir 7.4). This was most likely a combination of intraoperative blood loss, frequent phlebotomy. He did have guiac positive stool on which should be followed up (below). Ileus: Pt developed a post-op ileus on . Serial KUBs showed mild ileus. He was made NPO, given IV fluids, and serial abdominal exams were performed. He never had nausea or vomiting. On his diet was advanced to clear liquids, then full liquids, then full regular diet. He was making flatus and his abdominal exam was much improved on the day of weight:bold;">discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES: HTN: Pt was normotensive. Given normotension and , HCTZ/irbesartan were held. CAD: Patient was continued on ASA 81mg daily, metoprolol XL 100mg daily, Rosuvastatin 10mg qpm. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Haloperidol: Per report from the overnight team, Mr. had an adverse reaction to haloperidol which included worsened agitation and may have also included dystonic movements. This medicaiton and meds of its class should likely be avoided in future hospitalizations, or trialled with caution. Anti-hypertensives: Irbesartan/hctz held in setting of resolved but Patient was normotensive while inpatient and had poor PO intake, so his anti-hypertensives were not restarted. These should be restarted as indicated in the weight:bold;">outpatient setting. Post-op precautions: RLE weight bearing as tolerated with posterior precautions. Lovenox: Continue for 4 weeks (day 1: . Transaminitis: Pt had elevated AST and ALT on , which were downtrending on . RUQUS showed abnormal liver morphology that is unchanged from prior, as well as small perihepatic ascites which was confirmed with radiology to be too small to tap. Please check LFTs in weeks to ensure that these have normalized. He has follow-up with his GI doctor ( ) in , but this appointment can be moved up if LFTs have not normalized. Foley: Pt d/c-ED with Foley in place because of post-op urinary retention. Please do a void trial in one week from weight:bold;">discharge ( ). Anemia: Hgb downtrended from 13 on admission to 7.9 on weight:bold;">discharge. Stool was guiac positive; this should be followed up, likely w/colonoscopy, as an weight:bold;">outpatient. HPI: Mr. is a year old paraplegic secondary to severe spinal stenosis who was recently hospitalized at from for RLE cellulitis in the setting of taking high dose steroids for Crohn's disease-related uveitis. He underwent operative I&D on . Intraoperative wound cultures eventually grew out group A beta-strep. Blood cultures from later in his hospital course grew coag-negative staph. A VAC was applied to his wound, and he was discharged on a 2-week course of Linezolid per ID recommendations. He was re-admitted to twice in the interim, once for presumed pneumonia & UTI (treated with Zosyn and Cipro), and the second time for an apparent viral URI (no antimicrobials given). During this time, the patient has been off steroids and completed his course of antibiotics. More recently, the patient reports he has had persistent/worsening pain in his right ankle. He spoke to his about his concerns, who recommended he go to the hospital. He returned to , where his RLE was scanned and this reportedly showed evidence of small fluid collections. The patient then developed hypotension requiring pressors and he was therefore transferred to for further mamangement. Hospital Course: Mr. was admitted to the SICU and evaluated by the Acute Care service. He was on Levophed to maintain a MAP > 60 but that was easily weaned off after fluid resuscitation. His right foot was subsequently I&D'd at the bedside and cultures were obtained. His WBC was 12K. He was placed on Vancomycin and Zosyn and the Infectious Disease service was consulted. He was transferred to the Surgical floor the following day as he continued to be hemodynamically stable. The wound care nurse made recommendations for his left foot ulcers which were improving since his last admission and his right foot was cared for locally with saline damp to dry dressings BID. His right leg was improving daily in that the cellulitis was contained to the lower leg only and he had much less edema. On his antibiotics were changed to oral Linezolid from IV Vancomycin and Zosyn after final wound culture revealed CNS. The ID service felt that the most likely source of his recurrent leg cellulitis was staph or strep both of which he has had in the past and therefore recommended an additional 14 day course of Linezolid. He will be followed closely by the ID service and the ACS service. After showing daily improvement of his wounds he was discharged to home on with services for wound care. HPI: Mr. is a year old paraplegic secondary to severe spinal stenosis who was recently hospitalized at from for RLE cellulitis in the setting of taking high dose steroids for Crohn's disease-related uveitis. He underwent Incision and Drainage on . Intraoperative wound cultures eventually grew out group A beta-strep. Blood cultures from later in his hospital course grew coag-negative staph. A VAC was applied to his wound, and he was discharged on a 2-week course of Linezolid per ID recommendations. He was re-admitted to twice in the interim, once for presumed Lung infection & UTI (treated with Zosyn and Cipro), and the second time for an apparent viral URI (no antimicrobials given). During this time, The Patient has been off steroids and completed his course of antibiotics. More recently, The Patient reports he has had Progressive pain in his right ankle. He spoke to his about his concerns, who recommended he go to the hospital. He returned to , where his RLE was scanned and this reportedly showed evidence of small fluid collections. The Patient then developed hypotension requiring pressors and he was therefore transferred to for further mamangement. Hospital Course: The Patient was admitted to the SICU and Assessed by the Acute Care service. He was on Levophed to maintain a MAP > 60 but that was easily weaned off after fluid resuscitation. His right foot was subsequently I&D'd at the bedside and cultures were obtained. His WBC was 12K. He was placed on Vancomycin and Zosyn and the Infectious Disease service was consulted. He was transferred to the Surgical floor the following day as he continued to be hemodynamically stable. The wound care nurse made recommendations for his left foot ulcers which were improving since his last admission and his right foot was cared for locally with saline damp to dry dressings BID. His right leg was improving daily in that the cellulitis was contained to the lower leg only and he had much less edema. On his antibiotics were changed to oral Linezolid from IV Vancomycin and Piperacillin-Tazobactam after final wound culture revealed CNS. The ID service felt that the most likely source of his recurrent leg cellulitis was staph or strep both of which he has had in the past and therefore recommended an additional 14 day course of Linezolid. He will be followed closely by the ID service and the ACS service. After showing daily improvement of his wounds he was discharged to home on with services for wound care.

Original Text Perturbed Text
HPI: The patient is a male with history of CAD, remote smoking, and ESRD on dialysis. He currently resides in the . He burnt his left foot in a sweat lodge two months prior to presentation on . This was treated for an infection x2. Prior to this event he had known left leg claudication symptoms at approximately one half mile which is not limiting for him. He currently is not taking any antibiotics and the wound is being dressed with simple wet-to- dry dressings every other day. Hospital Course: The patient presented as detailed above. On review of his ultrasound ultrasound he had significant common iliac artery stenosis with chronic claudication in addition to the significant ulcer on the foot which probed deeply and required surgery for healing. The patient was recommended optimization of his circulation before surgery on his foot wound. For that he underwent an arteriogram and left SFA & CIA stent stenting as per the clinical pathway. The patient tolerated the procedure well (see operating notes)and was transferred to the floor in stable condition. Podiatry was consulted for his foot wound. Foot X-rays were performed and the patient underwent a left toe met head and proximal phalanx resection (see operative notes). The patient tolerated the procedures well and was transferred to the floor in stable condition. Currently the patient is doing well, assymptomatic and is being discharged to the prison facility. HPI: with ESRD with obtructive nephropathy s/p bilateral nephrostomy tubes on coumadin for afib, DM2 who presents with hematuria and BRBPR after being found to have an elevated INR to 12.3. Patient's daughter measured her INR this morning and it was 8. Went to for another INR reading, which was 12.3. Patient did not have bleeding at this time but upon going home noticed blood when attempting to use the restroom and so came to the ED. Patient and family noted blood in the nephrostomy tubes as well. Patient denies headache, lightheadedness/dizziness, confusion, vision/hearing changes, chest pain, palpitations, sob. states her abdomen feels "funny." When asked to explain further states "I feel like I'm bleeding." No overt pain, however. Had nonbloody diarrhea 2 days ago that has since stopped. Denies dysuria. Denies fever, chills. Patient's daughter administers all patient's meds and checks her INR weekly. She said that for the past 4 weeks, patient's INR has been in the 1's. Her dose has not changed, she continues on 1.5mg daily. She has not started any new abx or medications. Her diet is very consistent. She has been eating slighlty less over the past week. She had diarrhea 2 days ago that has since stopped. In the ED, initial vitals: 98.1 100 120/54 20 94%. Labs notable for INR 13.4. Hgb 10.7 (baseline 10). Gross blood around nephrostomy tubes. Rectal exam was guaiac positive but unclear if frank blood was coming just from vagina or also from rectum. Given 10mg IV vitamin K, 1u FFP, KCentra, and home metoprolol XL. Has 20G PIVs x2. Urology and GI consulted. CT scan showed ureteral and bladder bleeding with mild left hydroureteralnephrosis. Will get head CT prior to transfer. HPI: Mr. is a right-handed man with a history of DM and CAD who presents with dizziness. He was in his USOH until yesterday ( ) when he developed lightheadedness. He describes waking up in the morning and as he sat up becoming lightheaded, which worsened when he tried to stand. For the next 4 to 5 hours, every time he rose or even tried to lift his head up from bending forward he became lightheaded and believes he may have had a darkening of his vision but is not sure. He was perspiring a lot all day. He had mild nausea. He specifically denies vertigo or disequilibrium at this time. However, around 9 pm he was getting out of his car and had a distinct feeling of being off balance without having the lightheaded feeling (also still without vertigo). He could not walk straight and felt as if his body was being pulled to the left. He had to lean against the wall to keep from falling over. The worst symptoms lasted minutes but he had some residual difficulty walking straight for half an hour. On neuro ROS, Mr. he also reports at the time of this disequilibrium a high-pitched ringing in his ears, L > R. He denies headache, loss of vision, blurred vision, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, vertigo, or hearing difficulty. Denies focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae. For the next 4 to 5 hours, every time he rose or even tried to Raise his head up from bending forward he became lightheaded and believes he may have had a darkening of his vision but is not sure. He was perspiring a lot all day. He had mild nausea. He specifically denies vertigo or disequilibrium at this time. However, around 9 pm he was getting out of his car and had a distinct feeling of being off balance without having the lightheaded feeling (also still without vertigo). He could not walk straight and felt as if his Torso was being pulled to the left. He had to lean against the wall to keep from falling over. HPI: Mr. is a yo male with history of CAD, s/p NSTEMI (unknown anatomy 2 stents placed), sinus node dysfunction and multiple episodes of syncope over past 9 months, and hypertension who presents with an episode of syncope today. Patient reports that he went for a short walk and acutely felt lightheaded and also felt as those his legs were going to give out. He recalls falling into a snow bank and believes he lost consciousness. When he came to he noticed pain in his left hip which made it difficult to get up. He was able to walk home and call . He believes that he hit the L side of his head when he fell. He does have a HA but denies any nausea, vomiting or visual disturbances. The episode of syncope was not preceded by chest pain or shortness of breath though he may have experienced palpitations. Notably, the patient had another syncopal episode 2 days ago, also while taking a walk. During that event he fell but did not lose consciousness. In total he has had about 5 syncopal episodes over the past 8 months. He is followed by his cardiologist Dr. was planning on placing a pacemaker on . . In the ED vitals were T 100.3 HR 76 BP 135/65 RR 16 O2 Sat 95% RA. Patient had xray of left hip that was negative for fx or dislocation. . On the floor, patient reports feeling well except for a headache that started after fall. L hip only hurts when he tries to move around. Denies any lightheadedness or dizziness at this time. . On review of systems, he denies any prior history of stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. He denies recent fevers, chills or rigors. He denies exertional buttock or calf pain. All of the other review of systems were negative. . Cardiac review of systems is notable occasional shortness of breath with exertion x years. He denies chest pain,paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema . Hospital Course: with history of CAD, s/p NSTEMI, sinus node dysfunction and multiple episodes of syncope presenting with a syncopal episode and L hip pain. . . Syncope/Sinus Node Dysfunction: Numerous syncopal episodes in past several months most likely due to sinus node dysfxn, and pt was being worked up by Dr. pacemaker placement. Therefore, pt was admitted for pacer placement. He received Dual chamber pacemaker through Left cephalic, no complications. Received IV Vanco while admitted and discharged with short PO ABx course. evaluated him and deemed safe to go home, with services. Appt was made for pt to see Dr. device clinic f/u one week after discharge. . . CAD/s/p NSTEMI: No chest pain or active issues while admitted. Continued on home regimen of ASA, statin, ACE, clopidogrel, fish oil. Unclear why pt on Plavix when his NSTEMI was in , but deferred to primary cardiologist and continued on discharge. . Hematuria: Pt seen to have gross painless hematuria which spontaneously resolved through admission, however did have small amt just before discharge. Was concerning in setting of a recent fall, however CT abd/pelvis did not show any internal bleeding. DRE normal and was guiac negative. No change in vitals, Hct monitored and stable. Likely result of ASA, Plavix, and was scheduled for pain control with Ibuprofen until this was stopped for the bleeding. Also received one dose of subQ heparin. He was deemed stable and was to f/u 2 days after discharge with his PCP who was notified of this hematura. Will need f/u UA as outpt. . . L hip Pain: Had large ecchymosis from fall. Plain films without fx or dislocations. Pain initially controlled with scheduled Ibuprofen until hematuria developed at which point was stopped. Pain was not large and pt did not require further pain meds. . Large L renal cyst: Seen on CT abd. Pt states he's known about this for years. Per rads, appears benign. Would follow. HPI: female with hx of DM, obesity, kidney stones with stent placement in the past here with abaominal pain. Pt reports that yesterday she developed acute on set of pain, initiall in the left side/flank with radiation around to the pelvis. She began to have nausea and about episodes of NBNB vomiting. She had no diarrhea. The pain since she become localized to the central abdomen and epigastric region. The pain at times is quite severe and is a tightness/sharp pain. It is always present, but worses in waves. She reports that she thinks that her prior stone pain was similar to this, but doesn't remember its location very well. She reports prior gallbladder pain was different in that it was so bad she was going to die, she could not localize the pain at that time. She is also conceredn that her BS was high for her at 200. She denies fevers, has felt cold. SHe rpeorts that in the past 6 months she has been plagued with generalized waekness. SHe has been working with her MDs to determine the cause. shoed gastritis and a polyp. SHe denies weight loss. HPI: Mr. is a year old man with a history of hairy cell leukemia s/p cladribine on who presented to the ER with generalized weakness and a fall the day prior to admission. He reports feeling unwell for the past few days with fatigue and according to his wife has been eating and drinking much less. He cannot recall if he experienced any lightheaded symptoms prior to the fall and does not know the circumstances surrounding the fall. He was recently in a rehab facility after receiving Cladribine and was treated for PNA with levaquin, now home and off abx. In the emergency department, initial vitals: 97.8 71 121/71 18 100%. CXR and head CT were clear. ECG showed a junctional rhythm with a rate of 69 bpm. No ST/T changes. . Review of systems: (+) Per HPI (-) Denies fever, chills, night sweats, recent weight loss or gain. Denies headache, sinus tenderness, rhinorrhea or congestion. Denied cough, shortness of breath. Denied chest pain or tightness, palpitations. Denied nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation or abdominal pain. No recent change in bowel or bladder habits. No dysuria. Denied arthralgias or myalgias. Hospital Course: Mr. is a y/o M with PMH significant for atrial fibrillation, systolic & diastolic heart failure (EF 30%), hypertension, and hairy cell leukemia who was admitted to the oncology service with weakness and lethargy and was noted to have bradycardia was transferred to for management of symptomatic bradycardia. Symptomatic Bradycardia: Pt was noted to have HRs in the on the onc service, which while not associated with syncope, may be leading to symptoms of weakness and lethargy. His recent decline in functional status may be due to bradyarrhythmia. He was on a beta-blocker at low dose which was stopped. Despite this, he continued to be bradycardic in the and . Since patients weakness and lethargy could be from the bradycardia it was discussed with patient and family and decided that a pacemaker would be implanted to treat the bradycardia with the hope that this would improve his current symptoms. Pt had a dual chamber PPM permanent pacemaker placed on . He also had episodes of AVNRT so metoprolol succinate 25 mg PO daily was restarted. He was discharged on Cephalexin 250 mg PO Q8H Duration for 2 days for prophylaxis for pacemaker placement. Atrial Fibrillation: CHADS2 score of so should be on anticoagulation but is not currently. We held anticoagulation given that he needed a pacemaker insertion. Spoke with outpatient cardiologist who wants to hold off on anticoagulation given patients history of multiple prior falls at this time. Pt will consider starting coumadin when he follows up with his outpatient cardiologist. chronic systolic & diastolic heart failure (EF 30%): We stopped his beta-blocker given his symptomatic bradycardia. We continued aspirin 325 mg PO daily. Would consider starting lisinopril 2.5 mg daily as outpatient if renal function and BP remains stable. Dementia: Pt with normal TSH of 1.1 and Vitamin B12 level of 840. We stopped Aricept due to anticholinergic effect and continued Memantine 5 mg PO daily. Hairy Cell Leukemia: Pt is s/p 1 cycle of Cladribine. Pt was going to get Rituxan on but this is currently on hold. Pt will follow up with outpatient oncologist Dr. . Transitional Issues: 1. Pt to discuss starting anticoagulation and ACE-inhibitor with outpatient cardiologist 2. Pt will follow up with Dr. Hairy cell Leukemia 3. Pt will follow up in device clinic in 1 week on and then with Dr. in EP.
