HPI: yo G4P0 with symptomatic uterine fibroids desires abdominal myomectomy. Symptoms are heavy menses and persistent bloating. She had a Mirena IUD but requested removal after side effects (acne and weight gain). She would like to preserve fertility although she does not currently have a partner. : : Measures: 9.7 x 6.0 x 7. nteverted Description: Heterogeneous myometrium with multiple fibroids. The largest 3 were measured. 1. Right fundus 4.7 x 4.3 x 4.5 cm intramural 2. Anterior left mid body 2.1 x 2.2 x 2.0 cm intramural 3. Posterior mid body 3.0 x 1.7 x 2.3 cm subserosal Endometrium: Thickness: 0.9 cm Description: Visualized endometrium complex is within normal limits. Margins are somewhat indistinct due to heterogeneous myometrium. Incidental note of nabothian cysts in the cervix. Free fluid: None Right Ovary: Measures: 2.6 x 1.5 x 2.7 cm Description: Unremarkable sonographic appearance Left Ovary: Measures: 3.9 x 1.5 x 2.6 cm Description: Unremarkable sonographic appearance Hospital Course: On , Ms. was admitted to the gynecology service after undergoing abdominal myomectomy. Please see the operative report for full details. Her post-operative course was uncomplicated. Immediately post-op, her pain was controlled with IV Dilaudid/Toradol . On post-operative day 1, her urine output was adequate so her foley was removed and she voided spontaneously. Her diet was advanced without difficulty and she was transitioned to PO oxycodone/ibuprofen/acetaminophen. By post-operative day 2, she was tolerating a regular diet, voiding spontaneously, ambulating independently, and pain was controlled with oral medications. She was then discharged home in stable condition with outpatient follow-up scheduled. HPI: yo Nullipara with symptomatic uterine fibroids desires abdominal myomectomy. On post-operative day 1, her urine output was adequate so her foley was removed and she Micturated spontaneously. Her diet was advanced without difficulty and she was transitioned to PO oxycodone/ibuprofen/acetaminophen. By post-operative day 2, she was tolerating a regular diet, voiding spontaneously, ambulating independently, and pain was controlled with oral medications. She was then discharged home in stable condition with outpatient follow-up scheduled.

Original Text Perturbed Text
HPI: is a F with PMH of migraines and right vertebral dissection in on ASA who presents as a transfer from OSH found to have a new right-sided brain lesion concerning for tumor. Patient states that over the past ~1 month she has had difficulty keeping her travel plans and appointments in order. Approximately 1 week ago, the patient experienced changes in her smelling sensation. At first she felt that she had lost her sense of smell; then she realized that she started to get a strong metallic smell. On , the patient describes a burning metallic smell that was so strong she thought her car was malfunctioning; when she arrived home, she asked her fiancé to call EMS. En route to OSH with EMS, the patient reportedly had a violet seizure for which EMS was holding her down with force to keep her from falling off the stretcher. At OSH, MRI brain revealed a 7x8x9mm enhancing lesion in the right hippocampus with surrounding edema and extension into the choroid plexus. She was also noted to have new T9 compression fracture on T-spine XRay. She was transferred to for neurosurgical evaluation for the new brain lesion. hospital Course: The patient was admitted from the emergency department with MR imaging concerning for a new right hippocampal brain lesion, highly suspicious for malignant lesion. She also was found to have a T9 compression fracture on T-spine XRay. Right brain lesion with associated vasogenic edema MRI brain from the outside hospital revealed a 7x8x9mm enhancing lesion R hippocampus extending into chroid plexus with surrounding edema. Neurology-oncology and radiation oncology were consulted. Neuro-oncology recommended an LP for infectious workup and cytology, which was negative. The patient is planned for stereotactic biopsy on . Aspirin was held. Mesial temporal lobe epilepsy Given the seizure presentation, she was started on 1g keppra BID, which was uptitrated to 1.5g BID. cEEG was obtained, with multiple push-button events for metallic taste and strong scents, which revealed slowing, without clear epileptiform discharges. T9 compression fracture As above, CT Chest with and without contrast was ordered, which demonstrated mild superior endplate deformities to T8 and T9, without retropulsion. This was determined to be non-surgical, and a TLSO brace was ordered. Discharge planning The patient was discharged home in stable condition on with plan to return for elective biopsy on . HPI: Mr. is a year old man with a history of alcohol use disorder in the setting of homelessness, depression, anxiety, and PTSD who is presenting as a transfer from in for management of high risk alcohol withdrawal. Patient states that he has been drinking about 1.5L of vodka daily for the last 3 months and decided to check himself into a treatment facility in yesterday, last drink the AM of . He states that he began to have adrenergic symptoms with associated hypertension and tachycardia and was sent to ED for medical management of his alcohol withdrawal given his history of prior seizure and very heavy recent alcohol use. He plans on returning to once detoxed here. In addition to alcohol use, he also notes about 1 pack per day of tobacco use and occasionally smokes crack cocaine. He has injected drugs in the past, which is how he acquired HCV but no longer engages in this practice. He has a toxic family life and no friends that he can trust, as such he often lives on the streets which is when he starts drinking. He is currently unemployed without a source of income. He does not have a PCP, but takes the medications listed below every day from providers who give him short term prescriptions. In the ED was given diazepam 20mg IV, then 520mg of phenobarb over a period of 5 hours. weighs 200 lbs -> 90kg, 900mg is a 10mg/kg load. Now a 7 out of 10 overall compared to when he felt his worst at , feels anxious and reved up, can't relax, hands shaking, sweaty/hot. REVIEW OF SYSTEMS Negative except as stated in HPI. HPI: Ms. is a F with a known history of HTN/cervical Cancer who presented following a left basal ganglia hemorrhagic stroke. Per report, she called her husband from home several times throughout the day saying "she did not feel well." Once he arrived home in the evening he reportedly found her thrashing, moving all extremities, combative on the ground at which time he called EMS. She was brought to an outside hospital where CT head demonstrated left basal ganglia hemorrhagic stroke following which she was transferred to for further evaluation and care and was admitted to the neuro-med service. On presentation to the trauma bay she was noted to be expressively aphasic with significant physical exam findings of right upper and lower extremity complete loss of motor function and sensation. She remains motor/sensation in tact in the left upper and lower extremities. ICU Summary: On admission ( ) patient was maintained with hydralazine and Labetalol /w goal SBP <160. Neurosurgery was consulted who did not recommend surgical intervention. Course complicated by EtOH withdrawal and was placed on phenobarbital protocol and max dose Precedex gtt and weaned prior to transfer. Failed swallow eval, and required an NGT. A repeat head CT showed improvement in midline shift from 8 to 4mm. He was started on captopril and amLODIPine prior to transfer. Hospital Course: Ms. is a yo woman with hypertension, cervical cancer and EtOH abuse who was admitted with a left basal ganglia hemorrhage. Exam significant for mixed aphasia and right lower facial droop, flaccid right hemiplegia and dense hemisensory deficit with inattention to the right side of space. Given location and appearance of bleed as well as comorbid uncontrolled hypertension, suspect hypertensive etiology of the hemorrhage. Hospital course c/b EtOH withdrawal, uncontrolled hypertension, and UTI. Left basal ganglia hemorrhage: On admission ( ) she was evaluated by Neurology who recommend SBP <160 with hydralazine PRN for blood pressure parameters, with MRI. Neurosurgery consulted who did not recommend surgical intervention at this time and elected to follow serial exams. Neurologic deficits stabilized both clinically and by imaging. Failed speech/swallow evaluations x2 and required an NGT. Swallow study improved on , and video laryngoscopy on showed no signs of aspiration, and therefore Ms. was started on a pureed diet with honey prethickened liquids and medications crushed. EtOH withdrawal: Previously drank 3 bottles of wine per day. During hospitalization, she experienced significant withdrawal requiring phenobarbital and maximum doses of precedex. Her withdrawal limited transfer out of the ICU for several days. She was treated with high-dose thiamine, Folic acid and multivitamins. Precedex was eventually tapered off, followed by a phenobarbital taper. She has been off of phenobarbital since without signs of withdrawal. UTI: fever, mild leukocytosis on , prompting infectious workup, which revealed an abnormal urinalysis concerning for UTI. She was started on ceftriaxone , and urine culture then grew >100,000 cfu/mL E. coli, sensitive to ceftriaxone and Bactrim (among other antibiotics). Blood cultures have NGTD, and chest radiograph was without signs of pneumonia. Hypertension: Previously on amlodipine 10 mg daily alone at home. Blood pressure was maintained under goal BP 150/90. She required uptitration of medications, and was eventually stabilized on regimen of amlodipine 10 mg daily and captopril 75mg tid, which was converted to lisinopril 50 mg daily on discharge. Transitional Issues: - Continue uptitration of antihypertensive regimen as needed for goal normotension. - Regarding her aspirin: From a neurological perspective, it would be safe, if necessary from PCP's perspective, to restart at weeks post-bleed ( ). This will also be addressed at Neurologic follow-up in . AHA/ASA Core Measures for Intracerebral hemorrhage 1. Dysphagia screening before any PO intake? (x) Yes - () No 2. DVT Prophylaxis administered? (x) Yes - () No 3. Smoking cessation counseling given? () Yes - (x) No [reason () non-smoker - (x) unable to participate] 4. Stroke education (personal modifiable risk factors, how to activate EMS for stroke, stroke warning signs and symptoms, prescribed medications, need for followup) given (verbally or written)? (x) Yes - () No 5. Assessment for rehabilitation and/or rehab services considered? HPI: This is a gentleman with a pmhx. of advanced unresectable pancreatic cancer s/p CK, treatment with gemcitabine and FOLFIRINOX, also with multiple episodes of polymicrobial sepsis requiring ICU admissions, who is admitted with fevers to 101.1 and hypotension. Patient was seen in clinic on day of admission for scheduled chemotherapy. At the visit, he was found to have a BP of 78/44 and to be orthostatic. Blood cultures were drawn, he was given a unit of blood, and chemotherapy was held. Mr. was at home after appointment and felt chilled; his wife took his temperature and he was 101.1. He subsequently proceeded to ED for further evaluation. Mr. was diagnosed with pancreatic cancer in when he developed symptoms of obstructive juandice. He underwent ERCP, which demonstrated CBD stricture and a stent was placed. CBD brushing showed aytpical cells and pancreas FNA on demonstrated malignant cells consistent with adenocarcinoma. Treatment thus far has consisted of CK, gemcitabine (as bridge during CK) and FOLFIRINOX. In the ED, initial vitals were: 101.8 91 113/64 18 95% ra. Patient received 3L of IV, vancomycin, zosyn and tylenol. After fluids, patient became slightly hypoxemic but then recovered sats. On admission, vitals were: 99.0 85 101/60 13 100% on 4L. Hospital course: This is a gentleman with a pmhx. significant for locally advanced pancreatic cancer on FOLFIRINOX, with a history of polymicrobial bacteremia, now admitted with fever at home, hypotension, and gram positive cocci in blood. SEPSIS: Patient was recently admitted to OMED for ecoli sepsis, was on prophylactic antibiotics (cipro, levaquin) at home. He was admitted to OMED with fever at home and vanc . enterococcus from blood cultures. At the time of admission, there was a concern for sepsis given positive cultures and relative hypotension. He was started empirically on vancomycin and zosyn, home levaquin was discontinued. Infectious disease was consulted. No source was identified, although likely culprit was thought to be a recent GI procedure (stent exchange 2 weeks prior to admission). Serial cultures were obtained, both from the port and peripherally, and only set of cultures that were positive were enterococci from day of admission. Through his hospitalization, patient remained afebrile, hemodynamically stable with SBP>90s-100s (on metoprolol) and asymptomatic. Given concern that bacteremia could seed port, it was removed. Since CBC showed absolute eosinophilia with eos >500 on two consecutive days, vancomycin and zosyn were discontinued, and he was switched to daptomycin (based off sensitivities) and oral levaquin based off ID recs. Once the port was removed, patient was switched to oral linezolid and levaquin to complete a two week course. PANCREATIC CANCER: Locally advanced unresectable disease. On outpatient FOLFIRINOX which has been held given bacteremia and recent admissions. Chemo held during admission, further management deferred to outpatient providers. DVT: Diagnosed during hospitalization in . On therapeutic lovenox at home that was continued inhouse. CAD/CM/Afib: Continued metoprolol for rate control along with lovenox and ASA for anticoagulation. DMII: Oral hypoglycemics held at admission, continued on Lantus and ISS. These were restarted at discharge. HYPERLIPIDEMIA: Continue simvastatin, since niacin and fibrate not on formulary TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: CBC/Diff to be followed by Dr HPI: This is a gentleman with a pmhx. of advanced unresectable pancreatic cancer s/p CK, treatment with gemcitabine and FOLFIRINOX, also with multiple episodes of polymicrobial sepsis requiring ICU admissions, who is admitted with fevers to 101.1 and hypotension. Patient was seen in clinic on day of admission for scheduled chemotherapy. At the visit, he was found to have a BP of 78/44 and to be orthostatic. Blood cultures were drawn, he was given a unit of blood, and chemotherapy was held. The Patient was at Residence after appointment and felt chilled; his wife took his temperature and he was 101.1. He subsequently proceeded to ED for further evaluation. Mr. was diagnosed with pancreatic cancer in when he developed symptoms of obstructive juandice. He underwent ERCP, which demonstrated CBD stricture and a stent was placed. CBD brushing showed aytpical cells and pancreas FNA on demonstrated malignant cells consistent with adenocarcinoma. Treatment thus far has consisted of CK, gemcitabine (as bridge during CK) and FOLFIRINOX. In the ED, initial vitals were: 101.8 91 113/64 18 95% ra. Patient received 3L of IV, vancomycin, zosyn and Acetaminophen. After fluids, patient became slightly hypoxemic but then recovered sats. On admission, vitals were: 99.0 85 101/60 13 100% on 4L. Hospital Course: This is a gentleman with a pmhx. significant for locally advanced pancreatic cancer on FOLFIRINOX, with a history of polymicrobial bacteremia, now admitted with fever at home, hypotension, and gram positive cocci in blood. SEPSIS: Patient was recently admitted to OMED for ecoli sepsis, was on prophylactic antibiotics (Ciprofloxacin, levaquin) at home. He was admitted to OMED with fever at home and vanc . enterococcus from blood cultures. At the time of admission, there was a concern for sepsis given positive cultures and relative hypotension. He was started empirically on vancomycin and zosyn, home levaquin was discontinued. Infectious disease was consulted. No source was identified, although likely culprit was thought to be a recent GI procedure (stent exchange 2 weeks prior to admission). Serial cultures were obtained, both from the port and peripherally, and only set of cultures that were positive were enterococci from day of admission. Through his Hospitalization, patient remained afebrile, hemodynamically stable with SBP>90s-100s (on metoprolol) and asymptomatic. Given concern that bacteremia could seed port, it was removed. Since CBC showed absolute eosinophilia with eos >500 on two consecutive days, vancomycin and Piperacillin-Tazobactam were discontinued, and he was switched to daptomycin (based off sensitivities) and oral levaquin based off ID recs. Once the port was Withdrawn, patient was switched to oral linezolid and levaquin to complete a two week Treatment regimen. PANCREATIC CANCER: Locally advanced unresectable disease. On outpatient FOLFIRINOX which has been held given bacteremia and recent admissions. Chemo held during Hospitalization, further management deferred to outpatient providers. DVT: Diagnosed during Hospitalization in . On therapeutic lovenox at home that was continued inhouse. CAD/CM/Afib: Continued metoprolol for rate control along with lovenox and ASA for anticoagulation. DMII: Oral hypoglycemics held at admission, continued on Lantus and ISS. These were restarted at discharge. HYPERLIPIDEMIA: Continue simvastatin, since Nicotinic acid and fibrate not on formulary TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: CBC/Diff to be followed by Dr

HPI: This is a year old male with IDDM, who was admitted to the hospital on for a displaced half pin from external fixator right foot. He is weeks s/p R STJ and TN arthrodesis and application of external fixator with his hospital course complicated with wound infection, exposed hardware, s/p I+D is currently being treated with a 6 week course of IV Vancomycin and ERtapenem. Patient was due to stop IV antibiotics on and remain on PO suppression under hardware is removed. The Patient presented to the ED on day of admission with a concern for half pin in foot being displaced and now out of his skin. Denies fevers/chlls/nausea/vomiting/sob/cp. Hospital Course: The Patient was admitted to the podiatric surgery service on for loosening of the external fixator on his Right Lower Extremity. On admission, he was continued on current IV antibiotics of Vancomycin and Ertapenem. Ertapenem was switched to meropenem per hospital protocol. His Vancomcin dosing was increased per the Infectious Disease team based on his vancomycin trough being too low. He was taken to the OR for removal of tibial pins and debridement of the pin sites on . Pt was evaluated by anesthesia and taken to the operating room. There were no adverse events in the operating room; please see the operative note for details. Afterwards, pt was taken to the PACU in stable condition, then transferred to the ward for observation. A Wound VAC was applied to his Right plantar heel ulcer and medial foot ulcer. He was fitted for a bi-valved cast which was well padded to take pressure off of his heel ulcer. He remained NWB to the Right foot. Post-operatively, the Patient remained afebrile with stable vital signs; pain was well controlled oral pain medication on a PRN basis. The Patient remained stable from both a cardiovascular and pulmonary standpoint. His intake and output were closely monitored and noted to be adequtae. The Patient received subcutaneous heparin throughout admission. The Patient was noted to be delirious with psychosis during the hospital stay. He was seen by the medical team and several of his medications were changed. He was given Seroquel with noted improvement. He was placed on delirium precautions. He was evaluated by the Physical therapy team who recommended that he be sent to rehab. The Patient was subsequently discharged to home on POD5/HD6 to rehab with instructions for IV antibiotics (vancomycin and ertapenem), wound care including Wound VAC, Physical Therapy, and delirium precautions. The Patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan. During the admission through , patient had her tunneled line pulled and replaced two days later. She continued to receive Vancomycin while at HD. The patient presented yesterday with increased pain at the site of her right femoral dialysis line. Patient denies fevers, chills. She reports stable dyspena since her discharge on . She says that her chronic abdominal pain is stable. . Upon arrival to the ED 100.8 20 95% RA. Patient complained of right femoral pain at the site of her catheter. She received a dose of Vancomycin and a dose of Zosyn for questionable pneumonia on CXR. . Overnight, patient complains of persisted pain in the right femoral area at the site of her femoral catheter and also improved shortness of breath. Hospital Course: with SLE, ESRD on HD, and malignant hypertension was admitted from the emergency department with right groin pain at the sign of dialysis catheter, stable dyspnea, and abdominal pain and found to have ? PNA on CXR. . Right groin pain: Etiology likely dialysis line placement. Patient with resolved low grade fever since admission, no leukocytosis. U/S in ED unrevealing for abscess or pseudoaneurysm. Initial Xray questions consolidation but patient has no fever, cough and infiltrates have been there since untreated. Patient saturated well on room air and dyspnea improved throughout hospitalization after she was dialyzed. Repeat cxr after dialysis consistent with resolving pulmonary edema. . Abdominal pain: Stable. Chronic in nature. Patient has hx of SBO, although exam was reassuring with positive bowel sounds and +flatus. We continued to avoid IV pain meds and continued home hydromorphone, lidocaine patch, fentanyl, gabapentin. . HPI: Mrs. is a woman who presents with bulge and tissue protrusion for the last years. She states that her "bladder is falling out." She did have a pessary in the past, but developed a vaginal infection that was removed about a year ago. She does not want to have another pessary placed. She wishes to have surgical repair. She also complains of urinary incontinence with coughing and sneezing. She wears a small pad. She goes to the bathroom about four times a day and wakes up twice at night to urinate. She does complain of urgency and urge-type incontinence as well. Hospital Course: On , Ms. was admitted to the gynecology service after undergoing a robotic-assisted supra-cervical hysterectomy, bilateral salpingooophorectomy, sacrocolpopexy, tension-free vaginal sling and cystoscopy for pelvic organ prolapse and stress urinary incontinence. Please see the operative report for full details. Her post-operative course was uncomplicated. Immediately post-op, her pain was controlled with IV dilaudid and tordal. On post-operative day 1, her urine output was adequate and her Foley was removed with a voiding trial, the results of which are as follows: 1. Instilled 300 mL, voided 350 mL with 5 mL residual. 2. Instilled 300 mL, voided 350 mL. Her diet was advanced without difficulty and she was transitioned to oral dilaudid, ibuprofen and acetaminophen (pain meds). By post-operative day 1, she was tolerating a regular diet, voiding spontaneously, ambulating independently, and pain was controlled with oral medications. She was then discharged home in stable condition with outpatient follow-up scheduled. HPI: Mrs. is a woman who presents with bulge and tissue protrusion for the last years. She goes to the bathroom about four times a day and wakes up twice at night to urinate. She does complain of urgency and urge-type incontinence as well. Hospital Course: On , Ms. was admitted to the gynecology service after undergoing a robotic-assisted supra-cervical hysterectomy, bilateral salpingooophorectomy, sacrocolpopexy, tension-free vaginal sling and Urinary tract endoscopy for pelvic organ prolapse and stress urinary incontinence. On post-operative day 1, her urine output was adequate and her Foley was removed with a voiding trial, the results of which are as follows: 1. Instilled 300 mL, voided 350 mL with 5 mL residual. 2. Instilled 300 mL, voided 350 mL. Her diet was advanced without difficulty and she was transitioned to oral dilaudid, ibuprofen and acetaminophen (Analgesics). By post-operative day 1, she was tolerating a regular diet, voiding spontaneously, ambulating independently, and pain was controlled with oral medications. She was then discharged home in stable condition with outpatient follow-up scheduled.

HPI: woman past medical history of hyperlipidemia, carotid endarterectomy , and recent right hip fracture S/P ORIF in developed of a nonhealing right heel pressure ulcer and severe foot drop secondary to prolonged bedrest. She presents for angiogram and potential catheter based intervention. Hospital Course: VASCULAR SURGERY DISCHARGE SUMMARY Angio discharge summary: Ms. is an year old with non healing right heel ulcer after prolonged bedrest after hip fracture. She was admitted to the on and was taken to the endovascular suite and underwent a diagnostic angiogram. For details of the procedure, please see the surgeon's operative note. The patient tolerated the procedure well without complications and was brought to the post-anesthesia care unit in stable condition. After a brief stay, the patient was transferred to the vascular surgery floor where she remained through the rest of the hospitalization. Post-operatively, she did well without any groin swelling. She was able to tolerate a regular diet, get out of bed with assistance. Foley was discontinue and she is due to void at present. She was deemed ready for discharge. As her right lower extremity angiogram showed occlusion of the proximal superficial femoral artery through the popliteal artery with three-vessel runoff with occlusion of the posterior tibial and peroneal in the distal calf and predominant supply to the foot provided by the anterior tibial artery, we have arranged for the patient to return for right lower extremity bypass. Preoperative evaluation by anesthesia and vein mapping were done in the hospital. We have arranged for evaluation by Dr. prior to surgery for medical optimization and further cardiac testing if indicated. Dr. will contact the rehab with details of the surgery time and date when they are available. HPI: s/p colonoscopy with hot snare polypectomy of 1.2 cm sessile polyp in the ascending colon at on presented to an OSH with BRBPR. He was subsequently transferred to for further management. In the ED he was noted to have large amounts of bright red blood from his rectum. He was notably hypotensive despite initiation of the emergency transfusion protocol and the use of the rapid transfuser. While he was still in the ED he had a vasovagal episode and required a short period of chest compressions, but following initiation of ACLS protocol sinus rhythm was restored and his blood pressure increased. The patient was intubated, a MAC line placed in the RIJ, and he was resuscitated with 10 units prbc, 2 units FFP and 1 unit of platelets and phenyelphrine to support pressure. Despite transfusion the patient continued to be intermittently hypotensive, and thus he was brought to the OR for emergency colectomy hospital Course: The patient was admitted to the hospital with rectal bleeding. Upon admission,he was made NPO, and given intravenous fluids. While in the emergency room, he became hypotensive and the transfusion protocol was initiated. He was reported to have a vasovagal episode and required a short period of chest compressions. At this time, the patient was intubated, additional intravenous lines were placed and the patient received 10 units prbc, 2 units FFP and 1 unit of platelets. pressors were added for blood pressure support. The patient continued to have bouts of hypotensive and was brought to the operating room where he underwent an exploratory laparotomy, extended right hemicolectomy without anastomosis and temporary abdominal closure. During the operative course, the patient required additional blood products and had a 100cc blood loss. Because of the instability of the patient, the abdomen was left open. The patient was transported to the intensive care unit for hemodynamic monitoring. Initially he required pressors for blood pressure support. After stabilization of vital signs, the patient returned to the operating on POD 1 for re-anastomosis and closure of the abdomen. The operative course was stable with minimal blood loss. The patient required neosynephrine and levophed for blood pressure support. After the procedure, the patinet remained intubated and monitored in the intensive care unit. Post-operatively, there was concern for aspiration after gastric contents were suctioned from the endotracheal tube. In 24 hours, the pressors were weaned off and the patient was extubated. The patient resumed his aspirin and plavix. On POD 2, the patient was reported to have a drop in his hematocrit and the aspirin and plavix were held. The patient's hematocrit was closely monitored. On POD 4, the acute pain service was consulted for management of his surgical pain and the pain regimen was revised. After stabilization of vital signs, the patient was transferred to the surgical floor on POD 4. His bowel function was slow to return and the tube remained in place for bowel decompression. At this time, there was concern for an ileus. Serial hematocrits continued to be monitored. The and foley catheter were removed on POD 10 and the patient was started on sips and gradually advanced to a regular diet. After his hematocrit stabilized, he resumed his aspirin and plavix. The patient was evaluated by physcial therapy and after sessions, was cleared for discharge home with services. The patient was discharged home on POD 12 in stable condition. Follow-up appointments were made with the acute care service and with his primary care provider. On POD 4, the acute pain service was consulted for management of his surgical pain and the pain regimen was revised. After stabilization of vital signs, the patient was transferred to the surgical floor on POD 4. His bowel function was slow to return and the tube remained in place for bowel decompression. At this time, there was concern for an ileus. Serial hematocrits continued to be monitored. The and foley catheter were removed on POD 10 and the patient was started on sips and gradually advanced to a regular diet. After his hematocrit stabilized, he resumed his aspirin and plavix. The patient was evaluated by physcial therapy and after Appointments, was cleared for discharge home with services. The patient was discharged home on POD 12 in stable condition. Follow-up Appointments were made with the acute Care service and with his primary Care provider.

HPI: year old man with a history of hypertension ad no headache history who presented with headaches and hypertension to the 180s. The patient was driving and experienced sudden onset, severe posterior neck pain and headache which radiated to the crown and forehead and was associated with blurred vision as if a veil had been pulled over his eye, pins and needles over the let side of his face/head, and a flushed feeling. He went to a pharmacy and took his blood pressure, which was 187/114. The headache was constant throughout the day and continued overnight ad when he woke up. Yesterday, he began to feel throbbing over the left parietotemporal area as well. He took his blood pressure at the pharmacy again and it was 187/116. Per the patient, his blood pressure has never been nearly this high, but he does not remember how high it has been in the past. He denies any associated sweating or palpitations, or any recent BP medication changes. The patient was first started on anti-hypertensives about years ago and takes them regularly, one in the morning and one at night. He did not take additional blood pressure pills with this episode of hypertension. In the ED, his blood pressure was 193/106 initially. Wet read of a CTA neck showed patent carotid and vertebral arteries with no evidence of dissection, and a verbal report of the final impression prior to discharge was the same. He was given acetaminophen and nitroglycerin IV drip from 12:45am to 4:20am. It was discontinued due to SBP in the 130s. His headache subsided. He denies fever, chills, dizziness, palpitations, SOB, or chest pain. Hospital Course: Mr. is a year old man with a history of hypertension who presented with hypertensive emergency. Hypertensive emergency: Patient presented to the ED with recent sudden onset, severe, non-traumatic headache and severe hypertension. Troponin was negative x1 and EKG showed NSR with no ischemic changes, signs of hypertrophy, conduction defects of axis deviation. CTA neck was reassuring against a carotid dissection and neurological exam was consistently normal. CT head was negative for acute process including bleed. Patient was started on a nitroglycerine drip for goal SBP <140. Patient was continued on home losartan 100mg and felodipine was increased from 5mg to 15mg. Nitroglycerin gtt weaned on HD1. Given concerning history for possible subarachnoid hemorrhage with sentinel bleed from aneurysm, lumbar puncture was performed to more definitively rule out bleed. CSF was clear with no RBCs or xanthrochromia. The patient had a brief vasovagal syncopal event immediately after the procedure and was given a 500cc bolus of NS IV. He was observed overnight with stable SBP<130s with no headaches, chest pain, dyspnea or any other new symptoms. Mild renal insufficiency: unknown baseline, presented with creatinine 1.2. Unknown baseline - ?Mild vs. CKD stage II. Trended daily without any change. Encouraged oral fluid intake and was given 500cc NS as above. Likely sequela of longstanding hypertension. Recommended outpatient recheck at next PCP . HPI: History obtained via medical chart and through translator. This is an y/o male with HTN, CAD, DM2, CKD, who presents with symptoms of nausea and NB emesis x 2 days. The patient is unable to provide any history (although completely lucid and coherent) as to why he is in the hospital. The patient's daughter reports that the granddaughter recently had similar GI symptoms. However, she is worried about the patient living alone and feels that he is unable to take care of himself. She feels that his overall health is not good and wants him to have a full medical evaluation. Of note, the patient lives near his daughter, whose house burnt down recently; daughter and her family are staying in a hotel and the patient refused to come with them and has been staying alone in his apartment. The patient denies any f/c/s, chest pain, shortness of breath, or cough. He is unable to provide any further history. . In the ED, initial VS were T 100, BP 132/59, HR 87, RR 14, SaO2 92%/RA. His labs were notable for baseline anemia, thrombocytopenia, and Na of 127 which is new. He received 2 L NS. He was noted to be hypoxic to 86%/RA while in the ED, so CXR was obtained and was suggestive of a possible RUL infiltrate (per ED physician). He was given 1 gm IV CTX. Hospital Course: FAILURE TO THRIVE The patient was brought to hospital by daughter over concern for lethargy and poor appetite; he had a preceeding gastrointestinal illness with + family sick contacts which led to vomiting and diarrhea that were resolved on admission. His sodium was low, as discussed below. He had a full, cardiac/diabetic diet and did well with meals. He had moments where his affect was back at baseline per daughter and times where he was less than his usual state as described by daughter. HYPONATREMIA He appeared clinically euvolemic on exam with hyponatremia, with a history of recent volume losses with diarrhea/vomiting/anorexia. His sodium level was relatively unchanged by 2 L he received in the ED. He was placed on free water restriction of 1200cc. His sodium corrected to 135 by discharge. Hyponatremia was felt to be due most to poor PO intake. ACUTE SYSTOLIC CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE The patient had pulmonary effusions and elevated BNP. Echo showed new low-nlrmal EF with regional wma consistent with known coronary artery disease. No significant valvular disease seen; CT to follow-up effusion showed interval resolution. He was discharged on his home ACEI. CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE The patient had new WMA in area of previous reversible defect on MIBI. The patient and daughter/HCP were that he should not go to cath. He was placed on aspirin and a statin. He will follow-up with his PCP regarding if further workup is warrented. HYPERTENSION The patient was restarted on ACEI for discharge. His norvasc was discontinued edema. If BPs become less control GLAUCOMA The patient continued on his eye medications. RENAL CYST/MASS An incidental renal cyst was caught on Chest CT that had interval enlargement from . The patient had an ultrasound in the hospital which reveled a solid mass suspicious of RCC. The patient was notified and daughter notified, and referred to see urology as an outpatient to discuss options. MENTAL STATUS The patient was oriented to person and place but not with time; his daughter confounded examination with interpreter with mental status examination, but he was not oriented to time and his memory was impaired. He likely has a dementia though per his family he normally is quite sharp. He did not seem to have any acute reversible delirium CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE Renal function was near baseline during this admission, 1.3-1.5. This should be followed as outpatient. HPI: Ms is a year old woman with a history of COPD (uses O2 intermittently), intellectual disability (lives in group home), and CKD who presented with dyspnea and cough, and was admitted for COPD exacerbation. Pt recently hospitalized with COPD exacerbation with fever, tx with azithromycin and prednisone. Pt began to have symptoms until the evening when she developed dyspnea, coughing, wheezing similar to prior COPD flare. She reports intermittently using 3L at home. This am, pt reports ongoing sob, dry cough, but denies CP, palpitations, ongoing abdominal pain, n/v/d/c/melena/brbpr. 10pt ROS: Positive per HPI, otherwise comprehensive ROS negative. Hospital Course: year old woman with a history of COPD(on 3L NC at night), intellectual disability (lives in group home), depression, GERD, CKD stage III lithium, and other issues admitted with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure due to COPD exacerbation. Fever: Shortness of breath: Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: Acute COPD exacerbation: pulmonary edema: Small L pleural effusion: Presented with days of cough, dyspnea, and fever, likely c/w COPD exacerbation in setting of wheezing; potential trigger viral URI. No leukocytosis, and initial CXR without clear infiltrate to suggest PNA (did reveal mild pulmonary edema and small L pleural effusion, both stable from prior on . She does not carry a diagnosis of CHF (last TTE nl EF and no WMA in , and BNP WNL). Low suspicion for PE given cough/wheezing. She was treated with renally dosed levofloxacin x 5d, standing nebulizers, and an extended prednisone 40 mg PO QD ( ) with very slow improvement. Given initial concern for pulmonary edema, she received Lasix 20mg IV on , with no further doses given repeat CXR on without clear edema or PNA. At the time of discharge, she was back to her home O2 requirement and saturating >88% on room air during the daytime. determined her to be at her functional baseline and recommended discharge back to her group home. She is being discharged on a slow prednisone taper (60 mg PO QD x3d, 40 mg PO QD x3d, 20 mg PO QD x3d then stop), with addition of Spiriva to her COPD regimen. She will f/u with her geriatrics team on and with pulmonary on for COPD and evaluation of pulmonary nodules concerning for malignancy as below (earlier appointment requested; patient to be contacted if able to arrange). Ground glass lung nodules concerning for malignancy: Three ground glass pulmonary lesions were noted to be larger on CT scan compared to , concerning for locally invasive adenoCA. Ms. missed her pulmonary f/u appointment while hospitalized. The appointment was rescheduled for (earlier appointment requested; patient to be contacted if able to arrange). Hypernatremia: Initially hypernatremic, likely secondary to inadequate free water intake, improved slightly with on and resolved with 500cc D5W on . free water intake was encouraged. Na at discharge was normal. Thrombocytopenia: Appears chronic and stable from priors (b/l ~100s-150s); now stable without evidence of bleeding. CKD stage III: Cr at baseline, 1.4 at discharge. Nausea: In setting of frequent coughing, now resolved. EKG without clear ischemic changes, and troponin neg x 2. NSVT vs SVT with aberrancy: Brief episode on telemetry AM, with few beats of SVT on . Likely related to nebulizer treatments. Low suspicion for ischemia in absence of chest pain and negative cardiac biomarkers this admission. Remains on tele. HPI: Ms. is a year old female prsenting c/ R buttock abscess and cellulitis which started on when she noticed an itchy hard red lump on her R buttock. She presented to her PCP and was started on doxycycline at that time. On she presented to the ED with worsening pain in the area as well as low grade fevers and chills. In the ED she underwent I&D of the abscess, was given ancef 2 g, and sent home on dicloxacillin. She returned to the ED for wound check on and was found to have increased erythema surrounding the wound site, purulent drainage from the wound, and complaints of low grade fevers to 100.4 and chills at home. She also notes mild headache and mild LLQ soreness. At presentation in the ED vitals were 98.9, 69, 110/69, 16, 96% on RA. BCx were obtained and pt was started on IV vancomycin, given percocet x 2. A wound swab was obtained showing mixed flora, and the wound was repacked with iodoform gauze. Wbc was 6.8, lactate 0.8. Electrolytes showed K 6.4 -> 5.2 without EKG changes. Hospital Course: Ms. is a year old woman who presented with an abscess with surrounding cellulitis. She had failed outpatient treatment with 3 days doxycycline, then 2 days of dicloxacillin following I&D of abscess. She continued to have low grade fevers and chills at home, with expanding cellulitis. She reported to the ED for wound check 2 days following her I&D and was admitted for worsening cellulitis. She was started on vancomycin on . wound care was continued with daily packing of her wound. She continued to have drainage from the wound and the wound nurses were consulted to make further recommendations regarding wound care. Blood cultures were obtained on admission and showed no growth to date at the time of her discharge. wound cultures obtained at the time of her I&D were found to grow MRSA, sensitive to bactrim. Her cellulitis continued to improve on IV vancomycin and on she was transitioned to PO bactrim. She was given percocet prn for dressing changes. She was discharged home to complete a two week course of bactrim, with to help with dressing changes and follow up with Dr. in clinic. She was instructed to return for fevers, chills, or worsening redness, pain, or edema the site of her wound. HPI: patient is a year old female living in a rehabilitation facility who presented to the with epigastric pain, nausea and vomiting. The vomiting was non-bloody and non-bilious. She has a bowel movement about 12 hours ago, which was normal. The CT done at the was concerning for incarcerated gastric volvulus. It was read here as mesenteroaxial gastric volvulus with intrathoracic location of the gastric antrum and pylorus and patient was emergently taken to the operating room. Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to the General surgical Service for evaluation and treatment. After a brief, uneventful stay in the PACU, the patient arrived on the floor NPO, on IV fluids, with a foley catheter, and PCA morphine for pain control. The patient was hemodynamically stable. Neuro: The patient received PCA morphine until POD 6 with good effect and adequate pain control. She was on PCA as she continued to be on sips until the evening of her POD 6. When tolerating oral intake, the patient was transitioned to oral pain medications, in particular percocet, which controlled her pain equally well. CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. However, at the time of admission patient's troponin was increased at 0.20. patient's EKG was not concerning for acute myocardial infarction. Cardiology was consulted. The cardiac enzymed were cycled and trended down to normal values. The transthoracic echocardiogram was done and was consistent with hypertensive heart, otherwise normal. Aspirin will be restarted now, after discharge. Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirrometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. patient has just some atelactasis and minimal pleural effusion seen on chest x-ray, which did not require any treatment. GI/GU/FEN: Post-operatively, the patient was made NPO with IV fluids. Diet was advanced when appropriate, which was well tolerated. patient's intake and output were closely monitored, and IV fluid was adjusted when necessary. Electrolytes were routinely followed, and repleted when necessary. patient's bowel function returned and she had flatus and bowel movement prior to discharge. ID: The patient's white blood count remained normal. She did not experience any fevers. patient's surgical wound was covered until POD2, when the dressing was removed. The incision was clean, dry and intact and remained that way until the time of discharge with staples in place. The sites around the GJ tube and J tube remained clean, dry and intact. Endocrine: The patient's blood sugar was monitored throughout her stay; insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly if needed. Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined routinely; no transfusions were required. Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and venodyne boots were used during this stay; she was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan. Original Text Perturbed Text
HPI: y/o F with hx of DM, CHF secondary to idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy with EF of 10%, HTN, and CKD who complains of worsening weakness and dyspnea over the last days. She had been having a harder time getting up and walking around, and then yesterday afternoon, she was sitting on the couch and had acute worsening of the SOB in which she felt a "tight" chest and some wheezes. She felt that she could not get up off the couch and had a feeling of doom like she might die. Her children called the EMS who on arrival found her to have SBP in 180s, 88% on RA, and tachypneic. She was put on cpap and given 80 mg IV lasix, SLNG and morphine. . Upon arrival to the ED, she was feeling better. SBPs were in the 160s, and she received aspirin and nitropaste. She was chest pain free and able to be weaned from a non-rebreather to 2L NC. . This morning, she reports her breathing is back to normal, although she still feels weak and tired. She denies any recent fevers or chills, no cough, no abdominal pain or nausea or vomitting. No focal areas of weakness. No headaches or changes in her vision. She can usually walk up to 2 flights of steps without difficulties. She is an active woman with three adopted teenage daughters at home. She denies lower extremity edeam, PND, but she does sleep with 3 pillows. Hospital Course: y/o F with hx of DM, CHF, HTN and CKD who presents with several days of weakness and SOB which culminated in acute SOB the day of admission requiring a call to EMS. Was found to be hypertensive with acute diastolic heart failure with flash pulmonary edema. . Pump - last echo in showed EF of 10% and a dilated cardiomyopathy. Did not appear to be ischemic in nature, unclear etiology. Repeat echo while in hospital showed EF 35%. Has appeared not volume overloaded since admission. No SOB or chest pain while she was on the floor. Flash edema was likely due to hypertension and acute on chronic diastolic dysfunction. She had a signficant urine output to the lasix given in the emergency room with a rise in creatine. Gave 500 cc bolus and a follow up creatine check as an outpatient. . Coronaries - no known CAD, will continue ASA 81 and carvediolol and statin, do not know baseline lipids. Ruled out for MI. . Rhythm - NSR throughout hospitalization. . Dyspnea - likely from acute pulmonary edema secondary to actue diastolic heart failure. Responded well to IV lasix but was probably euvolemic to start with and got dehydrated. Received 500 cc bolus before discharge. Pt was anxious to leave and did not want to wait for repeat blood draw to see if creatinine had started to decrease. Was breathing well on discharge and continued to appear euvolemic. . HTN - was hypertensive initally, switched metoprolol to carvedilol and still was hypertensive the first day so increased dose to 12.5 mg bid. will help at home with medicine compliance. . CKD - Cr to 2.7 from 2.4 the day of admission; likely prerenal after large output from IV lasix. Will give 500 cc bolus and have labs rechecked on with Dr. . Patient very anxious to go home. Expect creatinine to decrease slightly with hydration, but have may slightly new baseline around low 2.0s now. . Hyperkalemia - moderately elevated, no EKG changes, likely in setting of acute renal failure. . DM - was moderately hypoglycemia, so decreased insulin to 30 mg bid, will have her continue to check sugars at home and can rediscuss it with Dr. at next visit. She denies lower extremity edeam, PND, but she does sleep with 3 pillows. HPI: Mrs. is a yo F with a history of depression and episodic vertigo who presents with acute onset abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea. The patient reports that she was in her usual state of health until this AM. She went out to eat breakfast in , ate pancakes, bacon and coffee, no one else has gotten sick and ate same meal) and approx 30 min after eating she began to have severe abdominal cramping. The cramping persisted for approx 3 hours and then she had the onset of severe diarrhea with blood and clots. She had 3 episodes of diarrhea and then called EMS. Upon arrival, per report the patient was lightheaded and found to have BP of 80/p. Thus she was brought to the ER. . In the emergency department, initial vitals: 97.6 102/66 72 14 100% 2L Found to have colitis, treated with ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV and metronidazole 500 mg IV x 1. . On arrival the patient is asymptomatic and reports no diarrhea since admission. On further discussion, the patient reports chronic constipation and abdominal cramping. She reports that on occasion she has severe abdominal pain with BM and does on occasion have diaphoresis and lightheadedness with this pain. This occurrs every months and has occurred for years. Additionally, the patient reports that she has had episodic vertigo for months. The patient has the sensation of the room spinning. It occurs rapidly, lasts for a minute and the resolves. She has had treatment for this with antiseizure medications without benefit. . Review of systems: (+) Per HPI Recent oral herpes outbreak that resolved last month. (-) Denies fever, chills, night sweats, recent weight loss or gain. Denies headache, sinus tenderness, rhinorrhea or congestion. Denied cough, shortness of breath. Denied chest pain or tightness, palpitations. No recent change in bladder habits. No dysuria. Denied arthralgias or myalgias. Hospital Course: yo F with history of depression and who presented with abdominal pain and diarrhea and found to have colitis. Colitis: The patient was treated with IVF. Stool samples were obtained and sent for culture. She was treated with Cipro and Flagyl for possible infectious colitis however, inflammatory bowel disease could not be excluded. The patient's symptoms improved overnight and her bloody diarrhea diminished. She was able to tolerate at full oral diet. Given that her symptoms were improving and she was hemodynamically stable, she was discharged with plans to complete a 2 week course of antibiotics. Outpatient GI follow-up was arranged to reassess the patient's symptoms in two weeks. The patient was instructed to seek medical attention sooner if her symptoms worsened. Stool cultures were still pending at the time of discharge. . Elevated creatinine: The patient's creatinine was 1.1 on admission but resolved to 0.9 post hydration. . Dizziness: The patient reported feeling lightheaded on admission but this symptoms resolved. This may have been related to hypovolemia in the setting of her diarrhea. She was asymptomatic and hemodynamically stable at discharge. . Depression: The patient was continued on her home medications. . The patient reports that she was in her usual state of health until this Morning. HPI: patient is a healthy who fell 30 feet while at work and came into Ed as a trauma stat, Unclear loss of consciousness. Remembers events. Brought in by EMS boarded and collared was found to have multiple right sided rib fractures, pulmonary contusion, small liver laceration, C7 TP fracture, nondisplaced anterior column fracture involving superior pubic rami as well as inferior column fracture and R nondisplaced clavicular fracture. The patient was hemodynamically stable. Hospital Course: Pt is a M s/p mechanical fall off a roof while shoveling and struck multiple poles falling down 30 feet, with + LOC. He was found to have rib, sternal, clavicular, pelvic and C7 TVP fractures. He had several small intraabdomianl injuries. The patient was hemodynamically stable and admitted to the for close monitoring and management of polytrauma. Orthopedics were consulted for nondisplaced anterior column fracture involving superior pubic rami as well as inferior column fracture and R nondisplaced clavicular fracture. They felt there was no indication for acute surgical intervention, the patient was weight bearing as tolerated to lower extremities and nonweight bearing to right arm with a sling to be worn for comfort. Pain was a major issue, due to multiple right sided rib fractures. patient with evidence of poor inspiratory effort and splinting with cough. Acute Pain Service was consulted and the patient had an epidural placed and a dilaudid PCA, with better pain control. The patient was transferred out of the ICU and to the floor on HD2. The epidural was removed on HD4 and the patient was converted to oral pain medication with continued good effect. Physical Therapy and Occupational Therapy worked with the patient during his hospitalization, and recommended rehab at time of discharge. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating with assist, voiding, and pain was well controlled. The patient was discharged to rehab. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan. HPI: HPI: G1P0 at with pregnancy c/b GDMA1, h/o of pancreatitis, transferred from for management of acute on chronic pancreatitis. This is Ms. fourth episode in her life of pancreatitis. In this current pregnancy, this is her second episode, last being on . patient reports noting left upper quadrant abdominal pain since midnight. The pain radiated to her left side and back. It was accompanied by nausea , no vomiting . She last ate at 0900 pm on . No recent fevers, chills, cp, sob. She otherwise feels well. Reports rare ctx, no VB, no LOF. +FM. patient presented to . She was given morphine 5mg x2 at 0600, 0900. She received Zofran at 0900 and started on IVF. Her amylase was 207 and lipase 725. AST 10, ALT 7, Alk phos 184. Her BMP was wnl. For her GDMA1, her last FG was 106. Of note, patient was recently admitted for pancreatitis on . She was seen by GI and the decision was made to defer ERCP given that there was no indication given c/f chronic pancreatitis. She had a negative work up for autoimmune pancreatitis. Per GI note, she has a low risk of gallstone pancreatitis (most common etiology in pregnancy) given that she has a pancreatic divisum which inherently protects her. She was discharged to home after she clinically improved and was able to be advanced to a regular diet. She was scheduled for outpatient follow up with GI on . Hospital Course: Ms. is a G1 with hx of chronic pancreatitis who was admitted to the antepartum service on at 38weeks GA for acute on chronic pancreatitis a/w GDMA1. she had a recent antepartum admission for the same issue and had workup including MRCP. Acute on Chronic Pancreatitis On arrival her labs were notable for normal LFTs, elevated alk phos (152), amylase (163), and lipase (347). She was made NPO and given IVF, famotidine, and Zofran prn. She was given tylenol and did not require any opiates for pain control. She was seen by Gastroenterology. Decision was made to defer delivery as this was not considered an acute indication for IOL even given term pregnancy. She was admitted to antepartum service. Her serum lytes were normal. On HD 1 she had signs/symptoms of abruption (see below) prompting immediate induction of labor. She had a vaginal delivery on . After her delivery on PPD 1 she was seen by Gastroenterology who recommended advancing her diet to bland low fat. They did not recommend any further inpatient workup for her chronic pancreatitis. PROM and Placental abruption On evening of HD 1 patient had an episode of bright red vaginal bleeding. On speculum exam she had a 15 cc clot + 2 scopettes blood cleared from vault with continued pooling of red bloody fluid in posterior fornix. PROM was confirmed with +pooling bloody fluid/+nitrazine/+ferning on microscopy. Decision was made for imemdiate induction with pitocin. She was started on PCN for GBS unknown. She underwent vaginal delivery on complicated by increased bleeding Postpartum due to LUS atony. her total EBL was 650cc. She recieved methergine x 1, cytotec, hemabate x1, TXA. Her hct went from 337 preop to 33.8 immediately Postpartum and then 28.4 on PPD 1 ) Postpartum She thus underwent an uncomplicated Postpartum course. Her vaginal bleeding was within normal limits. She tolerated a low-fat diet diet, voided spontaneously without issue, and ambulated independently. HPI: yo female with a history of lymphoma who is admitted for cycle 2 ICE. The patient states she has been feeling well since her last discharge. She denies any fevers, sore throat, shortness of breath, nausea, diarrhea, constipation, rashes, or dysuria. She does continue to have some thrush but states she has only been using the nystatin about two times a day. Of note she was last admitted from for FNA of a mediastinal hypervascular mass. She was also started on nystatin for thrush. Hospital Course: yo female with a history of lymphoma who was admitted for cycle 2 ICE. lymphoma (intermediate between DLBCL and Burkitt's) - Received cycle 2 ICE today. Tolerated well. She had an echo to re-evaluate previously seen pericardial effusion which was unchanged. She received a blood transfusion due to anemia. This was likely from dilution given her baseline hemoglobin is low. It will need to be follow up as an outpatient. She will follow up with her oncologist as an outpatient. Thrush - Continued nystatin. Encouraged patient to actually take QID times a day. Endometrial Cancer - Treatment on hold due to lymphoma treatment. Hypervascular Mass - FNA done last admission was acellular. Will need to follow up as an outpatient about any plans for a further biopsy by surgery which was previously discussed. HPI: s/p motorcycle vs auto accident tonight w/ left humeral head fracture, dislocation. Pt helmeted rider of motorcycle at ~30miles per hour when car crossed into his resulting in a collision. Pt denies any LOC, able to call for help. Found HD stable. He was BIBA to OSH where he was found to have a left humeral head fracture, dislocation. CT head/c-spine unremarkable. Pt denies any numbness/tingling/weakness in his left upper extremity since the accident. He has sharp pain, swelling localized to the left shoulder. No open wounds. Hospital Course: Hospital Course Mr. presented to the on after a MVC. He was evaluated by the orthopaedic surgery service and found to have a left proximal humerus fracture with significant dislocation He was admitted,consented, and prepped for surgery. On he was taken to the operating room and underwent an ORIF of his left proximal humerus fracture. He tolerated the procedure well, was extubated, transferred to the recovery room, and then to the floor. On the floor he was seen by physical therapy to improve his strength and mobility. The rest of his hospital stay was uneventful with his lab data and vital signs within normal limits and his pain controlled. He is being discharged today in stable condition. On he was taken to the operating room and underwent an ORIF of his left proximal humerus fracture. He tolerated the procedure well, was extubated, transferred to the recovery room, and then to the floor. On the Ground he was seen by physical therapy to improve his Muscle power and mobility. The rest of his hospital stay was uneventful with his lab data and vital signs within normal limits and his pain controlled. He is being discharged today in stable condition.

HPI: is a year old man with HIV on HAART and CNS lymphoma on maintenance HD-MTX who is admitted for planned chemotherapy. Patient's most recent HD-MTX was over a year ago on . He has been planning q12 month cycles. Of note, in the interim he was noted to have Kaposi's sarcoma lesion on his penis. Staging scans showed no visceral involvement, and lesion has since resolved. He was seen by Dr. . CT scan was notable for femoral head avascular necrosis and thyroid nodules. He has undergone MRI of pelvis/hip x2 and FNA of thyroid x2 for further evaluation with no specific findings to date He otherwise feels well. He denies fevers or chills. He gets intermittent headaches a few times a month. No visual changes. No recent cold symptoms. No cough, shortness of breath or chest pain. No N/V/D. No dysuria. No new leg pain or swelling. He does note a small pustule behind his right ear and a few scattered over his chest and back. No fevers, no erythema or tenderness, no fluctuant masses. Also notes intermittent right hip pain over the last few months. No other complaints. REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: A complete 10-point review of systems was performed and was negative unless otherwise noted in the HPI. Hospital Course: PRINCIPLE REASON FOR ADMISSION: is a year old man with HIV on HAART and CNS lymphoma on maintenance HD-MTX who is admitted for planned chemotherapy. CNS lymphoma: Most recent imaging shows no evidence of recurrence. Per patient request has been placed on q12 month schedule, although this cycle was delayed due to scheduling and concurrent medical issues. Urine was alkalinzed with IV and po sodium bicarbonate per protocol. He tolerated infusion of 8g/m2 on with some mild pruritis, which has been pretty consistent per patients prior episodes. Leucovorin rescue per protocol and monitored MTX level q24 hours until <0.1. tolerated treatment well without signficant side effects aside from mild transaminitis which persisted on discharge. He should have this monitored to ensure normalization of his LFT's. He will follow up in 12 months for his next HD-MTX with clinic visit with Dr. and repeat MRI. POC: Medial lumen of POC malfuncitoning. Needs stripping. Emailed IV access team to alert them for outpatient management. Dysuria: Developed single episode of dysuria day prior to discharge. UA did have white cells and bacteria. We empirically treated with cipro 500 bid x10 days (through . Urine culture with <10K CFU. He should follow up with his primary care doctor to ensure resolution of his dysuria/pyuria. Folliculitis: Scattered simple folliculitis on admission exam. Could consider mollascum given HIV status, but lesions appear to be more c/w simple folliculitis. No signs of systemic infection or even surrounding cellulitis or deeper skin infection. Stable. HIV: Well controlled, followed at . Continued home Stribild. Kaposi's sarcoma - Single isolated lesion, now resolved prior to admission. No evidence of visceral involvement. CAD: Takes single agent ASA at home. We held ASA in the setting of MTX and substituted plavix instead. We resumed ASA on discharge. We also held his statin. We continued metoprolol. HTN: Continued home metoprolol and lisinopril Thyroid nodules: Followed as outpatient. Cytology negative; final path pending. Plan for outpatient follow up as previously planned GERD Hx of gastric ulcer: Held home pantoprazole in setting of MTX. Substituted ranitidine Billing: >30 minutes spent coordinating and executing this discharge plan TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Complete Cipro on am - Repeat UA after completing antibioitics - needs to set up stripping of his medial lumen of port - in one year with Dr. in clinic and with MRI HPI: is a year old man with HIV on HAART and CNS lymphoma on maintenance HD-MTX who is admitted for planned chemotherapy. HPI: Mr. is a year-old gentleman who underwent a CABGx3 on with Dr. at . He had an uneventful post-op course and was discharged on day 4. He has been followed since for a superficial sternal wound infection and peripheral edema requiring increased doses of lasix. He presented to the emergency department with shortness of breath and chest pain that worsens with coughing. Hospital Course: Upon admission, an CXR was taken of Mr. chest, revealing a large left pleural effusion. A chest tube was placed, which drained 2.5 liters of serosanguinous fluid. He was placed on IV lasix. Vancomycin was started for sternal erythema. Left EVH site was found to be open with purulent drainage. This was cultured. Abx therapy was broadened to include cipro and flagyl. His vein harvest site improved greatly. Pt. has remained stable and is ready for discharge home. He should follow-up in the wound clinic in 1 week. HPI: Mr. is a year-old gentleman who underwent a CABGx3 on with Dr. at . He had an uneventful post-op course and was discharged on day 4. He has been followed since for a superficial sternal wound infection and peripheral edema requiring increased Dosages of lasix. He presented to the emergency department with shortness of breath and chest pain that worsens with coughing. Hospital Course: Upon admission, an CXR was taken of Mr. chest, revealing a large left pleural effusion. A Thoracostomy tube was placed, which drained 2.5 liters of serosanguinous fluid. He was placed on IV lasix. Vancomycin was started for sternal erythema. Left EVH site was found to be open with purulent drainage. This was cultured. Abx therapy was broadened to include cipro and flagyl. His vein harvest site improved greatly. Pt. has remained stable and is ready for discharge home. He should follow-up in the wound clinic in 1 week.

HPI: h/o anaplastic thyroid cancer, s/p total thyroidectomy, chemoradiation (completed , recently discharged after drainage of new L pleural effusion with cytology positive for malignancy, presenting this morning with sudden onset of L shoulder and neck pain. Pain woke her from sleep at 0200. Denies SOB or CP. Denies n/v. Denies radiation to the back. Denies edema/swelling. Pt. previously admitted for L chest discomfort, fever, and dyspnea. CXR showed large L pleural effusion and a CT chest showed mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy concerning for malignancy. Thoracentesis revealed exudative fluid, fluid gram stand and culture were negative. Seen by oncology. At time of discharge pleural fluid cytology was still pending (ultimately positive, see below). She was treated with five days of levofloxacin for possible pneumonia. A chest tube was placed to drain the effusion, which was removed prior to discharge. Scheduled to follow-up with IP this month. She also had several episodes of ectopic atrial tachycardia likely triggered by her underlying lung process. She was started on metoprolol. cytology report from was POSITIVE FOR MALIGNANT CELLS. Scattered highly atypical epithelioid cells, most consistent with metastasis from Patient's known anaplastic carcinoma of thyroid. In the ED, vitals were 99.2 102 138/88 20 96%. On exam, she was mildly tender over L shoulder/neck. RRR. Lungs sounds on L greatly diminished. Labs notable for WBC of 22 and Na of 127. EKG similar to prior, no ST changes. CXR showed L sided white out. Bedside ultrasound shows extensive loculated fluid in L lung field. Pain improved after 5 iv morphine. Given vanc and levo x1 in the ED. Interventional was consulted and drained effusion at bedside. Pleural fluid was sent for chemistry and cytology. WBC 378 RBC ROS per above. Otherwise negative. Hospital Course: Left pleural effusion: Pt. p/w new onset L chest pain in setting of recent hospitalization for new pleural effusion. cytology from the initial pleural effusion tapped during hospitalization in was positive for malignancy consistent with anaplastic thyroid cancer. Her CXR on admission showed total whiteout of L lung, and a L chest tube was placed in ED by IP on . On IP performed an thoracoscopy with lysis of adhesions and biopsy of a pleural mass; biopsy Pathology returned positive for anaplastic thyroid carcinoma. Her chest tube continued to have high output (~1.5 L per day) and she required IVF for one episode of tachycardia to 120s. She was discharged with stable vital signs, and was able to intake sufficient PO fluids daily. Given the concern for hypotension, her home metoprolol was held and not restarted upon discharge. She was scheduled for close outpatient follow-up with her PCP upon discharge. SIRS: Pt. was admitted with WBC of 22, lactate 7.2, tachycardia and hypotension, concerning for SIRS/sepsis in the setting of a new pleural effusion. However she remained afebrile and her leukocytosis did not improve on vancomycin and meropenem x5 days. Intravenous fluid resuscitation improved both her hypotension and lactate. Furthermore her pleural cultures were negative for growth. Given these results, oncology and the primary medicine team felt that she had a leukomoid reaction to new metastatic spread of anaplastic thyroid cancer and not sepsis. Hyponatremia: Presented to the ED with Na 127, compared to 132 at discharge. Serum osm ~264. Urine Na was low. Likely hypovolemic in setting of SIRS, with elevated lactate. Her serum Na improved with fluid resuscitation. Anaplastic thyroid cancer: Cause of pleural effusion given prior cytology and biopsy showing anaplastic cells. Patient was seen by at oncology on prior admission. Atrius oncology was notified that the Patient was in house and will see her as an outpatient and will follow-up with her regarding the results of her biopsy. Transitional issues -needs to follow up with PCP to address whether to restart metoprolol and assess volume status -needs close follow up to ensure she is taking adequate PO fluids due to high pleurex output. Consider outpatient IVF if hypovolemic. -Has pleurex capped to be drained by . Please drain sterily qd and please do not drain more than 1L at one time. See instructions on page 1. -needs further evaluation for staging and treatment of metastatic anaplastic thyroid cancer. Pathology of a pleural mass is positive for anaplastic thyroid. HPI: with PMHx of Crohns s/p colectomy w/end ileostomy, PSC/AIH s/p OLD on cyclosporine, hepatopulmonary syndrome on baseline O2, fibromyalgia, prior removal multiple abdominal organs presenting with ? fevers, volume overload. Recently admitted and found to have volume overload with echo showing hyperdynamic systolic function. Diursed and on discharge was 73 kg. The patient endorses compliance with her diuretic regimen since going home and has been urinating well. Forgot to put on her stockings while walking aroudn and had significant ankle edema, moreso on the left. At that point she used a significant amount Bengay on her ankle and used an electric blanket--this caused a fever to 101 and so she presented to , who sent her to emergency room. In ED initial vitals were 98.8 114 113/62 14 95% 2L. Noted to have WBC 2.3, HgB 6.6, Plt 24 (baseline). INR 1.8. Creatinine 1.2 over baseline 0.9. Ca 7.5, Mg 1.7, Phos 4.2. ALT and AST wnl, AP 121. Tbili 2.3, Alb 2.3. Lactate 1.2. UA negative for infection. UCG negative. CXR showed a left basal opacity is consistent with a small to moderate left layering pleural effusion and adjacent atelectasis. On floor patient denies cough or worsening abdominal pain. Her primary concern was the overuse of Bengay at home as opposed to ongoing volume overload and her admission wt is down from her prior discharge wt. Denies SOB. Hospital Course: with PMHx of Crohns s/p colectomy w/end ileostomy, /AIH s/p OLD on cyclosporine, hepatopulmonary syndrome on baseline O2, fibromyalgia, prior removal multiple abdominal organs presenting with reported fever, found to have anemia, and pleural effusions. ANEMIA, ACUTE BLOOD LOSS ANEMIA ON CHRONIC ANEMIA: On admission was below baseline of around 7, with guaiac + stool. Has been being treated for a crohn's flare, however an EGD performed this admission showed esophageal ulcerations on top of grade 2 varices with ?stigmata of recent bleeding. She was given a total of 3 transfusions of pRBC. Chronic anemia likely multifactorial (chronic disease, marrow suppression from immunosuppressive medication). Had a bone marrow biopsy in which revealed hypocellular marrow. VARICIES: Grade 2, no interventions were attempted given overlying ulcerations. Started on propranolol for ppx, not nadolol due to . SOB/PLEURAL EFFUSIONS: Initially with small effusions, asymptomatic. She reported a fever to 101 prior to admission, but did not have any documented fevers in the ED or on the floor. Not felt to be infected, however repeat CXR performed when she felt increasing SOB revealed that holding her diuretics (for GIB) had resulted in an increased effusion size. Diuretics were cautiously restarted (see below) and she subjectively improved. No fever, cough, pain or other signs/symptoms of infection. SWELLING: Recent weight on recent discharge is 73.7 kg. On admission (48 hours later) she was 71.7kg, and without an O2 requirement. See below ( ) for diuretic management. on CKD: Her on admission was likely due to hypovolemia from reinstitution of her diuretics on top of blood volume loss from her GIB. At a very recent admission, she had had been restarted on her previous regimen of diuretics (lasix PO 80 and spironolactone 50 daily) after a long period of medication noncompliance and severe . When she was re-admitted this stay, diuretics were held and then cautiously restarted and titrated to 20mg PO lasix and 50 spironolactone. Formulation of a medication regimen that did not cause but was sufficient to prevent ongoing accumulation of her pleural effusions was a challenge. prior to discharge her Cr improved to baseline. Urine osms 524 on admission, urine BUN 840 (FE Urea 48.6 indeterminite range). REPORTED FEVER: Likely related heating blanket and/or measurement error. No worsening abdominal pain (has chronic baseline pain around ostomy) or cough. UA negative. CXR with pleural effusion (has known hepatic hydrothorax) but no evidence of infiltrate, and clinically did not appear to have pneumonia/empyema. Fever was documented during this admission or in the ER, and antibiotics were deferred. Suspected complicated UTI: She developed symptoms of UTI with pyuria on day of discharge ( ). She was emperically treated for complicated UTI given her immunosuppression, and discharged with Bactrim for 7 days. Of note, she has had multiple UA's with pyuria and subsequent cultures with no growth in the past. This could be due to an atypical organism (STI) or due to her crohn's flare causing inflammation of the bowel or ureter due to proximity to inflamed bowel. She has a history of enterococcal UTI. She also has a history of fluoroquinolone intolerance. PSC/AIH s/p OLD : On prior admission, cyclosporin levels were checked in-house and ranged from . Home dose cyclosporine was continued with appropriate levels. She has Grade 2 varices on EGD , see above. Propranolol was started for ppx. CROHN'S DISEASE: On admission she was on treatment dose budesonide 9mg daily for a presumed prior Crohn's flare. Although contraindicated given her hepatic function, she is very averse to taking prednisone so this was continued with the caveat that it should be tapered as soon as possible. Inflammatory markers had been checked last admission and elevated. HEPATOPULM SYNDROME: Diagnosis from OSH. Here, pt denies sx of platypnea and was more dyspneic lying down. Given known ASD from prior echo, no repeat bubble study was done on prior admission. Pt with stated home O2 req which she intermittently required in house. FIBROMYALGIA: chronic issue, at baseline, continued home dose narcotics HoTHYROIDISM: Continued Levothyroxine 100mcg daily. CHRONIC PANCYTOPENIA: Longstanding for years. prior BM bx ( ) showed no concrete evidence of MDS. in-house. No transfusions were done on last admission, see above for current anemia discussion. ANXIETY/DEPRESSION: Continued Xanax, Zolpidem PRN, Lexipro 5mg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Patient started on propranolol this admission due to varices - Needs repeat EGD in year for monitoring of varices - Diuretic regimen changed to lasix 20 mg PO daily and spironolactone 50 mg PO daily - Consider outpatient referral to Pulmonary to confirm diagnosis of hepatopulmonary syndrome. She has had a bubble study, which was non-diagnostic due to likely presence of ASD. - Patient diagnosed with UTI on day of discharge, meets criteria for complicated UTI (immunosuppression).
