HPI: Mr. is a man who was admitted to on with cellulitis and worsening renal failure. His history is notable for severe TR, pulm HTN, diastolic HF, mild systolic HF, AF, CAD with ABG, DM, HTN, CKD with cardiorenal syndrome. He had had multiple admissions for fluid overload including ascites requiring paracentesis for volume overload. He was deemed extreme risk for surgical valve replacement, evaluated here for transcatheter valve treatment options including mitraclip to the tricuspid in the IVC. Plan was to medically optimize, however patient admitted to for cellulitis and treatment. Subsequent elevated BUN/Cr and initiation of hemodialysis for volume management via a right tunneled catheter. patient was tolerating large volume HD and UF in preparation for possible TV procedure. Hospital Course: Mr. was transferred on for evaluation of possible TV procedure. Renal was consulted for management and he continued with daily UF or HD and tolerated it well. Diuretics were stopped per renal recommendations. He was also having persistent chronic right hip pain and the pain service was consulted, Dr. , who recommended initially increasing his dose of oxycodone and topical analgesias, with Dilaudid PRN for severe pain and to be used during procedural testing and to tolerate HD. If this didn't work, they would visit injection options. They attempted a bedside injection using ultrasound but were unsuccessful. He should call the pain clinic to schedule a fluoroscopy guided injection. Skin care was also consulted and their wound care recommendations were followed throughout hospitalization. Social work was also consulted for support during his hospital stay. On patient was transferred to the CCU for hypotension. Hypotension: patient presented with persistently low BPs in the since . Heart rates did not correspondingly increased to lower blood pressures. In this setting, was felt to be due to medication effect from atenolol use in renal failure combined with volume removal by ultrafiltration. The patient showed no evidence of cardiogenic shock or fluid overload. He was briefly on dopamine which was subsequently weaned when he was initiated on midodrine for support. He continued hemodialysis per renal team. His atenolol was discontinued. HPI: is a woman, with history of BRAF-mutated metastatic melanoma complicated by hemorrhagic spinal cord metastasis, s/p T11-L2 radiation, who presents to the ER with headache. She was in her usual state of health until 3 days prior to admission when she was experiencing intermittant headaches which were improved with laying down and worse when sitting or standing. They were associated with mild nausea, but on the evening before admission, she experienced severe nausea and vomiting. Dr. asked her to take additional dexamethasone (6mg PO) which she vomited then tried to take another 4mg which she also vomited. She was taken to the ER where she received dexamethasone 10mg IV, morphine 4mg IV, and Zofran 4mg IV. On arrival to the floor, she states that she feels much better and her HA is . She also describes painful parasthesias in her bilateral for 2 weeks which is increasing, before, she had a period of a few weeks where they were insensate. She also describes mild photophobia which is unchanged. Review of Systems: (+) Per HPI (-) She denies fever, chills, night sweats, recent weight loss or gain. She denies blurry vision, diplopia, sinus tenderness, rhinorrhea or congestion. She denies chest pain or tightness, palpitations, lower extremity edema. She denies cough, shortness of breath, or wheezes. She denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain, melena, hematemesis, hematochezia. She denies dysuria, stool or urine incontinence (she has a Foley catheter). She denies arthralgias or myalgias. She denies rashes or skin breakdown. All other systems negative. Hospital Course: is a woman, with history of BRAF-mutated metastatic melanoma complicated by hemorrhagic spinal cord metastasis, s/p T11-L2 radiation, who presents to the ER with worsening melenoma, headache, and cerebral edema. 1. Neurological Issue: Patient came to the hospital with severe headache. After her lumbar puncture and some dexamethasone, the patient's headache resolved and did not return. She had unrelated headache for several hours s/p her second LP, which resolved with some fluids and caffeine. The dexamethasone was then tapered and the headaches did not return. 2. Oncology Issue: Patient received Vemurafenib 960 mg twice a day for her malignant melanoma. 3. Infectious Disease: There was some suspicion on admission of meningitis. Vancomycin and ceftriaxone were started. Soon after, our suspicion diminished and laboratory results indicated non-infectious etionlogy. The antibiotics were discontinued. 4. Gastrointestinal: Nausea and vomiting. patient's symptoms resolved after receiving lumbar puncture and dexamethasone. HPI: year-old man with T2DM, alcoholic cirrhosis, alcoholic cardiomyopathy (last EF in , and sarcoidosis, recent admission ( ) for mild , volume overload, and hyperbilirubinemia, now re-presenting from clinic due to concern for volume overload. Of note, Mr. was recently admitted for hyperbilirubinemia and on outpatient labs. Ultimately this was felt to be due to volume overload (?congestive hepatopathy and cardiorenal respectively) and symptoms improved with 1x diuresis. Prior to this, his most recent admission was in during which he was admitted for a short period of diuresis and improved. Since the patients discharge from the hospital , Patient reports continued abdominal swelling, worsening dyspnea on exertion (has to stop after about 3 minutes while walking to the bus). He also reports intermittent palpitations about daily and chest "tension" which has been ongoing since prior admission. REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: Cardiac review of systems is notable for absence of chest pain, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, syncope. Otherwise notable as above. On further review of systems, denies any prior history of stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. Denies exertional buttock or calf pain. Denies recent fevers, chills or rigors. All of the other review of systems were negative. Hospital Course: Patient is a year-old M with T2DM, alcoholic cirrhosis, alcoholic cardiomyopathy (last EF in , and sarcoidosis, recent admission ( ) for mild , volume overload, and hyperbilirubinemia, now re-presenting from clinic due to concern for volume overload. ACTIVE ISSUES Acute on chronic heart failure with reduced EF: Presented from clinic with clinical signs and symptoms of volume overload. Patient reported not being able to obtain his medications at various times over the past year. Most likely etiology of exacerbation. Diuresed well with 40mg IV Lasix BID. The rest of his HF medications were continued at the same dose. He was previously considered for an ICD due to his severely reduced EF, but did not complete placement for unclear reasons (the Patient reports not receiving a call back from the ICD team after completing a work-up). Discussed with primary cardiologist and attending of record this admission Dr. would ICD as outpatient and uptitrate heart failure medications as needed. Discharge weight was 59.9kg (did have elevated JVP on discharge though unclear how much tricuspid regurgitation was contributing) as lungs were clear and symptomatically felt improved. Paroxymsal atrial fibrillation: Lack of anticoagulation: Unclear history, but documented in chart and does have history of this per primary cardiologist Dr. . Is not on anticoagulation due to inability to be on NOAC cirrhosis, and poor cognitive status and history of missing appointments making warfarin monitoring unsafe. He was kept on heparin gtt while in-house but discharged off anticoagulation. H/o stroke: Statin previously discontinued due to cirrhosis and persistently elevated LFTs. Was not started on admission and was discharged off statin. H/o pulmonary sarcoidosis: Per chart review appears that Patient was previously seeing pulmonary for sarcoidosis and lung nodule follow-up but was lost to follow-up in . No active management in-Patient, but was made appointment after discharge to re-establish. CHRONIC ISSUES: Alcoholic cirrhosis: Transaminitis: No active management during admission. Has follow-up with outpatient hepatology scheduled. TRANSITION ISSUES: []discharged on PO Lasix 40mg BID, consider uptitration []lost to pulmonary follow-up for sarcoid and nodules, should have repeat chest CT in next weeks, and has pulmonary follow-up already scheduled. []consider ICD placement []consider anticoagulation for paroxysmal afib and h/o CVA (though due to cirrhosis and poor compliance with follow-up likely not candidate for DOAC or warfarin) []consider re-starting statin for secondary prevention for stroke if cleared by hepatology (previously on statin, discontinued to cirrhosis) HPI: year-old man with T2DM, alcoholic cirrhosis, alcoholic cardiomyopathy (last EF in , and sarcoidosis, recent admission ( ) for mild , volume overload, and hyperbilirubinemia, now re-presenting from clinic due to concern for volume overload. Of note, Mr. was recently admitted for hyperbilirubinemia and on Outpatient labs. Ultimately this was felt to be due to volume overload (?congestive hepatopathy and cardiorenal respectively) and symptoms Recovered with 1x diuresis. Prior to this, his most recent admission was in during which he was admitted for a short period of diuresis and improved. Since the patients discharge from the hospital , patient reports continued abdominal swelling, worsening dyspnea on exertion (has to stop after about 3 minutes while walking to the bus). He also reports intermittent palpitations about daily and chest "tension" which has been ongoing since prior admission. REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: Cardiac review of systems is notable for absence of chest pain, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, syncope. Otherwise notable as above. On further review of systems, denies any prior history of stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. Denies exertional buttock or calf pain. Denies recent fevers, chills or Shakes. All of the other review of systems were negative. Hospital Course: Patient is a year-old M with T2DM, alcoholic cirrhosis, alcoholic cardiomyopathy (last EF in , and sarcoidosis, recent admission ( ) for mild , volume overload, and hyperbilirubinemia, now re-presenting from Outpatient due to concern for volume overload. ACTIVE ISSUES Acute on chronic heart failure with reduced EF: Presented from clinic with clinical signs and symptoms of volume overload. Individual reported not being able to obtain his medications at various times over the past year. Most likely etiology of exacerbation. Diuresed well with 40mg IV Lasix BID. The rest of his HF medications were continued at the same dose. He was previously considered for an ICD due to his severely reduced EF, but did not complete placement for unclear reasons (the Individual reports not receiving a call back from the ICD team after completing a work-up). Discussed with primary cardiologist and attending of record this admission Dr. would ICD as Outpatient and uptitrate heart failure medications as needed. Discharge weight was 59.9kg (did have elevated JVP on discharge though unclear how much tricuspid regurgitation was contributing) as lungs were clear and symptomatically felt improved. Paroxymsal atrial fibrillation: Lack of anticoagulation: Unclear history, but documented in chart and does have history of this per primary cardiologist Dr. . Is not on anticoagulation due to inability to be on NOAC cirrhosis, and poor cognitive status and history of missing appointments making warfarin monitoring unsafe. He was kept on heparin gtt while in-house but discharged off anticoagulation. H/o stroke: Statin previously discontinued due to cirrhosis and persistently elevated LFTs. Was not started on admission and was discharged off statin. History of pulmonary sarcoidosis: Per chart review appears that patient was previously seeing pulmonary for sarcoidosis and lung nodule follow-up but was lost to follow-up in . No active management in-patient, but was made appointment after discharge to re-establish. CHRONIC ISSUES: Alcoholic cirrhosis: Transaminitis: No active management during admission. Has follow-up with Outpatient hepatology scheduled. TRANSITION ISSUES: []discharged on PO Lasix 40mg BID, consider uptitration []lost to pulmonary follow-up for sarcoid and nodules, should have repeat chest CT in next weeks, and has pulmonary follow-up already scheduled. HPI: with h/o Afib on Xarelto (factor Xa inhibitor) sustained mechanical fall this morning outside his granddaughter's sporting event. He recalls stepping on his left foot with his right foot and falling toward his left side. He did lose consciousness for several minutes until EMS arrived; witnesses did not report any seizure activity or loss of bowel or bladder continence. Upon arrival of EMS he was reportedly GCS 15 and fully neurologically intact. He was taken to on OSH where a CT scan of the head demonstrated a L frontal SAH, and he was transferred to for further evaluation and care. Currently he is complaining of pain in his left forehead and left shoulder, however he remains neurologically intact. ROS is negative except as noted. Hospital Course: Pt was admitted to the Neurosurgery service, floor status for neurological observation. He was given 1 Unit FFP for an INR of 1.7 and all anticoagulants were held. He remained neurologically stable and on he had a repeat Head CT which was stable. The patient was ambulating independently, tolerating a PO diet, voiding and had adequate pain control. He was cleared for discharge home at this time, which he was in agreement with. HPI: This is a woman with bilateral thoracic outlet syndrome. It appears to be neurogenic in nature. She had previously undergone a right first rib resection which resolved her symptoms on the right. However, she continues to have pain in the left upper extremity. Given these findings, the patient was consented for a left first rib resection. given the extent of fibrosis on the right brachial plexus and the inexperience of the resident assistant, Dr an attending first assistant. Hospital Course: was admitted on with RUE pain. She agreed to have an elective surgery. Pre-operatively, she was consented. A CXR, EKG, UA, CBC, Electrolytes, T/S - were obtained, all other preperations were made. It was decided that she would undergo: OPERATION PERFORMED: 1. Left first rib resection. 2. Neurolysis of left brachial plexus. Se was prepped, and brought down to the OR surgery. Intra-operatively, she was closely monitored and remained hemodynamically stable. She tolerated the procedure well without any difficulty or complication. Extubated iin teh OR. Post-operatively, transferred to the PACU for further stabilization and monitoring. Post CXR showed pneumothorax She was then transferred to the VICU for further recovery. While in the VICU she recieved monitered care. When stable she was delined. Her diet was advanced. When she was stabalized from the acute setting of post operative care, she was transfered to floor status. Pain service consult. Weaned from PCA to PO meds. On the floor, she remained hemodynamically stable with her pain controlled. She continues to make steady progress without any incidents. She was discharged home in stable condition. HPI: yo male with recent diagnosis of rectal cancer presents today for evaluation of rectal pain. patient has undergone neoadjuvant treatment and has been doing well. patient was seen in follow-up in Rectal Cancer Clinic and elected to proceed with proctosigmoidectomy, robotic/laparoscopic versus open, with temporary diverting ileostomy. Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to the Colorectal Surgical Service after the operation. After a brief, uneventful stay in the PACU, the patient arrived on the floor NPO, on IV fluids, with a foley catheter, and IV PCA for pain control. The patient was hemodynamically stable. Neuro: The patient received IV PCA with good effect and adequate pain control. When tolerating oral intake, the patient was transitioned to oral pain medications. CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirrometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. GI/GU/FEN: Post-operatively, the patient was made NPO with IV fluids. Diet was advanced when appropriate, which was well tolerated. patient's intake and output were closely monitored, and IV fluid was adjusted when necessary. Electrolytes were routinely followed, and repleted when necessary. ID: The patient's white blood count and fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection. Wound care was performed by the nursing staff.. Hematology: The patient's complete blood count was examined; no transfusions were required. Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and venodyne boots were used during this stay; was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. Diet was advanced as bowel function returned. Foley catheter was removed POD 3 and POD 5 - both times patient failed a voiding trial. Foley was replaced and leg-bag training was given. Urology follow-up was arranged at time of discharge. On POD 5 patient was re-visited by Ostomy team and received additional instructions. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan. HPI: yo G7P4 with known fibroids and menorrhagia s/p tx with provera presents for pre-operative evaluation for scheduled Abd MMY. The patient has a long history of heavy menstrual bleeding. Recently on , the pateint was seen in the ED for heavy bleeding. In the ED she was transfused a total of 2 units of PRBC; has a history of transfusion approximately year ago for the same issue. She reports having regular menses every month, but increasingly heavy flow for the past year, also associated with increased cramping and low back pain. She had tried norethindrone to help manage this, but had minimal improvement. Of note, she was seen and evaluated at earlier this year. EMB was negative in of this year. TSH was also normal earlier this year. She was started on Provera on discharge from the ED, and notes that her bleeding this cycle has largely resolved and she is overall feeling well. She stopped the Provera on in and since that time has had no bleeding. Hospital Course: On , Ms. was admitted after undergoing supracervical hysterectomy for symptomatic uterine fibroid. Please refer to operative report for details of the procedure. Her post-operative course was uncomplicated. She had an episode of dizziness when first getting upon POD1, at which time, her vital signs were stable. Her pre-op HCT was 33, wiht EBL 500cc, and post-op HCT was stable in at 24. By POD2, she was tolerating PO, voiding spontaneously, pain was controlled with oral medications, and ambulating independently without difficulty. She was then discharged home in good condition. HPI: year old female with h/o of pancreatitis (etiology unknown), presumptive dianosis of brucellosis, who is s/p 6 week treatment with doxycyline and rifampin in . She developed night sweats, low back pain, and feeling "crummy" for 5 weeks. She had had similar symptoms since , but they had resolved since treatment and for several weeks afterwards. She also has new chest discomfort and and dysnpea on exertion which began 5 days ago. . She first developed severe abdominal pain , nausea, and pain with eating. She was hospitalzied at for question of recurrent pancreatitis. She had a CT scan which showed mesenteric edema. She was discharged with continued symptoms. In she was hospitalized again at for similar pain for 3 days. For her abdominal pain she underwent an outpatient work up including CT scan which aslo showed mesenereic edema, MRCP, secretin test, Viocase, gastric emptying test. In she changed her diet to a low fat diet, which has helped her symptoms significantly. . Concurrently, in , she developed night sweats, and low back pain, low grade fevers ( ). She also notes chills during the day. She had an MRI of her back which was unremarkable. She underwent an outpatient workup with multiple ID titers, Brucella IgM returned positive in . She was treated with a 6 week course of doxycycline/rifampin. Her GI pain/back pain resolved. She gained some weight with the anitibiotics, despite her diet. . Five weeks ago her symptoms recurred. She also has new back pain between her shoulder blades, in addition to her low back pain. She has taken occassional tylenol for back pain with some relief. Due to concern that Brucella might have returned or for concern over another infectious disease, she was sent to clinic two weeks ago. At that time, it was felt that her diagnosis of Brucellosis may be false given one isolated IgM. She underwent a repeat workup of various infectious etiologies of her symptoms. . Last week, she noted increasing lightheadedness with activity. She Feels her heart racing. Feels like she's been on a treadmill for 45 minutes after minimal exertion which is new over the last five days. These symptoms are better with rest. She has also had chest pain which she has been haivng with activity but yesterday it was constant. CP is substurnal, described as pressure, , non radiating. . She called her ID doc who was concered about this new onset CP and DOE and was referred to the ED. In the ED, vss. She was monitored overnight with plan for AM stress, which was canceled this morning. O2 sats were monitored with ambulation, which dropped to 87% on RA (From 100% while resting). She was admitted for evaluation of CP, DOE, and hypoxia. . ROS: Pt denies blurry vision, eye pain, ear pain, changes in hearing, sore thorat, cough, lymph node swelling, palpitations, vomiting, constipation, diarrhea, blood in stool, blood in urine, change in urinary symptoms, lower extremity edema, joint pain, muscle pain, muscle weakness. Hospital Course: yof with nightsweats, low grade fever, back pain, abdominal pain, new onset chest pain and dyspnea on exertion concerning for possible PE. . Chest pain/shortness of breath: Pt has had pancreatitis of unclear etiology, vague abdominal pain and evidence of meseneteric edema which brings to question a possibility of hypercoagulability. Per Wells Criteria she has a moderate probabiliy of PE, for which she received a CTA despite a negative DDimer. CTA was negative for PE. Given that this was the most acute concerns (in addition to negative cardiac enzymes) and reason for hospitalization, she was discharged plan to continue work up as . She was afebrile, without tachypnea, and tachycardic only with activity. She should follow up with her PCP for more complete work up of her symptoms. . Fever - Pt has very low grade fevers in the setting of normal white count with predominance of Lymphocytes. Broad panel of infectious antigens sent by ID, many still pending. CT scan in suggested early mesenteric panniculitis, which may explain her fever, and abdominal pain. Malignancy was also considered. No antibiotics were started. Blood cultures were obtained to evaluate for underlying bacteremia. CT torso was completed during this hospitalization. It should be followed up by her PCP/ID doctors as . It would not explain why her symptoms improved with treatment with antibiotics. . The following tests should be done as outpatient, per ID recommendation. -Cardiac echo to assess valves; r/o ASD or PFO; r/o atrial myxoma - ACE; ANCA - ESR; CRP - Additional serologies to further evaluate mesenteric panniculitis: - serum crypto Ag - Whipples diseae by PCR - Flow cytometry and immunotyping - EBV PCR - evaluate spine with MRI . Bacteruria - Pt denied urinary symptoms, unlikely to be a true urinary tract infection. Antibiotic treatment was deferred. . HPI: Ms has had progressive left hip pain that has been refractory to conservative management. She elects for definitive treatment. Hospital Course: The patient was admitted to the orthopaedic surgery service and was taken to the operating room for above described procedure. Please see separately dictated operative report for details. The surgery was uncomplicated and the patient tolerated the procedure well. Patient received perioperative IV antibiotics. Otherwise, pain was initially controlled with a PCA followed by a transition to oral pain medications on POD 1. The patient received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis starting on the morning of POD 1. The foley was removed on POD 2 and the patient was voiding independently thereafter. The surgical dressing was changed on POD 2 and the surgical incision was found to be clean and intact without erythema or abnormal drainage. The patient was seen daily by physical therapy. Labs were checked throughout the hospital course and repleted accordingly. At the time of discharge the patient was tolerating a regular diet and feeling well. The patient was afebrile with stable vital signs. The patient's hematocrit was acceptable and pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen. The operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign. The patient's weight-bearing status is weight bearing as tolerated on the operative extremity. Ms is discharged to home with services in stable condition. HPI: Ms has had progressive left hip pain that has been refractory to conservative management. She elects for definitive treatment. HPI: Dr. is a year old male with history of dementia, DM2, hypertension, and anemia who presents with constipation and rectal pain for 3 days. Patient unable to provide history. MOLST indicates DNR/DNI. In the ED, initial VS were 97.9 105 169/77 18 99% RA. Rectal notable for guiaiac positive brown stool. The Patient was unable to tolerate digitial disimpaction due to pain. - Labs: WBC 20.7 (87.7N, 4.3L), H/H 10.8/32.6 (baseline Hct 41), plt 430, Cr 1.4 (baseline 1.2), glucose 351, lactate 1.5. VBG 7.40/39. UA with 1000 glucose. - CT ABD/PELVIS: Large stool burden, 6mm calculus within the bladder, multiple chronic findings. - Patient was given pantoprazole 40mg IV, morphine 2mg IV, and 1L NS. Attempt was made to disimpact overnight, however Patient was unable to tolerate it. This morning the Patient is still sleepy however he wakes up, says he has been tired for a while. Knows he is in the hospital for constipation. Able to say his first name, his two sons names, his specialty and years he practiced for. Having some abdominal discomfort. This son is present at the bedside and states the Patient has been having decline over the past 6 weeks. He has been eating less. He hasn't been sick at home. No respiratory or urinary symptoms. His fall was a month ago but has not had a decline since then. The decline was prior. He had some knee pain that has improved with steroid injection. He started reporting rectal pain yesterday and his last BM was 2 days prior to admission. He has no history of constipation prior. REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: All other 10-system review negative in detail. HPI: The patient is an woman with low grade non-Hodgkin's lymphoma complicated by aplastic anemia following treatment fludarabine, followed by Dr. . She was admitted from the Emergency Department today with a neutropenic fever. The patient reports developing a dry cough approximately three days ago. Because of the cough, her planned admission for ATG/cyclosporine for her aplastic anemia was delayed, and a CXR was performed which showed no evidence of pulmonary infection. She felt feverish today and took her temperature, which was 100.4. After calling , she was advised to seek evaluation in the Emergency Department. In addition to her cough and feverishness, she has experienced rhinorrhea over the past several days. She has had no dyspnea, skin rash, or joint pain and denies recent bleeding and bruising. She has recently been in close contact with her daughter, who has an upper respiratory infection. In the Emergency Department, she was noted to be febrile, with T 104.1 degrees. Her WBC was 1.1 and ANC 792. Blood cultures, a urinalysis, and a chest xray were obtained, revealing no clear source of infection. She was empirically started on cefepime 2g IV and was admitted for further management. Hospital Course: . Bronchitis: patient admited with neutropenic fever. She received neupogen and was started on cefepime. Azythromicin was added for concern for atypical bacteria with CAP. CXR was unremarkable, but CT scan was suggestive of bronchitis. Cefepime was stopped in day 2, when patient's ANC >1000, and pt completed a 5-day course of Azythromycin. patient's cough improved. patient did not require oxygen and is being discharged breathing comfortably in room air. . . Aplastic anemia: patient with HCT <25, will get blood. do ATG/CSA when patient recovers from surgery. Pt's HCT was followed in the hospital requiring multiple transfussions (6 RBC). patient's platelets are in low range range during hosptialization and required 2 units in the days. . . Abdominal obstruction: Pt with abdominal distention and decreased bowel sounds and CT scan showed complete obstruction of the descending colon, close to prior surgery site. Was taken to the OR in . Perioperatively, she received both packed red blood cells and platelets. Following an uncomplicated exploratory laparotomy,lysis of adhesions, and transverse loop colostomy, the patient was admitted to the surgical ICU for further management. She was transferred to the floor on . Primary care was transferred to the service on . patient will need close follow up and work up for recurrent colonic cancer. . . HPI: This is a year-old Male with a PMH significant for CAD (s/p stenting of ramus, , V.fib arrest s/p defibrillation with AICD placement, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, GERD, allergic rhinitis and asthma. . In , the Patient presented to pulmonology with chronic cough complaints which initially improved with decongestants and anti-histamines. He was noted to have abnormal CXR findings. In , Dr. RUL infiltrate with mass/effusion with eosinophillia (IgE 279) on Chest CT, and positive RAST for molds/aspergillus. Repeat CXR 4 weeks later showed interval worsening of RUL process; pt's coughing and allergic sx persisted. CXR demonstrated progression to RUL collapse with no interval w/o interval improvement --> suspected allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA). the Patient had CT chest findings of a right upper lobe infiltrate with mass or effusion, in the setting of eosinophilia (IgE 270) and a positive RAST for molds/aspergillus. A repeat CXR 4-weeks later demonstrated interval worsening of the RUL process and his allergic symptoms and coughing persisted. CXR in demonstrated progression to RUL collapse with no interval improvement and allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis was suspected. His WBC was normal, but with a peripheral eosinophilia of 30% (IgE 880). He was prescribed Prendisone 20 mg PO BID in with these findings. Apergillus galactomannan levels were 0.1 in . Steroids were deferred until bronchoscopy could be performed. . On , the Patient underwent bronchoscopy which was notable for an endobronchial lesion obstructing the right upper lobe resulting in right upper lobe collapse. The final bronch/BAL microbiology and washings demonstrated Aspergillus (non fumigatus, flavus, spp.) and Moraxella co-infection. The results were reportedly negative for underlying malignancy. Shortly following the bronchoscopy, pt began to develop low-grade fevers, at which time Dr. Itraconazole and cefuroxime ( ) for possible invasive aspergillus infection vs. aspergillosis of the RUL in conjuction with a possible post-obstructive process, either related to a malignancy or ABPA. Was then scheduled a for repeat bronchoscopy with IP(Dr. as endobronchial lesion observed on initial bronch was concerning for malignancy and needed further testing. Dr. recommended course of cefuroxime and then changed to Augmentin for possible post-obstructive process. He started itraconazole and cefuroxime at the same time, so it is not clear which of the agents are responsible. However, itraconazole can be associated with increased blood glucose levels, and furthermore is probably less immediately necessary since the larger concern would be for a bacterial postobstructive. As such, he should resume antibiotics stepwise, starting cefuroxime. He should also stop taking Tylenol around the clock so that we can have an idea of his fever curve. Should his fevers persist, he may need more aggressive antibiotics including with anaerobic coverage. Today pt came in for rigid and flexible bronchoscopy due to suspected endobronchial aspergillus infection leading to RUL endobronchial lesion. Bronch showed RUL endobronchial obstruction likely due to aspergillus conglomerate, and found pus coming out of the upper lobe. Cryo therapy and opened the upper lobe at least partially but more mucus plugging seen distally and CXR showed remaining opacity suggestive of post-obstructive pneumonia. In addition there was small amt bleeding during procedure which put Patient at risk for translocation of bacteria. IP requested admission to floor for IV antibiotics for post-obstructive pneumonia and possibly Amphotericin or other "stronger" antifungal but do NOT start ampho without consulting ID first. Hospital Course: 1. ABPA vs. aspergilloma (fungus ball) with post-obstructive PNA Patient was admitted to medicine for observation and IV antibiotics/antifungals after receiving diagnostic/therapeutic bronchoscopy with IP (Drs. and . During procedure IP found a RUL endobronchial obstruction likely due to aspergillus conglomerate and with pus coming out of the RUL distal to obstruction. Cryotherapy was used to open RUL partially. There was mild bleeding during procedure, raising concern for bacterial/fungal translocation and resultant sepsis. Also potential concern for underlying neoplasia/immunocompromise as there was question of invasive aspergillosis on bronchoscopy. Patient was thus admitted to floor for IV antimicrobials. Treated overnight with Itraconazole and empiric Vanco/Cefipime IV for broad GP/GN/pseudomonal coverage. He remained afebrile and asymptomatic overnight and WBC count was normal in AM. Per ID and pulm recs, he was discharged on Itraconazole nd Augmentin 7 day course, with instructions to attend clinic f/u with Dr. clinic f/u with Dr. , and to f/u with PCP . Studies pending on discharge: BAL pathology/cytology/cultures. 3.IDDM: stable on home regimen of fixed-dose insulin, with sliding scale added here to cover meals. Discharged on home meds (Levemir). 4. GERD: stable on home Omeprazole 5. CAD s/p stenting of ramus ( ): stable on home ASA. 6. VFib arrest s/p defibrillation with AICD placement: not active issue 7. Hyperlipidemia: home statin (Crestor) currently being held due to treatment with Itraconizole. Pt instructed to f/u with PCP to restart statin once finished with course of antifungals. 8. HTN: stable on metoprolol tartrate here; discharged on home metoprolol succinate 9. Allergic rhinitis: stable on home regimen of fexofenadrine-pseudoephedryne 10. Asthma: stable, not on inhalers at home. No issues during hospitalization. HPI: year old male with history of HTN, recurrent DVT and PEs with poor medication compliance s/p negative hypercoag workup, recent admission for right leg superficial femoral vein thrombus on warfarin chronicly who presents with increased RLE pain, and worsening edema x 1 week. Patient denies fevers, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations. Reports he has been taking medications, including warfarin and Lasix. Though when asked in detail, he says he takes all of his medications BID and when challenged, changes his dosing to whatever the interview says. In the ED, initial vitals: 97 69 141/73 18 96%. Labs significant for normal CBC, Chem 10. INR 2.4 therapeutic. LENIs with extension of his superfical venous thrombus. Vitals prior to transfer: 97.9 64 103/64 16 99% RA Currently, patient complains of mild pain and edema in bilateral lower extremities. Calls them "tree trunks." Otherwise feels well and has no complaints. ROS: No fevers, chills. No cough, no shortness of breath, no dyspnea on exertion. No chest pain or palpitations. No abdominal pain. No nausea or vomiting. HPI: Mr. is a year old M w/ hx of HLD, gout, and hypertensive urgency who is transferred from for STEMI. He was seen at Urgent Care today for a few days of intermittent chest discomfort and shortness of breath. An EKG and TTE were ordered. EKG showed findings concerning for STEMI and he was instructed to come to the ED. TTE was done but not yet read. CXR showed no acute abnormality. He went to , where EKG showed ST elevation in inferior leads and Q waves and troponin was reportedly 820. He was given a 5000 unit bolus of heparin, nitroglycerin pasta, , and morphine and transferred for cath. In the ED: Initial vitals were: 100.4 88 117/75 17 97% 2L NC EKG: Sinus rhythm. ST segment elevation in II/III; Q waves in III, aVF; ST depressions in aVL Labs/studies notable for: trop 0.76 , lactate 1.2 Patient was given: Heparin gtt Due to persistent chest pain and dynamic EKG changes, he was taken to the cath lab urgently. Cardiac catheterization showed thrombotic occlusion in his RCA without evidence of other cardiac disease. Balloon angioplasty was completed with TIMI 3 flow distally. No stent was placed. Access was R radial and TR band was placed. He was given Ticagrelor 180 mg 81 mg DAILY, a Tirofiban gtt, and heparin gtt. On the floor, he reports resolution of his chest pain. He is very overwhelmed, as he was previously healthy before all of this. Denies any CP, SOB, DOE, PND, . HPI: HISTORY OF THE PRESENTING ILLNESS: Ms. is a woman with a past medical history of Grade I meningioma s/p resection ( ) complicated by generalized seizures, post operatively complicated by resp failure secondary to airway edema, HTN, HLD, DMII, who presented with acute onset vertigo. She reports that she was feeling well up until yesterday morning. She was sitting at her computer when she suddenly had acute onset of dizziness. She reports that she felt as if the room was spinning. She stool up and immediately felt nauseous and lightheaded. She was able to walk to her living room, but then the nausea increased and she had two episodes of bilious emesis. Associated with these symptoms was epigastric abdominal pain. She reports chronic epigastric pain attributed to anxiety, but today her pain became worse. It was gnawing pain that did not radiate and improved with food. Her pain usually lasts minutes but today lasted all day. Her vertigo persisted for hours but then eventually resolved in the ED. She was however unable to stand without assistance. She does not use alcohol. She is a non-smoker. She denies any recent URI symptoms or changes in hearing, although does report some nasal congestion and sinus pain. She has a history of allergies. No chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea. No blood in her stool. She denies fever or sick contacts. She does report multiple episodes of formed stool on but denies any overt diarrhea. In the ED, initial vitals were: 97.2 72 181/68 16 98% RA. Labs were notable for normal CBC, chem panel, LFTs, troponin < 0.01, UA negative for infection, lactate mildly elevated at 2.3. CT head revealed stable left parietal encephalomalacia with no acute intracranial abnormality. CT abdomen was negative for any acute intra-abdominal process. She was given 1L NS, 4mg IV Zofran, 2mg PO Ativan, 20mg famotidine, 50mg HCTZ, 100mg losartan, and 100mg sertraline. She was seen by neurology, who felt that likelihood of posterior CVA was low given her lack of focal neurologic deficits, vertigo, or cerebellar signs. She was noted to have a gait abnormality, at baseline. Orthostatics were negative. They recommended workup for underlying medical illness, with consideration of MRI if the above unrevealing. On the floor, initial vitals were 97.4 112/58 72 18 98% RA. She reported that the vertigo had resolved but was complaining of a persistent dull headache that she associates with her sinus pain. Hospital Course: Ms. is a woman with a past medical history of Grade I meningioma s/p resection ( ) complicated by generalized seizures, post operatively complicated by resp failure secondary to airway edema, HTN, HLD, DMII, who presented with acute onset vertigo. Vertigo: Patient presented with sudden onset of dizziness, gait instability, nausea/vomiting while sitting at the computer. Symptoms continued and worsened over the course of the day, last for hours. She had previous episodes to this but none this severe. There was no association with positional change. There was no tinnitus or hearing change. Patient denied seizure/post-ictal state. In ED, orthostatics were negative and EKG was without ischemic change. Troponins were negative x2. CT Head was negative for acute process. Her symptoms had completely resolved by the morning. She did have risk factors for vascular disease and an MRI/MRA head/neck which was negative. Patient denies recent viral illness and quick resolution made vestibular neuritis unlikely. Patient had no nystagmus on exam, negative , and prolonged duration inconsistent with BPPV. She was discharged with empiric meclizine and suggestion for vestibular . Dyspepsia: Patient presented with nausea consistent with vertigo. Also had gnawing abdominal epigastric pain that improved with food. CT w/o contrast without evidence of acute abnormality. Patient was continued on famotidine but may benefit from outpatient EGD. Seizure disorder: - continued lamotrigine 150mg BID HTN: - continued losartan, HCTZ HLD: - continued simvastatin DM: - held metformin in hospital, restarted on discharge - ISS while hospitalized TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Unclear etiology of vertigo, but most likely peripheral cause. She will benefit from continued monitoring and counseling. Very unlikely to be central vertigo. - Prelim MRI/MRA of head/neck without evidence of vascular disease concerning for TIA. Final MRI read pending at time of discharge - New medication: Meclizine 25mg TID PRN: VERTIGO - Consider EGD as outpatient given dyspepsia despite H2 blocker treatment. also benefit from switch to PPI CODE: Full (confirmed) CONTACT: Name of health care proxy: Relationship: son Phone number: >30 minutes spent on coordination of care and counseling on day of discharge HPI: HISTORY OF THE PRESENTING ILLNESS: Ms. is a woman with a past medical history of Grade I meningioma s/p resection ( ) complicated by generalized seizures, post operatively complicated by resp failure secondary to airway edema, HTN, HLD, DMII, who presented with acute onset vertigo. She reports that she was feeling well up until The day before morning. She was sitting at her computer when she suddenly had acute onset of dizziness. She reports that she felt as if the room was spinning. She stool up and immediately felt nauseous and lightheaded. She was able to Ambulate to her living room, but then the nausea increased and she had two Vomiting Spells of bilious emesis. Associated with these symptoms was epigastric abdominal pain. She reports chronic epigastric pain attributed to anxiety, but today her pain became worse. It was gnawing pain that did not radiate and improved with food. Her pain usually lasts minutes but today lasted all day. Her vertigo persisted for hours but then eventually resolved in the ED. She was however unable to stand without assistance. She does not use alcohol. She is a non-smoker. She denies any recent URI symptoms or changes in hearing, although does report some nasal congestion and sinus pain. She has a history of allergies. No chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea. No blood in her stool. She denies fever or sick contacts. She does report multiple episodes of formed stool on but denies any overt diarrhea. In the ED, initial vitals were: 97.2 72 181/68 16 98% RA. Labs were notable for normal CBC, Blood work, LFTs, troponin < 0.01, UA negative for infection, lactate mildly elevated at 2.3. CT head revealed stable left parietal encephalomalacia with no acute intracranial abnormality. CT abdomen was negative for any acute intra-abdominal process. She was given 1L NS, 4mg IV Zofran, 2mg PO Ativan, 20mg famotidine, 50mg HCTZ, 100mg losartan, and 100mg sertraline. She was seen by neurology, who felt that likelihood of posterior CVA was low given her lack of focal neurologic deficits, vertigo, or cerebellar signs. She was noted to have a gait abnormality, at baseline. Orthostatics were negative. They recommended workup for underlying medical illness, with consideration of MRI if the above unrevealing. On the floor, initial vitals were 97.4 112/58 72 18 98% RA. She reported that the vertigo had resolved but was complaining of a persistent dull headache that she associates with her sinus pain. Hospital Course: Ms. is a Female with a past medical history of Grade I meningioma s/p resection ( ) complicated by generalized seizures, post operatively complicated by resp failure secondary to airway edema, HTN, HLD, DMII, who presented with acute onset vertigo. Vertigo: Patient presented with sudden onset of dizziness, gait instability, nausea/vomiting while sitting at the computer. Symptoms continued and worsened over the course of the day, last for hours. She had previous episodes to this but none this severe. There was no association with positional change. There was no tinnitus or hearing change. Patient denied seizure/post-ictal state. In ED, orthostatics were negative and EKG was without ischemic change. Troponins were negative x2. CT Head was negative for acute process. Her symptoms had completely resolved by the morning. She did have risk factors for vascular disease and an Cranial MRA which was negative. Patient denies recent viral illness and quick resolution made vestibular neuritis unlikely. Patient had no nystagmus on exam, negative , and prolonged duration inconsistent with BPPV. She was discharged with empiric meclizine and suggestion for vestibular . Dyspepsia: Patient presented with nausea consistent with vertigo. Also had gnawing abdominal epigastric pain that improved with food. CT w/o contrast without evidence of acute abnormality. Patient was continued on famotidine but may benefit from outpatient EGD. Seizure disorder: - continued lamotrigine 150mg BID HTN: - continued losartan, HCTZ HLD: - continued simvastatin Diabetes: - held metformin in hospital, restarted on discharge - ISS while hospitalized TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Unclear etiology of vertigo, but most likely peripheral cause. She will benefit from continued monitoring and counseling. Very unlikely to be central vertigo. - Prelim MRI/MRA of head/neck without evidence of vascular disease concerning for TIA. HPI: with a PMH of alcohol abuse (last drink , alcoholic cirrhosis (complicated by splenomegaly, ascites, esophageal varices), CAD s/p stents approximately 7 months ago, HTN, HLD, and NSAID-induced gastritis presents for evalaution of bloating, nausea, and vomiting. Patient states he has a long history of alcohol use (drinks 1 pint of peppermint schaps daily) he quit drinking years ago after finding out he had cirrhosis. However, over the past three months he has been under a significant amount of stress, including the death of his mother. Under these circumstances, he began drinking again. Last drink was yesterday am. Over the same time period he has begun to feel proressively more bloated. He denies any change in stool pattern, blood in stool but does note nausea as below. He denies past symptoms of withdrawl inclucing anxiety, trremors, or palpitations or seizures. Additionally, Patient reports that he has been having nausea for almost year, which he attributes to the "heart medications" which he began taking after he had two stent placed in his coronary arteries 7 months ago. He states that he vomits (non bloody) every other day, and sometimes doesn't take his pills so that he won't vomit in certain occassions such as when he goes on dates with his girlfriend. He denies experiencing chest pain but does endorse shortness of breath and dizziness, especially when initiating activity quickly such as getting up from a chair to answer the doorbell. He can walk approximately 30 feet before becoming short of breath. No orthopnea or PND. Symptoms were previously attributed to his medication especially his propanolol. He did try breaking the dose into BID but this was not helpful. Of note the Patient had both and EGD in notble for normal and mild antral gastritis for which he was started on a PPI. ROS: No fevers, chills, night sweats, or weight changes. No changes in vision or hearing, no changes in balance. No cough. No chest pain or palpitations. No nausea or vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation. No dysuria or hematuria. No hematochezia, no melena. No numbness or weakness, no focal deficits. In the ED, initial vitals: T 97.8 HR 116 BP 144/82 RR 22 98% RA Patient was given thiamine, folic acid, multivitamins, 2 g IV magnesium, and IVF in the ED. KUB was notable for changes consistent with cirrhosis. RUQ US showed no ascites and a cirrotic liver, CT abdomen pelvis showed no acute process. EKG was unchanged from prior. CXR showed no acute process. Liver was consulted and did not feel patients symptoms were related to cirrhosis. Patient was admitted to medicine for further management. Currently, Patient is comfortable in bed. Denies CP, SOB, and abdominal pain. Complains only of abdominal distension and hyperactive bowels after drinking the contrast for the CT. Of note, Patient states that he has not taken any of his medications today. Hospital Course: with a PMH of alcohol abuse (last drink , alcoholic cirrhosis (complicated by splenomegaly, ascites, esophageal varices), CAD, HTN, HLD, and NSAID-induced gastritis presents for evalaution of bloating, nausea, and vomiting. Abdominal distension. Benign abdominal exam, no guarding or rebound tenderness to suggest acute abdomen. No ascites on ultrasound or physical exam. KUB showed prominence of the small and large bowel loops which may be secondary to an early obstruction however CT abdomen was negative for obstruction. Patient also had regular bowel movements. Pain resolved at the time of discharge. Nausea/vomiting. Patient attributes to taking pills. Sometimes does not take his pills. This is dangerous since he should be taking plavix daily in light of his recent stent placement 8 months ago. Nausea may be due to propanolol or plavix. Would conider switching beta blocker on an outpatient basis. Until then, will take zofran prior to meds to see if nausea alleviated. Patient to follow-up with PCP, outpatient cardiologist, and outpatient hepatologist. Abnormal liver enzymes. AST and ALT elevated in a pattern consistent with alcoholic liver injury. Alkaline phosphatase elevated, GGT elevated indicating alk phos elevation due to billiary as opposed to bone pathology. Liver ultrasound showed nodular cirrhotic appearing liver without focal lesion. AST and ALT improved during his stay. Elevated lactate. Suspect some level of dehydration. Additionally Patient likely has poor clearance in the setting of liver failure. No signs of infection. Lactate improved during his stay. Anemia. Borderline microcytic anemia. Hct 36.3 better than Hct 29.2. Given history of gastritis and esophageal varices, must consider the possibility of chronic GI bleed causing iron deficiency anemia. Patient to follow up with PCP. Alcoholic cirrhosis. History of ascites, esophageal varices, and encephalopathy. No evidence of ascites on imaging performed . No signs of decompensation currently. Patient to follow up with outpatient hepatologist. Alcohol abuse. Has been drinking 1 pint peppermin schnaps for the past three months. No signs/symptoms of withdrawal noted during his stay. CAD. S/p stent placement 8 months ago. Negative serum troponin and CKMB during this visit. No EKG changes suggestive of ischemia to suggest ACS. SOB at baseline and improved since cath. Continued ASA, plavix, pravastatin. Patient will follow up with outpatient cardiologist. Dyspnea/Dizziness- possibly due to stable cardiac disease and actually improved from pre-cath. HTN. Continued propanolol. HLD. Continue pravastatin. Depression. Continue escitalopram. HPI: year old F s/p recent roux en y gastric bypass complicated by bile leak requiring ERCP and stenting on . Pt had repeat ERCP on with removal of the plastic stent in the CBD and has completed 5 day course of augmentin post procedure. Pt with JP drain still in place. Foley catheter left in place at gastrostomy site to keep tract patent in case of repeat ERCP. Pt presents today with suprapubic abdominal pain of two days. Reports dysuria yesterday. No fevers or chills no flank pain. Pt reports increased JP drainage since stent pull on . Typically cc/s increased from < 10 cc's prior to the procedure. Appearance of fluid was serous and serosanguinous. In the past day she had a total of 120 cc of drainage, so given her pain and increased drainage her recommended she come to the ED for evaluation. In the ED, pt afebrile. No leukocytosis. LFT's unremarkable. CT abdomen shows no fluid collection or abscess. Pt admitted for observation. ROS: currently menstruating, otherwise negative except as above Hospital Course: Assessment/Plan: y.o female s/p gastric bypass, CCY, c/b bile leak, recent ERCP, hypothyroidism who presented with suprapubic abdominal pain . Suprapubic abdominal pain initally concerning for UTI- pt with JP drain, recent bile leak and gastrostomy tube placement. Afebrile, LFTs WNL, CT without acute process. Gtube study without leak. Pt reported dysuria x1 prior to admission. UCX with mixed genital flora. She did not have any further symptoms of UTI so her empiric abx (bactrim) were discontinued. She was passing flatus and tolerating good PO without any n/v. She did have intermittent reports of constipation prior to admission but imaging did not show stool burden and abdomen was soft. Her pain improved/resolved during admission. . s/p recent CCY with bile leak-s/p JP drain and gastrostomy with foley catheter and recent ERCP with stenting. No fever, LFTs normal, CT without acute process. G tube study showed no leak. G tube removed by ERCP Team the morning of discharge. Plan to cover with gauze and f/u with outpt surgeon for ongoing care. If leakage persists pt to call her primary surgeon to discuss closure. JP still in place at time of discharge. will be following up with her primary surgeon for ongoing care. . hypothyroidism-continued synthroid . DVT PPx: hep SC TID . Transitional care 1.Pt to f/u with her primary surgeon for ongoing care, to discuss JP drain removal and to monitor gastrostomy site. See appointment below. HPI: y/o female w/known 5mm left proximal ureteral stone s/p ureteral stent placement who presents with flank pain. She c/o nausea. Of note, she was initially scheduled for ureteroscopy with left laser lithotripsy . She presented to ED with LEFT flank pain, nausea. U/S shows no hydronephrosis and KUB shows the stent in correct position. Hospital Course: y/o female w/known 5mm left proximal ureteral stone s/p ureteral stent placement who presents with flank pain. She c/o nausea. Of note, she was initially scheduled for ureteroscopy with left laser lithotripsy . She presented to ED with LEFT flank pain, nausea. U/S shows no hydronephrosis and KUB shows the stent in correct position. Ms. was admitted to Dr. service from the ED for overnight observation, pain control, and IV fluids. She was voiding without difficulty, and pain was less severe upon arrival to the floor. She was tolerating a regular diet. On HD2 she went to the OR late in the day and underwent Left ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy, left retrograde pyelogram, left ureteral stent placement. There were no adverse intra-operative events and she was taken to the PACU after the procedure and then sent back to the general surgical floor. Please see the detailed operative note. On POD1 she denied pain and was voiding without difficulty, tolerating a regular diet and endorsing good pain control with oral analgesics. Her indwelling left ureteral stent was removed at bedside by PA with nurse via the protruding string and gentle traction. At discharge, Ms. pain was well controlled with oral pain medications, she was tolerating regular diet, ambulating without assistance, and voiding without difficulty. She is given explicit instructions to call Dr. follow-up. HPI: y/o female w/known 5mm left proximal ureteral stone s/p ureteral stent placement who presents with flank pain. She c/o nausea. HPI: yo M c HCV, EtOH cirrhosis, sarcoidosis, h/o polysubstance abuse p/w AMS. Patient had been in EtOH detoxification program until 2 days PTA, at which time he was discharged home. The patient has had longtime struggles with insomnia and has used alcohol and/or BZDs in the past to help him sleep. After discharge from detox, his psychiatric meds were changed to Seroquel and Doxepin. He began to use the Seroquel (25 mg hs) to help him sleep. Per the patient's mother, the patient has been altered and had unsteady gait since returning home. The patient acknowledges that he has been unsteady on his feet and has had slurred speech in the mornings after waking up since starting the Seroquel at night, but does not feel that he has been confused. Patient has also noted increased abdominal swelling with a corresponding weight gain of 35 lbs. in the last mos. The patient also states that he had one episode of mild hemoptysis at home, characterized as a little bit of blood in phlegm. He states that this has happened to him before; he feels that it is associated with frequent coughing and his known pulmonary disease. Patient presented to pulmonary clinic today for follow-up and was documented as altered with elevated Cr and hypercalcemia, and was sent to the ED for further evaluation. Of note, the patient was precribed spironolactone but has not been taking this medication since . . In the ED, the patient's VS were T 96.6, P 76, BP 117/67, R 18, O2 95% on RA. He was found to have a distended abdomen and isolated rales at his RLL. CXR and RUQ U/S were performed, which were unchanged compared to prior. His Cr was noted to be 1.9, which is increased from his baseline. He was AAOx2 on initial presentation and had mild asterexis. A diagnostic para was attempted, but a suitable fluid pocket was not visualized. He was given one dose of lactulose and had a large bowel movement, after which he was AAO x3. VS at the time of transfer were 97.9, 72, 113/54, 18, 94% on RA (went down to 90%). . On the floor, the patient is stable without any acute complaints, other than chronic neuropathic pain in his hands bilaterally. . ROS: Denies fever, chills, night sweats, vision changes, rhinorrhea, congestion, sore throat, shortness of breath, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, BRBPR, melena, hematochezia, dysuria, hematuria. Endorses stable DOE. Hospital Course: AMS - Etiology likely Seroquel administration vs. excessive gabapentin serum levels in setting of renal failure vs. hepatic encephalopathy given improvement in alertness, orientation later into the day, and after a dose of lactulose. DDx would include hypercalcemia, toxicity (BZD, TCA levels positive on serum screen), psychotropic medications (Seroquel, Doxepin), or infection. EtOH level negative on serum tox screen. Low suspicion for infection given lack of fevers, no elevation in WBC. CXR stable. Patient continued to receive lactulose, renally dosed gabapentin, received aggressive IVF for hypercalcemia, and was not given Seroquel. The patient had improved alertness during hospital stay and no further episodes of slurred speech or difficulty walking. - The patient will likely need a new sleep aid to try instead of Seroquel. Trazodone was used in-house with some success but this should be further discussed as an outpatient. - Seroquel was discontinued on discharge. - Gabapentin was decreased on discharge and should be further titrated as an outpatient. . HCV / EtOH cirrhosis - MELD 18 on admission. Known varices on EGD in but no e/o bleeding at this time. Hct 39.1 on admission, although this may represent a hemoconcentrated value. No suitable pocket of ascites appropriate for paracentesis identified on U/S in ED. SBP was therefore thought to be an improbable etiology. - Continued lactulose to BM/day. . ARF - Cr 1.9 on admission, up from 1.1 as baseline. BUN:Cr ratio approx. 15:1. In setting of dry MM on exam, thought to be likely hypovolemic. Patient was aggressively repleted with IVF during hospital stay and creatinine returned to 1.2, at or near baseline of 1.1. UA unremarkable. . sarcoidosis - Patient's respiratory status stable. Had been on chronic steroid therapy as recently as , at which time the patient self-tapered himself off the steroids because he was having weight gain. Steroid-sparing therapy not opted for in setting of cirrhosis. Per OMR note, plan was to restart prednisone at 10 mg/d at pulmonary clinic appointment on day of admission. His being off steroid therapy for the last several months may explain possible recurrence of symptoms and worsening of hypercalcemia. - Prednisone was started at 10 mg/day as ordered in pulmonary clinic. . ulnar neuropathy - Patient has chronic pain requiring oxycodone for pain relief. Pulmonary thinks this may have be sarcoidosis and had planned to get EMG, but patient has not gotten this done yet. - continue oxycodone - renally dosed gabapentin HPI: yo M c HCV, EtOH cirrhosis, sarcoidosis, h/o polysubstance abuse p/w AMS. Patient had been in EtOH detoxification program until 2 days PTA, at which time he was discharged home. The patient has had longtime struggles with insomnia and has used alcohol and/or BZDs in the past to help him sleep. After discharge from Withdrawal, his psychiatric meds were changed to Quetiapine and Doxepin. He began to use the Seroquel (25 mg hs) to help him sleep. Per the patient's mother, the patient has been altered and had unsteady gait since returning home. The patient acknowledges that he has been unsteady on his feet and has had slurred speech in the mornings after waking up since starting the Seroquel at night, but does not feel that he has been confused. Patient has also noted increased abdominal swelling with a corresponding weight gain of 35 pounds. in the last mos. The patient also Reports that he had one episode of mild hemoptysis at home, characterized as a little bit of blood in phlegm. He Reports that this has happened to him before; he Experiences that it is associated with Recurrent coughing and his known pulmonary disease. Patient presented to pulmonary clinic today for follow-up and was documented as altered with elevated Cr and hypercalcemia, and was sent to the ED for further evaluation. Of note, the patient was precribed spironolactone but has not been taking this medication since . . In the ED, the patient's VS were T 96.6, P 76, BP 117/67, R 18, O2 95% on RA. He was found to have a distended abdomen and isolated Crackles at his RLL. CXR and RUQ U/S were performed, which were unchanged compared to prior. His Cr was noted to be 1.9, which is increased from his baseline. He was AAOx2 on initial presentation and had mild asterexis. A diagnostic para was attempted, but a suitable fluid pocket was not visualized. He was given one dose of lactulose and had a large bowel movement, after which he was AAO x3. VS at the time of transfer were 97.9, 72, 113/54, 18, 94% on Rheumatoid (went down to 90%). . On the floor, the patient is stable without any acute complaints, other than chronic neuropathic pain in his hands bilaterally. . ROS: Denies fever, chills, night sweats, vision changes, Runny nose, congestion, sore throat, shortness of breath, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, BRBPR, melena, hematochezia, dysuria, hematuria. Endorses stable DOE. Hospital Course: AMS - Etiology likely seroquel administration vs. excessive gabapentin serum levels in setting of renal failure vs. hepatic encephalopathy given improvement in alertness, orientation later into the day, and after a dose of lactulose. DDx would include hypercalcemia, toxicity (BZD, TCA levels positive on serum screen), psychotropic medications (Seroquel, Doxepin), or infection. Ethanol level negative on serum tox screen. Low suspicion for infection given lack of fevers, no elevation in WBC. CXR stable. Patient continued to receive lactulose, renally dosed gabapentin, received aggressive IVF for hypercalcemia, and was not given Seroquel. The patient had improved alertness during hospital stay and no further episodes of Dysarthric speech or difficulty walking. - The patient will likely need a new sleep aid to try instead of Seroquel. Trazodone was used in-house with some success but this should be further discussed as an outpatient. - Seroquel was discontinued on discharge. - Gabapentin was decreased on discharge and should be further titrated as an outpatient. . HCV / EtOH cirrhosis - MELD 18 on admission. Known varices on EGD in but no e/o bleeding at this time. Hct 39.1 on admission, although this may represent a hemoconcentrated value. No suitable pocket of ascites appropriate for paracentesis identified on U/S in ED. Blood Pressure was therefore thought to be an improbable etiology. - Continued lactulose to BM/day. . ARF - Cr 1.9 on admission, up from 1.1 as baseline. BUN:Cr ratio approx. 15:1. In setting of Oliguric MM on exam, thought to be likely hypovolemic. Patient was aggressively repleted with IVF during hospital stay and creatinine returned to 1.2, at or near baseline of 1.1. UA unremarkable. . sarcoidosis - Patient's respiratory status stable. Had been on chronic steroid therapy as recently as , at which time the patient Tapered off off the steroids because he was having weight gain. Steroid-sparing therapy not opted for in setting of cirrhosis. Per OMR note, plan was to restart prednisone at 10 mg/d at pulmonary clinic appointment on day of admission. His being off steroid therapy for the last several months may explain possible recurrence of Clinical manifestations and worsening of hypercalcemia. - Prednisone was started at 10 mg/day as ordered in pulmonary clinic. . ulnar neuropathy - Patient has chronic pain requiring oxycodone for pain relief. Pulmonary thinks this may have be sarcoidosis and had planned to get EMG, but patient has not gotten this done yet. - continue oxycodone - Kidney dosed gabapentin

HPI: h/o colon cancer s/p sigmoidectomy , lung metastasis s/p VATS LUL wedge resection and , s/p chemotherapy, ex-lap and SBR for closed loop small bowel obstruction local cancer recurrence in and history of pSBO treated conservatively in . She now presents with 3 days of nausea and emesis of bilious fluid and abdominal distension. She last had a bowel movement 2 days ago and usually has one bowel movement per day. She continues to pass flatus. She does NOT have abdominal pain. She denies fevers, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, or bloody stools. Hospital Course: The patient presented to Emergency Department on with nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. Upon admission, the patient was made NPO, given intravenous fluids, and underwent serial abdominal examinations. An NGT was placed for bowel decompression. The patient continued to have nausea, abdominal fullness after NGT removed. She underwent an x-ray of the abdomen which showed dilated stomach and proximal small bowel. She was taken to the operating room on HD 4, where she underwent an exploratory laparotomy, lysis of adhesions, and a side to side bypass. The operative course was stable ( see operative note), with a 20cc blood loss. The patient was extubated after the procedure and monitored in the recovery room. She was later transferred to the surgical floor for post-operative care. CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a Pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good Pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. GI/GU/FEN: The patient was initially kept NPO with a tube in place for decompression. On , the NGT was removed, therefore, the diet was advanced sequentially to a Regular diet, which was well tolerated. patient's intake and output were closely monitored ID: The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were none. HEME: The patient's blood counts were closely watched for signs of bleeding, of which there were none. Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile and hemodynamically stable. The patient was tolerating a diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan. HPI: year old female with a history of CAD, CHF (EF 30%) and recent complicated inpatient and outpatient course (see below), presents with recurrence of diarrhea after finishing course of flagyl. Course began in early , when she was treated for flu vs URI with a Zpack. She then was admitted with chest pain, found to have STEMI and had 6 stents placed, meanwhile being treated with IV antibiotics for pneumonia. After discharge she quickly was readmitted for chest pain which was thought to be due to gastritis seen on EGD. She developed diarrhea during that admission but was discharged, and continued to not be treated as an outpatient for approximately 1 week until she was admitted for persistent diarrhea and dehydration. At that time Clostridium difficile was diagnosed and treatment with Flagyl initiated. During that hospitalization the patient had gastrointestinal bleeding . She had a dropping hematocrit due to gastritis and C diff, but was stabalized after 2 units of pRBCs. She was later found to have Left DVT and was started on heparin/coumadin. Since then had had some complications with volume overload. The patient was doing well at home and completed course of flagyl on . She continued to feel well, have formed stools times a day for several days and then developed perfuse diarrhea. Stools were brown, watery, nonbloody, nonmucusy. She then also developed a fever to 101 and nausea/dry heaving and presented to an outside hospital ED. Transfered to per family request. patient had been tolerating minimal PO liquids and solids. Denies associated abdominal pain, chest pain, palpitations, lightheadedness/dizziness. In the ED, initial vitals: T 97.5 HR 95 BP 156/77 RR 18 O2 98RA, but had been running systolic blood pressures in the . She received Zofran, and 500mg PO flagyl and 1L IV fluids. Labs were significant for 11 with neutrophil predominance, INR therapeutic at 2.6, acidotic HCO3 of 16, Mg 1.6, renal function , HCT 38 up from yest. Per transfer summary, patient had lost 5lbs Hospital Course: year old female with a history of CAD status post several stents, chronic systolic CHF with and EF of 30%, DVT on coumadin, presents with recurrent diarrhea several days after finishing course of Flagyl. . 1) Clostridium difficile colitis: Her current infection with C. diff is likely a recurrence after her initial flagyl course. The patient initially had associated with nausea/vomiting and appeared quite ill with tachycardia, fevers, tachypnea and baseline hypotension. She improved with hydration and initiation of IV flagyl/po vanco. No significant abdominal pain or leukocytosis. The patient will complete a 14 day course of Vancomycin 125mg by mouth every 6 hours. . 2) Acute on chronic systolic CHF with EF of 30%: Initially the patient was treated with IV fluids to remedy her severe dehydration secondary to diarrhea. Once her diarrhea improved the patient developed edema in her legs bilaterally, while her lungs remained clear. The patient was treated with IV Lasix and her home Lasix dose was increased from 20mg by mouth every other day to 20 mg by mouth every day. The patient has follow up with her cardiologist. She was also continued on lisinopril and metoprolol. . 3) Hypotension/Tachycardia: When the patient first arrived in the hospital she was severely dehydrated and this caused hypotension (80/50s) and tacchycardia (100). This was likely secondary to volume depletion. She was treated with controlled fluid resusitation. Her home blood pressure medications were also changed. Her nitroglycerine patch was discontinued and her Metoprolol was changed from 75mg po BID to 25mg po BID. She is now normotensive. . 4) hx Deep vein thrombosis: She was continued on her home coumadin with goal INR . This dose was adjusted while on antibiotics. She will have her INR checked on . . 5) CAD s/p multiple stents: Pt is currently not on aspirin due to a hx of gastritis as well as current ongoing plavix (recent stent) and coumadin (recent DVT) When she completes the coumadin course for her DVT, she will restart on aspirin. She was also continued on metoprolol, lisinopril, statin. . 6) CODE status: FULL CODE . HPI: year old woman with hypertension, hyperlipidemia and pseudoxanthoma elasticum (elastic tissue disease), sleep apnea on CPAP and a cardiac history significant for CABG in with a LIMA to LAD, SVG to OM and SVG to RCA. She also has peripheral vascular disease and has stents to right internal iliac artery and RSFA . Most recent ABIs done were 0.43 on the right, 0.48 on the left. . On , had cardiac and peripheral angiography which showed 40% distal LMCA stenosis. The LAD was patent. The LCX was occluded. The RCA had a 60-70% mid vessel stenosis. The LIMA to LAD was patent. The SVG to OM was patent. The SVG to RCA had a 90% stenosis at the touchdown site. S/p successful PCI to the SVG to RcA with a 2.5 x 12mm Promus DES. Peripheral angiography was notable for bilateral occluded SFA lesions with significant collateral and reconstitution at the popliteals. . She Had stress echo on which was concerning for questionable anteroseptal wall abnormality. . She is active at baseline with walking on treadmill 30 min times a week. She gets claudication symptoms after walking 10 mins but able to complete 30 mins with bearable pain, however states this pain is better than prior. She recently lost 85 lbs using the metafast weight loss program. . ROS: (-) TIA (-) CVA (-) GIB (+) sleep apnea (-) DM She denied chest pain or SOB or lower extremity swelling. She denied PND or orthopnea. Hospital Course: year old female with CABG in with a LIMA to LAD, SVG to OM and SVG to RCA along with PVD who had SVG->RCA DES in . She was transferred to for catheterization after abnormal stress echo. She had PCI with SVG->RCA DES on . She did well post-procedure and discharged to be followed up with her primary care physician and interventional cardiologist. There is consideration for left main PCI later on. . CORONARIES: CABG in with a LIMA to LAD, SVG to OM and SVG to RCA along with PVD who underwent SVG->RCA DES with consideration for staged left main PCI later on. HPI: Per Dr. has class III morbid obesity with weight of 273.1 pounds as of with her initial screen weight on as 277.8 pounds, height of 66.5 inches and BMI of 43.4. Her previous weight loss efforts have included, Nutrisystem, the diet, , off-label prescription weight loss medications fenfluramine/phentermine ("Fen/Phen"), hypnosis, hCG and 's "I Can Change" program losing up to 40 pounds but her weight loss attempts have failed to produce lasting results. She weighed 227 pounds in , 242 pounds in , 255.7 pounds in and she weighed 267 pounds one year ago. Her highest adult weight was 277.9 pounds on . She stated she has been struggling with weight since high school and cites as factors contributing to her excess weight genetics, grazing, too many refined carbohydrates, emotional eating, convenience eating and lack of exercise. She does walk twice a week for 30 minutes and does have a treadmill at home that she is planning to use. She does enjoy swimming in the /summer and is looking forward to the warmer weather. She denied history of eating disorders - no anorexia, bulimia, diuretic or laxative abuse and she denied binge eating. She does have history of depression and has been seen by a therapist, has not been hospitalized for mental health issues and she is on psychotropic medication (venlafaxine). Hospital Course: The patient presented to pre-op on . Pt was evaluated by anaesthesia and taken to the operating room where she underwent a sleeve gastrectomy. There were no adverse events in the operating room; please see the operative note for details. Pt was extubated, taken to the PACU until stable, then transferred to the ward for observation. Neuro: The patient was alert and oriented throughout hospitalization; pain was initially managed with a PCA and then transitioned to oral Roxicet once tolerating a stage 2 diet. CV: The patient remained stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. GI/GU/FEN: The patient was initially kept NPO. An upper GI study (POD1) was negative for a leak, therefore, the diet was advanced sequentially to a Bariatric Stage 3 diet, which was well tolerated. patient's intake and output were closely monitored. ID: The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were none. HEME: The patient's blood counts were closely watched for signs of bleeding, of which there were none. Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating a stage 3 diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.
