HPI: Ms is a female known to the general surgery service after undergoing small bowel resection and revision of ileoanal anastamosis in for SBO/internal hernia, who has a history of total proctocolectomy with ilealanal pouch in at an OSH for a history of ulcerative colitis with persistant abdominal pain and recurrent SBOs now thought to have Crohns disease. She presents complaining of abdominal distention that started about 1 week ago associated with nausea without vomiting. She reports decreased PO intake. She continues to have 6 BMs a day but is now noticing blood in stool occasionally over past week. She then developed severe abdominal pain over the past few days. She denies fevers, chills, chest pain, dyspnea, or dysuria. Of note, she underwent sigmoidoscopy in by Dr showed normal ileum but +inflammation at distal rectum, and was recommended to continue Canasa suppositories. Hospital Course: Ms. presented to the ED with nausea, abdominal pain and distention. She was admitted to the Surgery Service for observation overnight. On HD 3, Gastroenterology was consulted for further evaluation of her abdominal pain given her history of Inflammatory Bowel Disease. Per their recommendation, a MR was obtained. This showed mild thickening of the small bowel in the right lower quadrant with associated mesenteric lymphadenopathy. There was again no evidence of obstruction. An ESR and CRP showed mild elevation. Per GI, it was felt that her pain was not secondary to Crohn's. Her diet was slowly advanced subsequently over the next three days and she tolerated a regular diet. Her pain was well controlled with pain medication and her nausea improved. Given that there was no evidence of obstruction, the decision was made for Ms. to follow-up with Dr. as an outpatient in order to schedule her hernia repair. She was discharged home in stable condition on HD 5. HPI: Ms is a female known to the general surgery service after undergoing small bowel resection and revision of ileoanal anastamosis in for SBO/internal hernia, who has a history of total proctocolectomy with ilealanal pouch in at an OSH for a history of ulcerative colitis with persistant abdominal pain and recurrent SBOs now thought to have Crohns disease. She presents complaining of abdominal distention that started about 1 week ago associated with nausea without vomiting. She reports decreased Oral intake. She continues to have 6 BMs a day but is now noticing blood in stool occasionally over past week. She then developed severe abdominal pain over the past few days. She denies fevers, chills, chest pain, dyspnea, or dysuria. Of note, she underwent sigmoidoscopy in by Dr showed normal ileum but +inflammation at distal rectum, and was recommended to continue Canasa suppositories. Hospital Course: Ms. presented to the ED with nausea, abdominal pain and distention. She was admitted to the Surgery Service for observation overnight. On HD 3, Gastroenterology was consulted for further evaluation of her abdominal pain given her history of Inflammatory Bowel Disease. Per their recommendation, a MR was obtained. This showed mild thickening of the small bowel in the right lower quadrant with associated mesenteric lymphadenopathy. There was again no evidence of obstruction. An ESR and CRP showed mild elevation. Per GI, it was felt that her pain was not secondary to Crohn's. Her diet was slowly advanced subsequently over the next three days and she tolerated a regular diet. Her pain was well controlled with pain medication and her nausea Relieved. Given that there was no evidence of obstruction, the decision was made for Ms. to follow-up with Dr. as an Ambulatory in order to schedule her hernia repair. She was discharged home in stable condition on HD 5.
ICD: K52.9 ICD: K52.9

HPI: Ms. is a yo G2P0 @ 34w3d with chronic hypertension who presents for routine prenatal visit. She was admitted to the antepartum service from for severe range blood pressures. Her medications were increased to Nifedipine CR 60 mg BID and labetalol 200 mg TID with improved control of her blood pressures. A repeat 24 hour urine was not significantly different from her baseline. Her labs remained stable and fetal testing was reassuring. She did receive a course of rescue betamethasone and was rescue complete on . She was discharged on and has been lost to follow up since that time. Reports cccasional tightening contractions. Denies VB, LOF. +FM. Denies HA, vision changes, RUQ/epigastric pain. She does report worsening lower extremity edema up to her knees. She is currently taking baby aspirin, PNV, nifedipine 60 mg BID and labetalol 200 mg TID. She has been taking her blood pressures at home and reports that they have been "good," but did not bring the readings with her today. She reports that she is "okay" today. Hospital Course: On Ms. was admitted for IOL for chronic hypertension with superimposed pre-eclampsia with acute kidney injury. Upon admission she was started on IV magnesium and her labor was induced with PO cytotec, foley bulb, and then Pitocin. She subsequently had an uncomplicated vaginal delivery. Postpartum she received 24 hours of IV magnesium and was then continued on nifedipine 60 mg BID and labetalol 200 mg TID with appropriate blood pressures. Throughout her admission her creatinine continued to improve, initially 1.6 on admission with downtrend to 1.2 on day of discharge. She was discharged home on in stable condition with appropriate follow-up scheduled. HPI: Ms. is a yo G2P0 @ 34w3d with chronic hypertension who presents for routine prenatal visit. She was admitted to the antepartum service from for severe range blood pressures. Her medications were increased to Nifedipine CR 60 mg BID and labetalol 200 mg TID with improved control of her blood pressures. A repeat 24 hour urine was not significantly different from her baseline. Her labs remained stable and fetal testing was reassuring. She did receive a course of rescue betamethasone and was rescue complete on . She was discharged on and has been lost to follow up since that time. Reports cccasional tightening contractions. Denies VB, LOF. +FM. Denies HA, vision changes, RUQ/epigastric pain. She does report worsening lower extremity edema up to her knees. She is currently taking baby aspirin, Propranolol, nifedipine 60 mg BID and labetalol 200 mg TID. She has been taking her blood pressures at home and reports that they have been "good," but did not bring the readings with her today. She reports that she is "okay" today. Hospital Course: On Ms. was admitted for IOL for chronic hypertension with Pre-eclampsia superimposed on chronic hypertension with acute kidney injury. Upon admission she was started on IV magnesium and her labor was induced with PO cytotec, foley bulb, and then Oxytocin. She subsequently had an uncomplicated vaginal delivery. Postpartum she received 24 hours of IV magnesium and was then continued on nifedipine 60 mg BID and labetalol 200 mg TID with appropriate blood pressures. Throughout her admission her creatinine continued to improve, initially 1.6 on admission with downtrend to 1.2 on day of discharge. She was discharged home on in stable condition with appropriate follow-up Planned.

HPI: The patient is a year old man with OSA on Bipap, mixed restrictive and obstructive lung disease admitted with dsypnea and 21 pound weight gain over 2 weeks with edema and wheezing on initial presentation on , after seeing PCP. He was admitted to after presenting with worsening abdominal distension and weight gain. He was also having increased shortness of breath and could not get from the bed to the door without gasping for air. While there, he was given TID lasix, 20 TID then 40 TID (although home diuretic is bumex). Weights did not come down and still markedly edematous. He was sent down to to take off fluid and didn't feel there was a good pocket. He was also given prednisone and Levoquin for possible concominant COPD exacerbation Per PCP the patient came to her in and she did obtain hepatitis serologies at the time which were netgative. He did come with a diagnosis of "cirrhosis" with remote history of alcohol use. He had an EGD in records) that showed varices and portal gastropathy but was not set up with a hepatologist. He has had multiple admissions to for colitis, ?Urosepsis. Patient's other PMHx significant for CAD with s/p stents, colon cancer s/p resection and chemo, dCHF, cirrhosis as described above, Asthma/COPD, and OSA. Also had a circumcision recently due to recurrent UTIs Pain: The patient denied abdominal pain, but did not pain consistent with his arthritis, for which he typically takes medications at home. He notes that his arthritis pain had been increased in the days and weeks leading up to admission, in the setting of his significant weight gain. Hospital Course: This is a year old man with known OSA on Bipap, mixed restrictive and obstructive lung disease, as well as cirrhosis, admitted with dyspnea and 21 pound weight gain over 2 weeks with edema and wheezing on initial presentation. We suspect that he is well above an ultimate dry weight, but given his insidious weight gain, cannot be sure of his eventual dry weight. He did lose a significant amount of weight during the admission, and continues to appear volume overloaded overall. His pulmonary and abdominal symptoms improved with the initial phase of diuresis, although we suspect he will benefit from further diuresis as an outpatient. CHF - Pt's dramatic weight gain and dyspnea were strong indicators of heart failure. Had also had an echo on at OSH which showed Suboptimal image quality, views were extremely limited. BNP at OSH is not markedly elevated now but this could be falsely low due to obesity. Upon presentation to our team, we diuresed him with IV lasix to which he put out 5L urine. However, his Cr increased mildly, indicating a possible component of right sided heart failaure, so a cardiology consult was requested for right heart cath. He underwent a right heart cath showing elevated pressures in all chambers indicating BiV failure. We continued to diurese the patient but switched him to PO torsemide 40 mg daily and spironolactone 25 mg daily. The thought behind this was to have better intestinal absorption with torsemide (vs. bumex which was his outpatient med) and spironolactone would be good for his cirrhosis and systolic dysfunction. We were able to remove a signficant amount of fluid but patient remained total body volume overloaded on exam at the time of discharge. His pulmonary examination had improved significantly, and he did not require supplemental oxygen with ambulation. He reported his DOE had greatly improved, as had his joint pains. We do suspect that he will require significant continued diuresis, and developed a close plan with his internist and home services tailored to heart failure for ongoing . Liver cirrhosis - etiology likely NASH given DM and obesity and was diagnosed years ago. Had negative serologies for hepatitis in the past and shown to be negative. cirrhosis was confirmed by EGD at OSH from shows varices with portal gastropathy. Liver was consulted who recommended guided paracentesis and determine etiology of ascites. This resulted in a high SAAG and low TP consistent with likely increased portal pressures with component of CHF. They also recommended outpatient EGD, AFP, serial RUQ US, and slew of labs (hep serologies, iron studies, AMA, , IgG). positive at 180, the rest were unremarkable. He was also started on nadolol (from metoprolol) and spironolactone as above. He will follow up with Hepatology as an outpatient. It should be noted at no point during his presentation was he thought to be in decompensated cirrhosis. ARF- Baseline Cr of 1.0 with peak of 1.7 at OSH. His FeUrea at 25% with high BUN/Cr all consistent with pre-renal. Diuresed initially given evidence that his kidneys slightly recovered since onset of diuresis at OSH (see above for lasix dose). As we needed to diurese, we tolerated a Cr of 1.7-1.8 during this hospitalization. Patient had high urine output with no signs of uremia. Chest pain- Pt experienced chest pain during his hospitalization that was likely GERD given no EKG changes. He was also started on iron, which may have been causing nausea and GERD. Iron was stopped and patient kept receiving his GERD meds with resolution of chest pain Iron Deficiency Anemia- diagnosed during this hospitalization. Hx of colon cancer and last scope in with polyps with recs for repeat q years. Needs repeat colonoscopy, can do as outpatient. We held off iron therapy as it was causing constipation ? PNA at OSH - received levaquin at OSH because the left hemidiaphragm was obscured on CXR there. CXR here does not show infiltrate here. Levoquin was d/ced COPD - was treated as COPD exacerbation with Levoquin and steroids. CXR does not show much hyperinflation and history is not too convincing for COPD flare. We d/c further steroids. He continued to receive albuterol and ipratropium q 6 hrs CAD - no chest pain now, although was reporting some chest pain over the past week prior to admission to that radiated to R shoulder. There were no ST changes on EKG. We continued atorvastatin 20 mg. We changed his aspirin 325 to aspirin 81 as there was no obvious indication for high dose aspirin Knee pain- continued oxycodone 5mg q 6 hrs prn Asthma - on nebs for COPD/PNA. GAD - continued home alprazolam. . Hypothyroidism - cont home levoxyl. . GERD - continued home omeprazole 40 mg BID Constipation- continued senna, colace, mirolax Transitional Issues -Patient was admitted for CHF and bumex was changed to torsemide outpatient. He was also put on spironolactone. His beta blocker was changed to nadolol from metoprolol for variceal bleeding prophylaxis. -He was seen by the Liver service. He will need an MRI of the liver due to an inadequate ultrasound related to his obesity as well as upper and lower endoscopies for workup for iron deficiency anemia. He has had endoscopies relatively recently, and will speak with his internist about when to proceed with further examinations. -Pt was found to have iron deficiency anemia. Given his history of colon cancer and colonoscopy every years, he should have a repeat colonscopy ASAP as outpatient (last one in with polyps). -Pt was also found to have high TSH with normal free T4. These labs should be repeated as outpatient. We did not change his home dose of levothyroxine - Palliative care was consulted, given the patient's request to discuss his goals of further treatment. The patient was focused on returning home directly following the admission, and chose to defer the inpatient team's recommendation to be transitioned to an acute rehab for ongoing diuresis. He was arranged with a heart-failure focused home program and close PCP in keeping with his goal of returning home to continue diuresis. HPI: Mr. is a yo gentleman with hx of atrial fibrillation, HTN, TIA, and disease who is presenting after a holter monitor showed several pauses one of which was 5.9 seconds. The patient says that he has been in overall good health. He used to work out several days a week on a treadmill. About 3 weeks ago he was at his regular work out and he became extremely dizzy. He had to slow down his work out and he felt improved. After that he had increasing episodes of dizziness with exertion. He never lost consciousness or passed out. They were all while he was moving around. He finally had an episode crossing the road and decided he needed to be evaluated. He presented to urgent care. At urgent care he had an ECG w/ atrial fibrillation and they recommended the patient see his regular cardiologist. He saw his cardiologist on and was no longer in atrial fibrillation but was bradycardia. She had recommended a holter to look for pauses. He wore the holter over the weekend and pressed the button a few times. They called him today and told him to go to the ED for evaluation because of long pauses on the holter. In the ED he was afebrile. He had an isolated BP up to 176 otherwise his systolics were in the 140s. He remained 99% on RA. His exam was non-concerning. He had a laboratory evaluation that showed a Cr of 1.1, normal CBC, and a trop < 0.01. INR 2.8 He had a cxr that was normal. ECG: sinus brady to 47 On the floor he endorsed the above. Hospital Course: year old man with history including paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (CHADS2-VASc 5, on warfarin), HTN, HLD, disease who presented with symptomatic sinus bradycardia and conversion pauses most consistent with sinus node dysfunction/sick sinus syndrome in the setting of longstanding AF. Transitional Issues: []Patient to follow up with Dr. , device clinic follow-up in 1 week []continued on home Coumadin regimen on discharge, next INR check []Replaced home 2.5 bid isradipine with 5 amlodipine qd Active Issues: Atrial fibrillation, Paroxysmal Symptomatic Bradycardia Had episode of presyncope while walking and recurrent exertional lightheadedness. Holter with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and up to 5.9s conversion pauses noted. While symptoms of lightheadedness do not entirely correlate with time of pauses on monitor, he clearly has evidence of sinus node dysfunction and resting sinus rates 40-50s in the absence of nodal agents. EP consulted and recommend pacemaker implant for sinus node dysfunction. Patient is in agreement. has been discussed with his primary cardiologist, Dr. he has been stable on warfarin and preferred to continue. Dual chamber PPM placed . Interrogated the following day, pacer function normal with acceptable lead measurements and battery status. Discharged with the following plan: continue home Coumadin, repeat INR check at clinic on , follow up 1 week in device clinic. Chronic Issues: Parkinsons - continued home amantadine, ropinirol, rasagiline Hypertension - held home isradipine on admission, started on 5 amlodipine. Per patient preference, discharged on amlodipine. HPI: male with a history of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy s/p septal ablation and myomectomy, s/p ICD, presenting with chest pain. Patient usually has episodes of chest pain 4 times per day that last 20 minutes each. Today, he had onset of pain around 5 pm that continued longer than usual (has not yet been pain free), but without shortness of breath. He does report palpitations at times during these episodes, which he reports as being new (though has been noted during past admissions). He had lightheadedness at time of onset. He also had nausea with vomiting x 2 prior to coming to the ED. As the plan with Dr. was to present for care if pain lasted more than 90 minutes, he came into the ED today. His last cardiology admission was in , which is the last time he remembers having chest pain that lingered. During that admission, EP study was done, with no inducible arrhythmias. A number of medication changes were attempted (digoxin, quinidine) but stopped at patient's request. No etiology of his pain was uncovered. In the ED today, initial vitals T 97.9, BP 125/81, HR 123, RR 18, O2 sat 98% on RA. As SL-NTG was contraindicated, he was given morphine 4 mg IV boluses for pain. First set cardiac biomarkers was negative, and he was admitted to . On review of systems, ? TIA (thought at one time that diagnosis was in question). He denies any prior history of stroke, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. denies recent fevers, chills or rigors (though does note feeling warm en route to the ED). All of the other complete review of systems were negative. Cardiac review of systems is notable for absence of dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, syncope. Hospital Course: Chest Pain: Patient was admitted to the hospital after experiencing a prolonged period (>90 minutes) of left sided chest pain. He underwent extensive work up for chest pain, given his history of NSVT, he was monitored with telemetry and showed no evidence of arrhythmia. Electrophysiology was also consulted to perform a device interrogation of his ICD, which showed no recorded arrhythmic events or ICD firing. patient's EKG showed no acute changes and two sets of cardiac biomarkers were negative. Given nature of pain (sharper, knife-like), patient underwent CTA which was negative for Pulmonary Embolism or Aortic Dissection. His chest pain resolved with IV Morphine, and he was discharged to follow up with his outpatient Cardiologist. Inflammed Pancreas: CTA was notable for peripancreatic stranding per Radiology report. Amylase and Lipase were obtained and noted to be normal. He also showed no clinical signs of pancreatitis as he was able to tolerate full meals with no pain, nausea. CARDIAC: PUMP: On Echo in , LVEF noted to be >55%, euvolemic on examination. He has history of HCM s/p spetal ablation and myomectomy. He was continued on his home regimen of Metoprolol, verapamil and ASA. ISCHEMIA: patient's last Cardiac Catheterization was performed recently on and showed a right dominant system with normal coronaries. RHYTHM: Given his risk for sudden cardiac death, he has a Guidant Vitality DS model T 125 dual chamber ICD. On admission to the ED, he was noted to be in sinus tachycardia with prior noted RBB in the ED. As mentioned previously, he had no recent episodes of NSVT on device interrogation. HPI: male with a history of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy s/p septal ablation and myomectomy, s/p ICD, presenting with chest pain. Patient usually has episodes of chest pain 4 times per day that last 20 minutes each. Today, he had Start pain around 5 pm that continued longer than usual (has not yet been pain free), but without shortness of breath. He does report palpitations at times during these episodes, which he reports as being new (though has been noted during past admissions). He had lightheadedness at time of onset. He also had nausea with vomiting x 2 prior to coming to the ED. As the plan with Dr. was to present for care if pain lasted more than 90 minutes, he came into the ED today. His last cardiology admission was in , which is the last time he remembers having chest pain that lingered. During that admission, EP study was done, with no inducible arrhythmias. A number of medication changes were attempted (digoxin, quinidine) but stopped at Patient request. No etiology of his pain was uncovered. In the ED today, initial vitals T 97.9, BP 125/81, HR 123, RR 18, O2 sat 98% on RA. As SL-NTG was contraindicated, he was given morphine 4 mg IV boluses for pain. First set cardiac biomarkers was negative, and he was admitted to . On review of systems, ? TIA (thought at one time that diagnosis was in question). He denies any prior history of stroke, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of Operation, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. denies recent fevers, chills or rigors (though does note feeling warm en route to the ED). All of the other complete review of systems were negative. Cardiac review of systems is notable for absence of dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle edema, syncope. Hospital Course: Chest Pain: Patient was admitted to the hospital after experiencing a prolonged period (>90 minutes) of left sided chest pain. Electrophysiology was also consulted to perform a device interrogation of his ICD, which showed no recorded arrhythmic events or ICD firing. Patient's EKG showed no acute changes and two sets of cardiac biomarkers were negative. Given nature of pain (sharper, knife-like), Patient underwent CTA which was negative for Pulmonary Embolism or Aortic Dissection. His chest pain resolved with IV Morphine, and he was discharged to follow up with his outpatient Cardiologist. Inflammed Pancreas: CTA was notable for peripancreatic stranding per Radiology report. Amylase and Lipase were obtained and noted to be normal. He also showed no clinical signs of pancreatitis as he was able to tolerate full meals with no pain, nausea. CARDIAC: PUMP: On Echo in , LVEF noted to be >55%, euvolemic on examination. He has history of HCM s/p spetal ablation and Uterine surgery. He was continued on his home regimen of Metoprolol, verapamil and Aspirin. ISCHEMIA: Patient's last Cardiac Catheterization was performed recently on and showed a right dominant system with normal coronaries. No pain with passive motion of hip, knee, ankle, toes Saphenous, Sural, Deep Peroneal, Superficial Peroneal, Tibial sensation intact to light touch Extensor Hallucis Longus, Flexor Hallucis Longus, Tibialis Anterior fire 1+ posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis pulse LABS: I personally reviewed labs and noted Hematocrit 37, Peripheral White Blood Cell Count 8, ESR/CRP Pending IMPRESSION: Right Hip Drainage, seroma, hematoma s/p revision total hip arthroplasty rule out septic hip. Discussion with regarding the diagnosis, differential diagnosis and options for further diagnosis and treatment. I discussed alternatives of treatment, advantages and disadvantages of different treatment methods and relative risks and benefits RECOMMENDATIONS: Incisional Vac Continue Partial Weight Bearing Precuations/Posterior Hip Precautions/Abduction Precautions Hold Antibiotics Hospital Course: The was admitted to the orthopaedic surgery service for post-op wound drainage. Based on our exam and lab results, we felt it was unlikely she has a post op infection, and an incisional vac was placed. Her WBC remained normal and her CPR trended down during her hospital stay. She seen by physical therapy, and she was cleared to discharge back to home with home care. Her pain was controlled with oral pain medications. The received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. At the time of discharge the was tolerating a regular diet and feeling well. The was afebrile with stable vital signs. The hematocrit was acceptable and pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen. The operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign. The weight-bearing status is: Right leg 50% weight bearing for 1 month, abductor off precautions for 2 months (no active abduction), knee immobilizer for 1 week but should come out four times per day for knee ROM, no hip flexion more than 60 degrees. Posterior precautions. No strenuous exercise or heavy lifting until follow up appointment. Mobilize frequently. Ms is discharged to home in stable condition. HPI: Pt.is y/o male with hx of hypertriglyceridemia, HTN, OSA, DMII, neuropathy, essential tremor, and prior diagnosis of acute pancreatitis 3 weeks ago at in after experience abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, and several episodes of loose stools . CT at that time was notable for mild acute pancreatitis/duodenitis, a high density fluid collection posterior to the pancreatic tail, and a small nonocclusive filling defect in SMV suspicious for thrombus. patient underwent IVF resuscitation and with an hospital course and was subsequently discharged home. Since discharge pt. states he as been unable to tolerate Po intake eating solid food once in the past 10 days. While traveling for a funeral the past couple of day his emesis, abdominal pain, and diarrhea have intensified culminating in evaluation at where CT findings were again notable for edema and inflammation around the pancreas, fluid collection in both the tail(5.7x3.1cm) and head(2.9x2.2cm) of the pancreas, as well fluid extending down the right abdomen. On presentation, pt is not in acute distress, persistently hypotensive 80-90 systolic despite receiving 3L at the OSH endorsing continued abdominal pain, dry mouth, diarrhea, a frustrating lack of PO intake. Pt denies nausea/vomiting today, chest pain, LOC, prior MI, melena, or headache. Hospital Course: Mr. is a y/o male with hx of hypertriglyceridemia, HTN, OSA, DMII, neuropathy, essential tremor, and recent diagnosis of acute pancreatitis at in after experiencing abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, and several episodes of loose stools . He was discharged from and then later presented at in with abdominal pain and emesis. CT findings at were again notable for edema and inflammation around the pancreas, fluid collection in both the tail(5.7x3.1cm) and head(2.9x2.2cm) of the pancreas, as well fluid extending down the right abdomen. The patient was transferred to in hypovolemic shock and was admitted to the Acute Care Surgery service on . Blood culture from grew staphylococcus coagulase negative and he was started on vancomycin. Repeat blood cultures were sent which were negative and vancomycin was discontinued. The patient was transferred to the ICU and was made NPO with IVF for hydration. Gastroenterology was consulted for endoscopy. Abdominal ultrasound revealed gallstones and his pancreatitis was thought to be due to gallstone pancreatitis. On , CTA was done to evaluate for SMV thrombus and a nonocclusive thrombus was seen in the splenic vein. The patient was started on a heparin drip which was later transitioned to warfarin with lovenox bridging. On , the patient went for upper endoscopy with Gastroenterology which revealed multiple shallow nonbleeding clean-based ulcers in the examined duodenum, a markedly edematous and distorted pancreatic parenchyma in the setting of acute pancreatitis, several acute pancreatic and peripancreatic fluid collections. The CBD could not be assessed due to distorted anatomy in the setting of acute pancreatitis. A nasojejunal tube was placed so that the patient could receive tube feedings. Tube feeds were initiated on which the patient tolerated. Tube feeds were later changed from continuous to cycled. On , the patient was taken to the operating room where he underwent laparoscopic cholecystectomy. This procedure went well (reader, please refer to operative note for details). After remaining hemodynamically stable in the PACU, the patient was transferred to the surgical floor. Pain was managed with a hydromorphone PCA initially. On POD 2, the PCA was d/c'd and oxycodone and acetaminophen were prescribed. The patient continued on Tube feeds which he tolerated. Given that the patient lives in , follow-up care appointments were arranged in his home state. Please see discharge worksheet for further details. INR check . All other INR check with primary care doctor in Dr. . At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs. The patient was tolerating Tube feeds, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient was discharged home with services for Tube feeds. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan. HPI: This is a yo M with a history of AI and AS s/p AVR (x3) and TAVR, MR, HTN, TIA, atrial fibrillation off Coumadin and aspirin, , CKD who p/w worsening SOB and leg swelling for the last few weeks. Patient reports that he went to see his PCP last . She increased his torsemide for two days (80 mg) (home dose is 60 torsemide x5 days, 80 torsemide x 2 days). Called office today and reported continued fatigue and SOB and pt was sent him. Reports that his weight is stable at 210 lbs (fluctuates between 208-210). SOB is worse with exertion and relieved with rest. Also endorses worsening orthopnea-has had to add pillow and often sleeps in recliner. No PND. Says he can walk yards w/o dyspnea. Also endorses productive cough that has worsened over the last days. Sputum is clear for the most part but is sometimes thicker and yellow. Also endorses intermittent CP that he thinks may be due to cough. Noticed it last night while sitting. Not associated with exertion. Is sharp in nature without radiation, n/v, diaphoresis. Not worsened with breathing. Felt more when coughing. Reports that he has been compliant with his medications and diet. Says food is bland and has no salt. In the ED initial vitals were: 97.5 56 162/43 16 97% RA EKG: showed afib with ventricular rate in the . No new ischemic changes. Labs/studies notable for:H/H of 9.9 and 32, Creatinine of 3.6 and proBNP of . Patient was given: Vitals on transfer: 97.9 56 175/50 18 92% RA ROS negative for fever, chills, n/v, diarrhea, urinary sx. Does endorse increase in constipation. On the floor, Patient appears to be breathing comfortably Hospital Course: This is a year old gentleman with a history of AI and AS s/p AVR (x3) and TAVR, MR, HTN, TIA, tobacco abuse, atrial fibrillation off Coumadin and aspirin, dCHF, non-insulin dependent diabetes, CKD and previous GI bleed who presents with diastolic heart failure exacerbation. Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure: Patient presented with dyspnea, orthopnea, elevated BNP, and pulmonary edema on CXR. He has known AVR s/p TAVR as well as MR. of exacerbation was unclear. He was diuresed with IV lasix drip and eventually transitioned to PO diuretics. TAVR team were consulted for possible intervention given known paravalvular leak; they recommended outpatient follow up with Dr. . He was continued on home carvedilol. Hypertension: Initially hypertensive to SBP 170s in ED. Home imdur was increased. He was diuresed as above. BP improved. Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation: Patient remained in sinus rhythm. Home amiodraone and carvedilol continued. Anticoagulation was held given history of GI bleed in the past. on CKD: Pt presented with elevated Cr of 3.6 from baseline 3.0 which peaked at 4. Likely in setting of acute heart failure exacerbation. Cr 4.1 at discharge.
